description: Celery (Apium graveolens) is a major garden vegetable, grown for its blanched leafstalks which are eaten raw and also used in cookery. It is a biennial, sometimes annual, herbaceous plant with leaf-stalks 6-15 inches long, bearing 3 pairs and a terminal leaflet that is coarsely serrated and ternately lobed or divided. The flower-stalks are 2-3 feet high, branched and leafy, with white, inconspicuous flowers borne in compound umbels. Seeds are very small, flattened on the sides, and broader than long. Wild plants have an acrid, pungent flavor. The genus Apium comprises about 15-20 species of annual or perennial glabrous herbs with pinnate or pinnately compound leaves, and small greenish-white flowers in compound umbels. Apium graveolens is the important species for horticulture. Var. rapaceum is celeriac, a form where the crown of the plant is thickened and turnip-like. Wild celery grows 1-2 feet high in marshy places near the sea on the coasts of Europe, Africa, and Asia, and has run wild from cultivation in parts of North America. Some individuals are allergic to celery. Celery can be classified into two general groups: green varieties and self-blanching varieties. Self-blanching varieties are more easily blanched and offer cultural advantages for commercial growers, especially for summer crops and late markets, allowing more plants per acre when using boards for blanching. However, light-colored varieties are generally considered inferior in quality to green sorts. Home gardeners often plant light-colored kinds for summer and green varieties for fall and winter. Varieties mentioned include White Plume (vigorous, tall, self-blanching, inferior quality to Golden Self-blanching), Golden Self-blanching (most popular and profitable in California, 14-20 inches tall, compact, stocky, easily blanched to creamy white), Rose-ribbed Golden Self-blanching (rose tinge on stems, attractive for home garden), Giant Pascal (old green-stem, unsurpassed quality, 30 inches or more in rich moist soils), Winter Queen (popular green variety for commercial growers, compact, easier to store), French Success (stocky, compact winter variety with excellent keeping qualities), and Boston Market (famous for excellent quality, low, compact, crisp, tender). Celery plants cannot tolerate drought at any stage of growth and require a well-controlled irrigation system. Good drainage is crucial, as standing water can cause serious damage. Over-irrigation can also be detrimental, leading to the loss of soluble fertilizer and water-logging the soil, which forces roots closer to the surface.
cultivation: Celery thrives in muck soils, which provide ideal conditions due to high organic matter (60% or more), looseness, and friability. A water-table depth of about 3 feet is favorable. It can also be grown successfully on a variety of friable soil types with adequate moisture, plant-food, and vegetable matter. New muck lands require clearing, drainage, lime for acidity, and initial planting of other crops like corn for a year or two, with fall plowing and repeated cultivation. Other soils benefit from fall plowing with liberal manure application. Soil preparation should conserve moisture. Fertilizing is crucial, as celery is a shallow-rooted plant. Stable manure is considered the best fertilizer, providing both organic matter and plant-food. It should be applied near the surface, preferably rotten or composted after plowing and worked in with a disc-harrow. Market gardeners often apply 30-40 tons per acre, or 10 tons on muck land. Commercial fertilizers are also essential, especially if manure is limited. Celery requires much nitrogen (at least 4%), and an abundance of potash and phosphoric acid (e.g., 4% nitrogen, 8% phosphoric acid, 10% potash). Side-dressings of nitrate of soda or complete fertilizer (100-200 pounds per acre) can be applied every three weeks after the crop is well started. Seeds are sown in well-pulverized open ground, typically in March, April, or May. For the early crop, plants are set in the open ground around May 10 in northern latitudes to avoid frost. Late crop seedlings can be transplanted after June 20, or early July, depending on the variety. Plants 3-5 inches high are preferred. Rows should be straight, using a marker or rope-and-peg. Planting distances vary: 7-8 inches apart each way for 'new celery-culture' (self-blanching, suited for greenhouses/coldframes with sub-irrigation, but prone to heart-rot in hot, moist weather). More common is 18 inches to 2 feet between rows and 4-5 inches apart in the row, especially for Golden Self-blanching when boards are used for blanching. For horse implements, allow at least 28 inches between rows. When soil is used for blanching, 4-5 feet between rows is needed. Seedlings are transplanted when large enough, planted 6 inches apart in furrows 3.5 feet apart, at an average depth of 3 to 5 inches. Transplanting should be done rapidly, with plant-beds watered 24 hours in advance. Frequent shallow tillage is necessary, avoiding deep tools. A spike-tooth cultivator is recommended for horse tillage. Hand weeding may be required. Mulching with 3-4 inches of aerated horse-manure conserves moisture, reduces labor, and provides nourishment. A well-controlled irrigation system is imperative, and good drainage, often achieved through tile drainage, is equally important to prevent losses from standing water. Initially, heavy watering is required for vigorous plants, but the quantity is reduced once growth begins, ensuring water does not stand on the surface. Irrigation, especially overhead systems, makes the crop more certain and improves quality. Watering should be thorough and done in the evening or at night. Blanching is done to achieve creamy white stalks by excluding light. Green varieties are blanched with earth, usually starting in early September in the North, gradually ridging the soil up to the full height of the stems. Early crops are mostly blanched with boards (hemlock, pine, cypress, 10-12 inches wide, 14-16 feet long, secured with wire hooks). This takes 10 days to two weeks. In the South, blanching is entirely by boarding-up, often using cheaper cypress boards, with celery planted in double rows (8-10 inches apart, plants 6-8 inches apart) to maximize board use. Soil is sometimes plowed against the board bases to further exclude light. In California, nearly all celery is banked with earth as it nears maturity, with earth drawn higher with each banking. Approximately three to four weeks after transplanting, the field is 'crowded' by moving earth away from young plants to improve air circulation and remove close weeds. Ridges formed are flattened with a large wooden roller (for small plants) or a cultivator (for larger plants). When plants are 12 to 15 inches tall, earth is drawn up to them from between the rows, an operation called 'splitting,' which encourages tall, straight growth and is repeated twice, with the last aiding blanching. Harvesting occurs from late June (coldframes) to August (late crop) in the North, and from October through March, with the peak in November, December, and January, in California. Plants are lifted with forks or cut with a knife, then prepared for market. They are often tied in bunches of a dozen and packed in standard crates. Roots may or may not be trimmed. For local markets, any convenient crate size is used. Storage for the late crop includes city cold-storage (roots on, minimal trimming), trenches in well-drained ground (10-14 inches wide, plants packed closely, covered with board troughs, and later earth/manure for insulation), coldframes (covered with boards and straw/hay), ordinary house cellars, or specialized cement/brick structures and pits, all requiring ample ventilation. In the South (e.g., Florida), sowing is done in July-September during warm, rainy weather. Seed-beds are 18-36 inches wide, with rows across. Heavy burlap is placed over beds after sowing to conserve moisture, cool soil, and protect from rain. After germination, cheese-cloth screens are used for shade and air circulation. Sub-irrigation systems are common in productive southern regions.
propagation: Celery is propagated by sowing seeds. The greatest care must be taken in procuring high-quality seed to avoid issues like pithy stalks or seed shoots. Reliable dealers are essential, and some growers import seed or buy large quantities to test a small portion first. Self-blanching varieties are mostly from France, green varieties from California. Seed can retain germinating power for at least six years if stored in sealed jars at a stable temperature. Celery seed is very small (about 70,000 seeds per ounce), but it's safer to expect around 10,000 plants per ounce due to germination rates and seedling fragility. Seeds are slow to germinate and should be planted in fine, constantly moist (but not wet) soil. For the early crop, seed is seldom sown before March 1. Earlier sowing is possible but requires facilities to prevent crowding and checked growth, which can lead to unsalable seed shoots. Seeds are sown in beds of artificially heated frames or greenhouses, or in flats. Sowing in drills 2 inches apart, covered with no more than ⅛ inch of earth, is preferred over broadcasting. A mix of muck, sifted coal-ashes, sand, and bone-meal is excellent for starting plants under glass. Beds should be covered with burlap until germination, kept moist, and then provided with plenty of light, sunshine, and fresh air. A temperature of 70-75°F is ideal for germination, dropping to 60-65°F after plants emerge, with good ventilation. Once rough leaves appear, seedlings are transplanted into beds or flats, spaced 1.5-2 inches apart. Using flats half-filled with rotten manure and topped with rich soil promotes strong root development, allowing transplanting to the open ground with minimal growth check. Young celery plants are sensitive to low temperatures and should not be moved from warm environments to coldframes too early. Spraying seedlings with bordeaux mixture can prevent fungous diseases. For the late crop, seed is sown in open ground or protected beds as early in spring as the soil can be prepared. Delay can cause crop failure. Outdoor seedbeds require careful moisture control, often using overhead irrigation or shading with brush/lath screens or moist burlap. Thinning may be necessary for stocky plants. Seedlings are typically transplanted directly to the permanent bed or field once they reach about 3 inches in height. If plants are 5 inches or taller, their tops should be cut back before transplanting.
pests_and_diseases: Celery does not have any serious insect enemies. However, it is susceptible to several diseases. The most important disease in California is late blight (Septoria petroselini var. apii). Other significant diseases include blights (Cercospora apii and Septoria petroselini var. apii), leaf-spot (Phyllosticta apii), and rust (Piccinia bullata). Fungous diseases, including heart-rot, can also occur, especially in hot, moist weather when plants are spaced too closely, leading to reduced air circulation. Spraying seedlings several times with bordeaux mixture can prevent loss from fungous diseases. The application of bordeaux mixture in the seedbed and frequent applications in the field help control these diseases and prevent losses in storage. Earlier applications of bordeaux mixture are considered most effective, and crop rotation is also desirable for disease prevention.

description: Walnuts (genus Juglans) are deciduous trees, 10–40 meters tall, with pinnate leaves (200–900 mm long, 5–25 leaflets) and chambered pith. The 21 species range across the north temperate Old World and New World. The Latin name, Juglans, derives from "Jupiter's acorn," and the word walnut from Old English "foreign nut." Walnuts are broadly categorized into three horticultural groups: Persian/English (J. regia), North American (J. nigra), and East Asian (J. Sieboldiana and allies), including the butternut (J. cinerea). Walnut kernels are rich in food properties and pleasing to taste, with most species being fairly rapid growers. The Persian walnut (J. regia) is the primary commercial species, known for its large nut and thin shell, and is native from the Balkans to southwest China. Black walnut species are more difficult to crack. Walnut trees produce male cylindrical catkins and female flowers in spring. The wood is hard, and the Persian walnut is widely cultivated for its delicious nuts.
cultivation: Walnuts require sun and moderate water; they are deciduous and edible. Commercial cultivation, particularly of J. regia, is extensive in California (45,000-50,000 acres) and other regions like Iran (Shahmirzad orchard, 700-750 ha) and Kyrgyzstan (230,700 ha). Optimal conditions include deep, rich alluvial loam with humus, good drainage (6-8 feet deep), and irrigation. Coastal regions with high humidity and moderate temperatures are favorable, while inland areas are susceptible to sun-scald, though new varieties are being developed. Clean culture with winter cover-crops (e.g., melilotus clover, vetch, rye) is common, planted after harvest (Sept/Oct) and plowed under in March/April. Irrigation is typically by furrow system (1-6 applications per season), ensuring water penetrates 6-8 feet, with pre-harvest irrigation aiding shuck opening and clean nut drop. Pruning is generally minimal, focusing on removing lower limbs, but annual thinning can enhance yield. Interplanting young groves with crops like lima beans, other vegetables, small-fruits, alfalfa, or nitrogen-fixing plants (Elaeagnus, Alnus) is practiced. Optimal tree spacing is considered 60 feet apart for larger varieties on rich soils. Harvesting involves shaking nuts from trees, gathering them, washing, drying, culling, sacking, and bleaching (using electrolyzed salt-brine or a solution of chloride of lime and sal soda with sulfuric acid) to achieve an attractive light tan color, followed by further drying and grading. Walnuts are light-demanding, benefit from wind protection, and are drought-hardy. For nut production, selecting compatible cultivars for pollination is crucial, and 'late flushing' varieties are important in northern latitudes to prevent spring frost damage. The Black Walnut (J. nigra) is cultivated primarily for its valuable wood, and Hinds' Black Walnut (J. hindsii) is widely used commercially as a rootstock for J. regia trees.
propagation: Walnut propagation has shifted from seedlings to budding and grafting in commercial quantities over the last 10-15 years. Popular varieties for propagation include Placentia, Eureka, Franquette, El Monte, and Prolific, with Placentia and Eureka dominating southern California nurseries. Black walnuts are widely used as rootstocks; J. Hindsii (northern California species) is favored for its vigor and resistance to adverse soil conditions. Eastern black walnuts (J. nigra) are considered less vigorous, and southern California black walnuts (J. californica) are less used due to profuse suckering and slower growth. Royal hybrid (J. nigra x California species) is also used as a rootstock, believed to be resistant to excessive soil moisture. The Paradox hybrid (J. regia x black species) is vigorous but not practical for wholesale. Nursery propagation typically involves crown-grafting in place: one-year-old black walnut roots are grafted in the second spring using a whip-graft, tied, waxed, and protected. Young trees are staked, and one-year-old trees (6-10 feet high) are preferred for planting, usually cut back to 5 feet, or 18 inches in some inland areas to train a single sprout. Walnut species freely interpollinate, leading to natural hybrids like Paradox and Royal, which are often used as rootstocks. Hinds' Black Walnut (J. hindsii) is widely used commercially as a rootstock for J. regia trees, implying grafting as a propagation method for the Persian walnut.
pests_and_diseases: The primary insect and fungous issues affecting walnuts are walnut aphis and walnut blight or bacteriosis (Pseudomonas juglandis). Walnut aphis can be controlled with tobacco sprays, though damage is usually occasional. Currently, there is no known control for walnut blight, but selecting blight-resistant varieties offers future promise. Minor losses are attributed to red-spider, codlin-moth, and melaxuma. In California commercial production, J. Hindsii and Juglans x Paradox rootstocks are utilized for their resistance to Phytophthora and and, to a limited extent, oak root fungus, although trees grafted on these rootstocks can still succumb to black line. The Butternut (J. cinerea) is currently endangered by an introduced disease called butternut canker, which is caused by the fungus Sirococcus clavigignenti.

description: The fig (Ficus carica) is a picturesque deciduous tree, typically 10-30 ft tall, with muscular, twisting branches that spread wider than they are tall. Its wood is weak and the sap contains irritating milky latex. It can also grow as a multiple-branched shrub or be espaliered. The bright green leaves are large (up to 1 ft), single, alternate, and deeply lobed, rough hairy on top and soft hairy underneath. The tiny flowers are hidden inside the green "fruits" (synconium). The fruit is botanically a hollow, pear-shaped receptacle containing many minute seeds, growing like a branch from the side of the shoot. Common figs have all-female flowers requiring no pollination. Other types include caprifigs (male and female, requiring Blastophaga grossorum wasp), Smyrna figs (needing cross-pollination by caprifigs), and San Pedro figs (first crop independent, second crop dependent on pollination). The "fruit" bears a breba crop in spring on old growth and a main crop in fall on new growth. It has a tough peel (green, brown, or purple) that often cracks when ripe, revealing a white inner rind and a seed mass with jelly-like flesh. Edible seeds are numerous and hollow unless pollinated, with pollinated seeds providing a nutty taste to dried figs. It is a warm-temperate fruit native to Asia, capable of withstanding 10° to 20° of frost under favorable conditions, thriving in areas where temperatures do not fall below 18°F and where summers are not cold, windy, or excessively foggy. It is a long-lived, ornamental tree. Commercial figs are often dried. Key botanical varieties include the inedible Capri fig (Ficus carica var. sylvestris), the Smyrna fig (Ficus carica var. smyrniaca) which requires caprification for fruit perfection and is best for drying, and self-fertilizing table/preserving varieties (Ficus carica var. hortensis). Figs are classified by shape (round, turbinate, pyriform, obovate), skin color (green, yellow, brown, purple, black), and flesh color (white, opaline, red) and texture (melting, spicy, juicy, coarse, dry). Popular varieties include Celeste (hardy, excellent for canning), Turkey/Brown Turkey (hardy, prolific, dwarf growth), Brunswick (large, good for fresh market), and Magnolia (vigorous, prolific, good for canning, but prone to splitting in rainy weather in some regions). In California, it is noted for thriving without culture or pruning where moisture is sufficient and the soil is deep. The annual California crop of dried figs exceeds 4,000 tons, with local consumption of fresh figs also large and increasing. Dried Smyrnas of California, such as the Roeding's Calimyrna variety (Erbeyli), are considered equal to the best from Asia Minor and contain 64% sugar. Other varieties planted include Kassaba, Checker Injir of Scios, Bardajic, and the purple Bulletin Smyrna. Related species mentioned are Ficus sycomorus (Sycamore fig, too tender for outdoor culture in America), Ficus religiosa (sacred fig/peepul tree), and Ficus elastica (India rubber tree).
cultivation: Figs grow best and produce high-quality fruit in Mediterranean and dry warm-temperate climates, requiring full sun all day to ripen palatable fruits. Rain during fruit development can cause splitting. They can grow in wetter, cooler areas with extra care, but diseases limit their utility in tropical climates. Fully dormant trees are hardy to 12°-15°F, but active growth can be damaged at 30°F. Plants killed to the ground often resprout from roots. Hardiest cultivars (e.g., Brown Turkey, Brunswick, Blue Celeste) are recommended for areas like the Willamette Valley, Sierra Nevada, and high desert, while all cultivars suit other parts of California. Chilling requirements are less than 300 hours. California is considered to offer the best climate for commercial fig-growing, with potential also in Arizona, New Mexico, Texas, the extreme South, Hawaii, Australia, large portions of South Africa, Mexico, and the west coast of South America. They are suitable for USDA Zones 10-12 and Sunset Zones 4-9, 12-31, warmer 32, and pots. In northern and middle parts of the United States (e.g., North of Philadelphia to the Carolinas), figs can be grown as a bush and protected each winter by bending branches to the ground and covering with earth, pine boughs, matting, and straw, or by erecting a frame over the plant and covering with heavy carpet. Keep roots as dry as possible during winter, raising a berm to exclude melting snows. In northern climates, they are best grown as tub or pot plants that can be brought indoors. Dormant buds are more susceptible to freezing than wood; freezing may also create a trunk without live buds, with regrowth possible only from roots. These methods typically yield one, or at most two, crops. In the southeastern Atlantic and Gulf states, figs are planted 10x15 or 12x20 feet apart in thoroughly plowed, pulverized clay or heavy, uniformly moist soils, avoiding light sandy soils prone to nematode damage. Planting occurs in January/February in the lower South, or after spring opens in colder sections, ensuring roots do not dry out. Young fig trees should be watered regularly until fully established. In dry western climates, water mature trees deeply at least every one or two weeks; desert gardeners may water more frequently. Mulch the soil around the trees to conserve moisture. Drought-stressed trees will not produce fruit and are more susceptible to nematode damage. Recently planted trees are particularly susceptible to water deficits, often runt out, and die. Figs are best grown in a bush form with three or four trunks, rather than a single tree form, to minimize loss from severe winter weather. Trees become enormous and will shade out anything beneath; they are not a fruit tree for small places. The succulent trunk and branches are unusually sensitive to heat and sun damage, and should be whitewashed if particularly exposed. Roots are greedy, traveling far beyond the tree canopy; fine roots invading garden beds may be cut without loss. Fig trees are productive with or without heavy pruning, which is essential only during initial years. Trees should be trained according to fruit use, such as a low crown for fresh-market figs. Since the crop is borne on terminals of previous year's wood, avoid heavy winter pruning, which causes loss of the following year's crop. It is better to prune immediately after the main crop is harvested, or with late-ripening cultivars, summer prune half the branches and prune the remainder the following summer. If radical pruning is done, whitewash the entire tree. Pruning should be minimal, focusing on removing suckers, dead/injured branches, and shaping the tree in early seasons, cutting branches back entirely rather than to stubs. As figs are shallow-rooted, deep tillage is avoided; shallow cultivation is performed weekly or bi-weekly from spring until July 1, followed by cover-crops like cowpeas or beggarweed. Regular fertilizing is usually necessary only for potted trees or when grown on sands. Excess nitrogen encourages rank growth at the expense of fruit production, and the fruit often ripens improperly. Fertilize if branches grew less than a foot the previous year, applying 1/2 - 1 pound of actual nitrogen, divided into three or four applications from late winter/early spring to July. Liberal use of stable manure and commercial fertilizer is recommended. Figs are eye-catching container specimens, for which slow-growing cultivars are best. For container grown plants, replace most of the soil in the tub every three years and keep the sides of the tub shaded to prevent overheating in sunlight. Figs must be allowed to ripen fully on the tree before they are picked; they will not ripen if picked when immature. A ripe fruit will be slightly soft and starting to bend at the neck. Harvest gently to avoid bruising. Fresh figs do not keep well and can be stored in the refrigerator for only 2 - 3 days. Some varieties are delicious when dried, taking 4 - 5 days in the sun and 10 -12 hours in a dehydrator. Dried figs can be stored for six to eight months. Forcing figs under glass requires temperatures of 50°F at night and 65°F during the day initially, increasing to 60-65°F at night and 75°F or more during the day, with ample air and moisture. In California, trees are headed low (18 inches) for table figs to facilitate picking, or higher for drying figs, which are allowed to ripen and fall. Caprification, involving the fig-wasp (Blastophaga grossorum) and Capri figs, is essential for Smyrna fig varieties. Figs are also considered for avenue trees in California due to their deciduous nature and ability to thrive with minimal care under suitable conditions.
propagation: Figs are very easily propagated, primarily from hardwood cuttings. Cuttings of well-ripened dormant wood from the previous season's growth, 4 to 5 inches long and cut through the nodes, are taken in the fall or winter. Foot-long pieces of dormant wood, less than 1 inch diameter, with two-year-old wood at the base, or one-year twigs with a heel of two-year branch, can also be used. Cuttings can be started with bottom heat in eastern states or planted directly in nurseries in California in December or January, ready for the orchard in a year. Single-eye cuttings are also viable. Dipping cuttings in a rooting hormone and allowing them to callus for one week in a moist place at 50-60°F is beneficial. Summer cuttings can be made but do best if defoliated and winterized in refrigeration for 2-3 weeks before potting; leafy shoots require a mist bed. New varieties are obtained from seeds, particularly fertile seeds from imported Smyrna figs or occasionally from common varieties. Budding is best done by the annular or ring method, and cleft-grafting is possible in February in California with extreme care to exclude air. Particularly rare cultivars may be propagated on rootstocks, or older trees can be topworked by whip, cleft or crown grafting, or chip or patch budding. Rooted cuttings should be planted in 22 to 30 feet squares, depending on soil capacity and ultimate tree size. Keep roots moist until planted. Never transplant or disturb a young tree while it is starting new growth in spring, as this is likely to kill it. Cut the tree back to 2 ft high upon planting and whitewash the trunk.
pests_and_diseases: Figs are generally robust, with California trees noted for having very few insect enemies and not being subject to disease. However, several pests and diseases can affect them. Nematodes (root-knot) can cause serious damage to roots, especially in light sandy soils, forming galls and stunting trees. Fig tree roots are also a favorite food of gophers, who can easily kill large plants; planting in a large aviary wire basket can offer passive control. Birds can cause significant fruit damage, though deer are not particularly attracted to figs. Fruit fermentation on trees, known as 'fig-sour,' can occur in some districts and seasons, sometimes linked to over-irrigation, lack of vitality, or tender/juicy varieties. The fig moth (Ephestra cautella) is a dangerous pest, widely distributed in America (though not yet in California), infesting fruit with eggs and larvae. Mitadulid and Carpophilus dried fruit beetles can enter ripening fruit through the eye, introducing fungi and rots; they often breed in fallen citrus fruits, so keeping a clean orchard by destroying fallen fruits and avoiding planting near citrus trees is recommended. Euryphid mites are carriers of mosaic virus, which, though formerly considered benign, probably causes crop reduction. Symptoms resemble potassium deficiency (leaves marbled with yellow spots, light-colored veins) and are often not apparent until the tree is older or stressed; avoid purchasing infected trees and isolate symptomatic ones. Botrytis causes a blast of branch terminals, drying them out and turning them charcoal-like, usually starting from frost-damaged half-grown fruits and entering the main stem as a reddish necrotic zone; this infection is generally self-controlling and stops in spring, preventable by removing mummies and frost-damaged fruits. Fig canker, a bacterium, enters the trunk at damaged zones, causing necrosis, girdling, and branch loss, often starting at sunburned areas, so whitewashing exposed branches is important. Rhyzopus smut attacks ripened fruits on the tree, causing a charcoal black coating inside, worst on cultivars with large, open eyes. Most ripe fruit losses are from Endosepsis (Fusarium) and Aspergillus rot, introduced by insects (including pollinating wasps); these cause fruits to burst or exude a ropy, mucus-like substance, rendering them inedible. The best control for these is to destroy all crop for one year, apply diazinon granules beneath trees to eliminate insect vectors, and destroy adjacent wild trees. Penicillium fungus can attack dried fruits in storage but is controlled by keeping them dry or sulfuring before storage.

description: Schefflera arboricola (syn. Heptapleurum arboricolum) is a plant in the Araliaceae family. It is an evergreen shrub which can reach to 3-4 m tall. The trailing stems are weak and frequently scramble over other vegetation. The leaves are palmately compound, and have 7-9 leaflets. Leaflets are 9-20 cm long and 4-10 cm across in the wild, but normally smaller when cultivated. Flowers are produced in a 20 cm panicle of small umbels, each umbel 7-10 mm diameter with 5-10 flowers.
cultivation:
A common houseplant, popular for its ability to tolerate neglect and poor growing conditions. If given great care, it can grow up to 10 inches per year. Many cultivars exist with a variety of leaf colors and patterns, with variegation ranging from creamy-white to yellow edges or centres, and dwarf forms especially selected for. It can be useful as a bonsai as well.

The plant prefer bright light, but will grow well in lower light as well. This tropical plant prefers to be kept moist, but never let it sit in water, or the roots will rot. Soil should be moist but not wet.
propagation: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and give bottom heat. Air-layering can be done in spring. Plant seeds in spring and maintain temperature at 66-75F (19-24C).
pests_and_diseases: Scale insects, thrips, mealybugs.

description: The Strawberry Tree (Arbutus unedo L.) is an evergreen shrub or small tree in the family Ericaceae, native to the Mediterranean region and western Europe north to western France and Ireland. Due to its presence in South West Ireland, it is also known as Irish strawberry tree, and Killarney strawberry tree. The Strawberry Tree grows to 5-10 m tall, rarely up to 15 m, with a trunk diameter of up to 80 cm. Zone: 7-10 The leaves are dark green and glossy, 5-10 cm long and 2-3 cm broad, with a serrated margin. The hermaphrodite flowers are white (rarely pale pink), bell-shaped, 4-6 mm diameter, produced panicles of 10-30 together in autumn. They are pollinated by bees. The fruit is a red aggregate drupe 1-2 cm diameter, sometimes called arbutus-berry, with a rough surface, maturing 12 months at the same time as the next flowering. The fruit is edible, though many people find it bland and mealy; the name 'unedo' is explained by Pliny the Elder as being derived from unum edo "I eat one", which may seem an apt response to the flavour. They mainly serve as food for birds but in some countries they are used to make jam and liqueurs. An evergreen Tree growing to 9m by 8m at a medium rate. It is hardy to zone 7 and is not frost tender. It is in leaf all year, in flower from October to December, and the seeds ripen from October to December. The flowers are hermaphrodite (have both male and female organs) and are pollinated by Bees. The plant is self-fertile.
cultivation: Unlike most of the Ericaceae, it grows well in limy soils. It is best planted in a sheltered position due to its late flowering habit (see first paragraph). When grown as a tree rather than a multi-stemmed shrub, it is important to select one stem that becomes the main trunk, keeping any other basal sprouts pruned off. It prefers well-drained soil and only moderate amounts of water. The Strawberry Tree is naturally adapted to dry summers, though also growing well in the cool, wet summers of western Ireland. It is therefore useful for planting in areas with a dry-summer climate, and has become a very popular garden specimen in California and the rest of the west coast of North America. It is hardy in USDA Hardiness Zones 8 to 10. The plant prefers light (sandy) and medium (loamy) soils, requires well-drained soil and can grow in heavy clay soil. The plant prefers acid, neutral and basic (alkaline) soils. It can grow in semi-shade (light woodland) or no shade. It requires dry or moist soil. The plant can tolerate maritime exposure. It can tolerate atmospheric pollution. Requires a nutrient-rich well-drained moisture-retentive soil in sun or semi-shade and shelter from cold drying winds, especially when young. Grows well in heavy clay soils and in dry soils. Most species in this genus require a lime-free soil but this species is fairly lime tolerant. Succeeds in fairly exposed maritime positions. A tree in a very exposed position at Rosewarne in N. Cornwall was looking rather tattered in April 1987 but it was 4.5 metres tall and carrying a very good crop of immature fruit. Tolerates industrial pollution. Plants have withstood temperatures down to -16°c without injury at Kew. They grow very well in S.W. England, fruiting well in Cornwall. Plants resent root disturbance and are best placed in their final positions whilst young. Give them some protection in their first winter. The strawberry tree flowers in November and December, the fruit takes 12 months to ripen and so the tree carries both mature fruit and flowers at the same time and is incredibly beautiful at this time. The flowers have a soft honey scent. There are a number of named varieties developed for their ornamental value. 'Elfin King', 'Croomei' and 'Rubra' are all small forms that fruit well when small. The variety 'Rubra' was 1.2 metres tall at Kew in late 1990 and was laden down with fruits and flowers. 'Elfin King' only reaches a height of 1 metre, comes into bearing when young and fruits well. It is ideal for container culture. 'Croomei' is said to be a more reliable fruiting form.
propagation: Seed - best surface sown in a cold frame as soon as it is ripe. Stored seed should be soaked for 5 - 6 days in warm water and then surface sown in a shady position in a greenhouse. Do not allow the compost to become dry. 6 weeks cold stratification helps. The seed usually germinates well in 2 - 3 months at 20°c. Seedlings are prone to damp off, they are best transplanted to individual pots as soon as they are large enough to handle and should be kept well ventilated. Grow them on in a greenhouse for their first winter and then plant out in late spring after the last expected frosts. Basal cuttings in late winter. Cuttings of mature wood of the current season's growth, November/December in a frame. Poor percentage. Layering of young wood - can take 2 years.
pests_and_diseases: Pests include Scales and Thrips, and diseases include anthracnose, Phytophthora, root rot, and rust.

description: The Raisin tree (Hovenia dulcis) is a deciduous tree that can grow up to 70 feet, though cultivated specimens typically reach about 30 feet with a singular trunk and rounded head. It has a moderate growth rate. The bark is deeply fissured, and branches are gently undulating. Its cordate, glossy green leaves are large (up to six inches) and borne alternately. Small, self-fruitful, cream-colored flowers bloom in late spring in great masses. The edible 'raisins' are actually swollen mature flower stalks (peduncles) that turn translucent reddish-brown and develop a pear-like flavor when ripe and fall to the ground. The actual fruit is an inedible brown pod. The tree is cold-hardy to about -10° F and is not particularly suitable for container culture.
cultivation: For cultivation, full sun is recommended to hasten flowering and ripening, though it is native to partially shaded sites. It tolerates a wide range of soil conditions but thrives in sandy loam. Regular moisture supply is beneficial, although the tree is somewhat drought-tolerant. Light to moderate fertilization in mid-spring is probably useful. The tree tends to self-prune by dropping lower branches as it grows.
propagation: Propagation can be done by seeds, which have an impermeable seed coat. Methods to aid germination include scarifying the seed coat (nicking with a file or soaking in concentrated sulfuric acid for two hours, followed by thorough washing), or soaking in hot tap water (140° F) for three consecutive days. Freezing the seed has also shown some success. After treatment, seeds are planted in potting soil, covered with clear plastic, and placed in bright light, germinating within a week to a month. Seed-grown plants typically bear fruit in 7-10 years, possibly as early as 3 years under good conditions. The plant can also be propagated by softwood cuttings taken in late summer and by root cuttings. Grafting has seen little work.
pests_and_diseases: Raisin trees are apparently free of any significant pests and diseases. Deer may browse the foliage, but gophers do not seem to be attracted to the roots.

description: Kowhai are a tree native to New Zealand. They are a legume in the genus Sophora. In total there are eight species, of which sophora microphylla is the most common.
cultivation: The kowhai is a common feature along river edges throughout all of New Zealand. The trees will usually get to around 8 metres high with a similar spread. They prefer free draining soil.
propagation: The seeds need to be treated specially to make them grow. In nature the hard outer shell of the Kowhai seed erodes away as it tumbles down streams, so the shell needs to be damaged somehow to allow water to soak into it. To allow this, one can take small nicks out of the shell using a pair of nail clippers or some similar instrument. After the chitting, the seed needs to be placed in a container of water to soak for a night. The seeds are best planted in a coarse mix to allow for good drainage, this prevents rotting or fungal infections. The germination period is 14-21 days. The Kowhai can be grown from cuttings during autumn and early winter. They will take about 60-100 days to root and can be planted out in spring.
pests_and_diseases: The Kowhai caterpillar is a bright-green and black caterpillar that strips the leaves of the kowhai. The treatment for such a problem is the use of an insecticide such as Orthene (NZ), Mavrik (NZ), Shield (NZ) or Super Shield (NZ).

description: Humulus lupulus (Common hop) is a species of Humulus in the Cannabaceae family. Common hop is a dioecious, perennial herbaceous climbing plant which sends up new shoots in early spring and dies back to the cold-hardy rhizome in autumn. It is native to the temperate Northern Hemisphere. The flower cones of the plant, known as hops, are used in the production of beer. Perennial herb shoots often grow 25-30 ft. long in the season: rough-hairy: lvs. ovate or orbicular-ovate in general outline, deeply 3-lobed (sometimes 5-7-lobed), or the upper ones not lobed, margins strongly and uniformly dentate, petioles long: staminate fls. in panicles 2-6 in. long: hops (mature pistillate catkins) oblong or ovoid, loose and papery, straw-yellow, often 2 in. or more long, glandular and odoriferous.—Native along rivers and in thickets in the northern states and Canada, and southward in the Alleghanies and Rockies; occurs as far south as Fla. and Ariz. Much cult, for "hops," used in brewing, and extensively run wild from cult, plants. The hop makes an excellent arbor or screen plant. Var. aureus has yellow foliage. The Rocky Mt. form, common in Colo, and New Mex., has been separated as var. neo-mexicanus, Nels. & Ckll., and it is in the trade: it has more deeply divided lvs. and more sharply acuminate bracts than the ordinary hop; If .-segms. from broad-lanceolate to nearly linear, acuminate, with resin particles on the lower surface.
cultivation: It is hardy to zone 5 and is not frost tender. It is in flower from July to August, and the seeds ripen from September to October. The flowers are dioecious (individual flowers are either male or female, but only one sex is to be found on any one plant so both male and female plants must be grown if seed is required) and are pollinated by Wind. The plant is not self-fertile. It is noted for attracting wildlife. The plant prefers light (sandy), medium (loamy) and heavy (clay) soils. The plant prefers acid, neutral and basic (alkaline) soils. It can grow in semi-shade (light woodland) or no shade. It requires dry or moist soil and can tolerate drought. Easily grown in a good garden soil in sun or semi-shade. Prefers a deep rich loam and a warm sheltered position. Plants can succeed in dry shade if plenty of humus is incorporated into the soil, once established they are also somewhat drought tolerant. Hops are reported to tolerate an annual precipitation of between 31 and 137cm, an annual temperature in the range of 5.6 to 21.3°C and a pH of 4.5 to 8.2. Plants are very hardy tolerating temperatures down to about -20°c when dormant. The young shoots in spring, however, can be damaged by any more than a mild frost. A climbing plant, supporting itself by twining around the branches of other plants. Hops are frequently cultivated, both commercially and on a domestic scale, in temperate zones for their seed heads which have many medicinal qualities and are also used as a flavouring and preservative in beer. There are many named varieties. They grow best between the latitudes of 35 - 51°N and 34 - 43°S, with mean summer temperatures of 16 - 18°C. Generally, for beer making, the unfertilized seed heads are preferred and so most male plants are weeded out. Hops are fairly deep rooted, but with a network of shallow feeding roots. These horizontal feeding roots spread out at depth of 20 - 30 cm in the soil and give rise to fibrous roots in upper layers of soil. The vertical roots develop downwards to a depth of about 150 cm with a spread of 183 - 244 cm and have no fibrous roots. The bruised leaves are refreshingly aromatic whilst the flowers cast a pleasing scent. A food plant for many caterpillars. Dioecious. Male and female plants must be grown if seed is required.
propagation: Seed - sow spring in a cold frame. Germination is fairly quick. Prick out the seedlings into individual pots as soon as they are large enough to handle and plant out in the summer or following spring. Division in spring as new growth begins. Very easy, you can plant the divisions straight out into their permanent positions if required. Basal cuttings in March. Harvest the shoots when they are about 10 - 15cm long with plenty of underground stem. Pot them up into individual pots and keep them in light shade in a cold frame or greenhouse until they are rooting well. Plant them out in the summer.
pests_and_diseases: Hop leaf aphid (Phorodon humuli), Red spider mite (Tetranychus urticae), Downy mildew (Pseudoperonospora humuli), Powdery mildew (Podosphaera macularis), List of hop diseases.

description: The papaya (Carica Papaya) is a short-lived, fast-growing, woody, large herb, often likened to a palm, growing to 10-12 feet (or up to 25-30 feet). It generally branches only when injured and all parts contain latex. The trunk is hollow, green or deep purple, straight, cylindrical with prominent leaf scars. Leaves emerge directly from the upper stem in a spiral on long petioles, deeply divided into 5 to 9 main segments, varying from 1 to 2 feet in width, with prominent yellowish ribs and veins. The five-petalled flowers are fleshy, waxy, and slightly fragrant. Plants can bear only short-stalked female flowers, bisexual (perfect) flowers, or only male flowers clustered on panicles. Sex change can occur temporarily during high temperatures or after beheading. There are two main types: Hawaiian (pear-shaped, about 1 pound, yellow skin, bright orange or pinkish flesh, small black seeds, easier to harvest) and Mexican (much larger, up to 10 pounds, 15 inches long, yellow, orange or pink flesh, less intense flavor, slightly easier to grow). Ripe papaya is juicy, sweetish, and somewhat like a cantaloupe in flavor. Caution is advised when harvesting unripe fruit due to latex fluid causing irritation/allergic reactions. The fruit and leaves contain carpaine, an anthelmintic alkaloid, which can be dangerous in high doses. Unripe papaya has contraceptive/abortive effects in large amounts. The fruit, as well as other parts of the plant, contains papain, an enzyme resembling animal pepsin in its digestive action.
cultivation: Papayas require warmth throughout the year and are damaged by light frosts; prolonged cold without overhead sprinkling will kill them. Cold, wet soil is almost always lethal, and cool temperatures alter fruit flavor. They make excellent container and greenhouse specimens. Ideal locations are warm spots with sunshine and reflected heat, free from wind, such as against a house foundation. They are best planted in mounds. Papayas need light, well-drained soil and are easily killed by excess moisture. The soil should be moist in hot weather and dry in cold weather. Plastic coverings may be useful in winter to prevent over-wetting. They do not tolerate salty water or soil. Watering is critical; plants should be kept on the dry side to avoid root rot but need enough water for large leaves. In winter, they prefer to remain as dry as possible, especially if frost-injured. Fast-growing papayas require regular monthly applications of nitrogen fertilizers, adjusted to plant response. Hot organic fertilizers like chicken manure can be used with deep irrigation in warm weather. Phosphorus deficiency causes dark green foliage with reddish-purple discoloration of leaf veins and stalks. Papayas do not require pruning, but some growers pinch seedlings or cut back established plants to encourage multiple trunks. For frost protection, overhead covering (frame with bedding/plastic) and electric light bulbs can provide warmth. Potted plants can be moved indoors. Mexican papayas are more hardy than Hawaiian varieties. Papayas are ready to harvest when most of the skin is yellow-green; they ripen further at room temperature to fully yellow and slightly soft. Dark green fruit will not ripen properly off the tree. Mature fruit can be stored at 45°F for about 3 weeks. Green papayas should not be eaten raw due to latex, but can be boiled as a vegetable. Young leaves are cooked like spinach in the West Indies.
propagation: Papayas are normally propagated by seed. Seeds should be extracted from ripe papayas, washed to remove the gelatinous covering, dried, dusted with a fungicide, and planted as soon as possible as viability decreases rapidly in storage. Plant seeds in warm (80°F), sterile potting mix about 1/2 inch deep to prevent damping off. Germination can take two to five weeks, sometimes sped up with gibberellic acid. Seedlings usually flower 9-12 months after germination. Seedlings do not transplant well; plant them in large containers and transplant only once into the ground, carefully to avoid root ball damage. Drench potting mix with a fungicide (benomyl or captan) to prevent damping off. Plant at least three or four plants to ensure females, or plant hermaphroditic types. Papayas can also be grown from semihardwood cuttings, which should be hardened off for a few days, then propped with the tip touching moist, fertile soil until roots form. Cuttings planted in summer root rapidly and should fruit the following year.
pests_and_diseases: The papaya fruit is susceptible to the Papaya Fruit Fly (Toxotrypana curvicauda), a wasp-like fly that lays eggs in young fruit. Larvae feed in the central seed-mass and then the flesh, rendering fruit unfit for consumption. Control methods include destroying wild plants and infested fruits, and developing thick-fleshed varieties to prevent the female from reaching the seed cavity. Other potential problems include thrips, mites, white flies, red spider, fruit spotting bugs, mildew, anthracnose, root rot, and various virus diseases. Nematodes can attack roots, contributing to plant decline. Gopher damage can be avoided by planting in wire baskets. Papaya plants should generally be replaced every 4 years or so. Papaya leaf-spot (Pucciniopsis caricae), a fungous disease, can attack foliage in winter, forming small black masses on leaf undersurfaces, but is not very destructive and is controlled by spraying with bordeaux mixture.

description: Beech (Fagus) is a genus of ten species of deciduous trees in the family Fagaceae, native to temperate Europe, Asia, and North America. They are ornamental trees, chiefly grown for their handsome foliage, good habit, and conspicuous bark color, and are also valuable timber trees. Leaves are entire or sparsely toothed, from 5–15 cm long and 4–10 cm broad. Flowers are small, single-sex (monoecious), with female flowers borne in pairs and male flowers as wind-pollinated catkins, produced in spring shortly after new leaves appear. The bark is smooth and light gray. The fruit is a small, sharply three-angled nut (beechmast) 10–15 mm long, borne singly or in pairs in soft-spined husks (cupules). The nuts are edible, though bitter, with a high tannin content. Most beeches have green leaves, but some are red (e.g., Fagus sylvatica). They are tall, hardy trees of noble, symmetrical habit, attractive at every season, especially in spring with young, delicate green foliage and graceful, drooping staminate flowers. American species have lighter bark, broader, rounder heads, and less shining leaves that turn clear yellow in fall, while European species have more ovate heads and shining foliage that turns reddish brown in fall and often remains through winter. Beech wood is hard, very close-grained, and largely used in the manufacture of different articles and for fuel, but it is not very durable in the soil. An oil is pressed from the sweet, edible nuts in Europe, used for cooking and other purposes.
cultivation: Beech grows on a wide range of soil types, acid or basic, provided they are not waterlogged. The tree canopy casts dense shade, and the ground flora beneath may be sparse due to dense leaf litter. The beech prefers dryish situations and grows best in sandy loam and limestone soil. It requires a loamy, preferably calcareous soil, shuns poor sand and swamp, and can ascend to 3,500 feet in the Alps. It prefers north and east exposures, endures much shade, protects and improves the soil, and produces large amounts of wood per acre.
propagation: Beech is propagated by seeds sown in fall where there is no danger of their being eaten by mice, or dried after gathering and kept mixed with dry sand until spring. The young plants should be transplanted every second or third year; otherwise, they make long tap-roots and cannot always be transplanted successfully. Varieties are grafted on seedling stock, usually in the greenhouse in early spring, as grafting in the open typically gives not very satisfactory results.
pests_and_diseases: The beech blight aphid (Grylloprociphilus imbricator) is a common pest of beech trees. Generally, the clean dark green foliage of beeches is rarely attacked by insects or fungi.

description: Broccoli is a plant in the cabbage family (Brassicaceae), whose large flower head is used as a vegetable. It is classified in the Italica cultivar group of the species Brassica oleracea. Broccoli has large flower heads, usually green in color, arranged in a tree-like fashion on branches sprouting from a thick, edible stalk. The Broccoli leaves are also edible. The mass of flower heads is surrounded by leaves. Broccoli most closely resembles cauliflower, which is a different cultivar group of the same species. Broccoli is a long-season cauliflower, similar to cauliflower except that its vegetative parts are somewhat coarser, the heads somewhat smaller, and it does not form an edible curd early in its life as does cauliflower.
cultivation: Broccoli is a cool-weather crop that does poorly in hot summer weather. Broccoli grows best when exposed to an average daily temperature between 18 and 23 °C. When the cluster of flowers, also referred to as a "head" of broccoli, appear in the center of the plant, the cluster is green. Garden pruners or shears are used to cut the head about an inch from the tip. Broccoli should be cultivated before the flowers on the head bloom bright yellow. It is cultivated only in climates having a mild winter, when it can be planted the summer before and carried through the winter to form heads early the following spring. For best results, the seed should be sown at the same time as that of autumn cabbage and the plants transplanted to the field about the same time, so that they will make their vegetative growth during the late summer and autumn. Although there is cultivar variety who can be best planted in early spring in zone 3-4. Where winters are mild, the plants can be left in the open, but in more rigorous climates at the approach of cold weather, a small number of plants can be lifted with earth adhering to the roots, stored in a suitable root-cellar, and the following spring transferred to the open to form heads.
propagation: Seed.
pests_and_diseases: Pests: Cabbage Butterfly, Cabbage Fly, Cabbage Looper, Cutworm. Diseases: Clubroot, Black Rot, Mildew.

description: Malva verticillata, also known as Chinese Mallow, is a herbaceous plant growing to 1.8m. It is an annual or biennial, originating from Europe and Asia. It prefers full sun to part-sun and regular water. The plant is edible and hardy to zone 6 and frost. It flowers from July to September, with seeds ripening from August to October. The flowers are hermaphrodite and self-fertile, requiring insect pollination.
cultivation: Easily grown in the garden in any soil, although it likes good drainage and moderate fertility, as well as part or full sun. It is not particular about soil alkalinity and requires moist soil. Grown as a salad crop in parts of Europe. Variety 'Crispa' has more tender leaves with attractive curled edges. It has been cultivated for over 2,500 years in China. The plant can produce concentrated levels of nitrates if the soil is high in nitrogen, especially from inorganic nitrogen fertilizers. If nitrogen levels are not elevated, leaves are safe to eat.
propagation: Grow from seed planted in early spring in situ. Usually germinates by 2 weeks.
pests_and_diseases: Prone to rust fungus, but immune to rabbits.

description: Acacia is a genus of shrubs and trees belonging to the family Fabaceae, also known as thorntrees, wattles, whistling thorns, yellow-fever acacia, or umbrella acacias. The genus was divided into five in 2005, with the name Acacia retained for Australian species. There were roughly 1300 species worldwide, with about 960 native to Australia. Leaves are generally compound pinnate, but many species, especially Australian and Pacific island ones, have phyllodes (vertically flattened leaf-stalks) or cladodes (modified photosynthetic stems) that serve as leaves. The small flowers have five very small petals, almost hidden by long stamens, and are arranged in dense globular or cylindrical clusters, typically yellow or cream-colored, but can be whitish, purple, or red. Many species bear spines. Acacias are quick-growing and short-lived, reaching maturity around thirty years. They are used for various purposes including cookery, perfumery (A. Farnesiana), tanning (bark), gum arabic (A. Senegal), medicine (A. Catechu), soap/hair-wash (A. concinna), forage, dyes, fiber, scented wood, furniture, cabinetwork, fence-posts, fuel, and as ornamental or street trees (A. melanoxylon, A. dealbata).
cultivation: Acacias grown out-of-doors do not tolerate much frost, thriving in winter temperatures as low as 18-20°F. Some species like A. cyclops and A. retinodes are alkali-resistant. Once established, they are drought-tolerant but grow rapidly with ample water and good soil. For greenhouse cultivation, they prefer cool winter temperatures (40-50°F) and do not like heat, making them unsuitable for forcing. They flower in March and April if wintered cool. Acacias are of easy culture, requiring good drainage and a coarse, turfy loam with added leaf-mold or well-rotted spent hops. They need abundant water and daily syringing in early summer. Pruning is essential after flowering to maintain shape. Pot-grown plants should be shifted to larger pots as needed, encouraged with genial heat, syringing, light, and air, and plunged outdoors after frost danger, then returned to the greenhouse before fall frosts. Popular pot-plants include A. armata and A. Drummondii. A. pubescens is suitable for training on pillars, A. Riceana for bushes, and A. longifolia for permanent greenhouse borders.
propagation: Acacia propagation is primarily by seeds or cuttings from half-ripened wood. Seed germination can be difficult; methods to improve yields include immersing seeds in hot water (around 80°C) for 12-48 hours or placing them in ashes among dying embers until cool. These methods soften the hard seed-coats and hasten germination, which typically occurs in 7 days to 3-4 weeks. Seeds can be sown under glass year-round (early spring is natural) or in open ground in March or April. After germination, seedlings are pricked off into flats or pots and kept in pots until ready for permanent transplanting to prevent tap-roots from growing too rapidly. Cuttings should be taken from half-ripened side shoots of the main stem with a heel, ideally in June. No bottom heat is required, but cuttings should be covered with a close frame, kept moderately moist, and cool by shading. They root slowly but freely and should be potted immediately after rooting, not planted in open soil until well-rooted.
pests_and_diseases: Acacia seeds can be ruined by weevils that lay eggs in flower-buds. Cottony cushion scale and black scale are found to a limited extent but have not proved troublesome. In Australia, Acacia species serve as food plants for larvae of hepialid moths (e.g., Aenetus ligniveren), which burrow into the trunk. Other Lepidoptera larvae, such as Brown-tail, Endoclita malabaricus, and Turnip Moth, also feed on Acacia. Leaf-mining larvae of bucculatricid moths, like Bucculatrix agilis (exclusively on Acacia horrida) and Bucculatrix flexuosa (exclusively on Acacia nilotica), also feed on Acacia.

description: Hibiscus, or rosemallow, is a large genus of about 200-220 species of flowering plants native to warm, temperate, subtropical and tropical regions throughout the world. The genus includes both annual and perennial herbaceous plants, and woody shrubs and small trees. Seven species are commonly grown, one is annual, another is a perennial that is like a shrub, two are shrubs that are deciduous and three shrubs that are evergreen. These seven are cultivated primarily for their showy flowers, though one is grown mostly as food and another for its colored foliage. The leaves are alternate, simple, ovate to lanceolate, often with a toothed or lobed margin. The flowers are large, conspicuous, trumpet-shaped, with five or more petals, ranging from white to pink, red, purple or yellow, and from 4-15 cm broad. The fruit is a dry five-lobed capsule, containing several seeds in each lobe, which are released when the capsule splits open at maturity. Hibiscus (old Latin name). Including Abelmoschus and Paritium. Malvaceae. Rose-mallow. Showy-flowered garden and greenhouse herbs and shrubs; in the tropics some of them are trees. Hibiscus is a polymorphous genus, allied to Gossypium, Abutilon, Althaea and Malva, the species widely distributed in temperate and tropical countries: herbs or shrubs, or even trees, with leaves palmately veined or parted: parts of the flower in 5's; calyx gamosepalous, 5-toothed or 5-cleft, subtended by an involucel of narrow bracteoles; corolla usually campanulate, showy, of 5 distinct petals; stamens united into a 5-toothed column; ovary 5-loculed, bearing 5 styles: fruit a dry, more or less dehiscent capsule. Between 150 and 200 species, of which perhaps 20 occur in the U. S. Horticulturally, there are 4 general groups of Hibiscus—the annuals, the perennial border herbs, the hardy shrubs, and the glasshouse shrubs, to which might be added the treelike species of tropical countries that are often planted along roadsides and about dwellings. The perennial herbaceous species are among the boldest subjects for planting in remote borders or in roomy places, particularly in soil that is damp. These plants, of the H. Moscheutos type, are commonly known as marsh-mallows, but this name properly belongs to Altheae officinalis. In recent years, improved and valuable hardy forms of the native herbaceous rose-mallows have been introduced. They bloom throughout a long season. The Meehan Mallow Marvels were introduced in 1905, the first successful cross having been made in 1898. They are stated to be hybrids of H. coccineus, H. militaris, and H. moscheutos. They are in pink, shades of red, and white; the flowers often have an eye of different color. The Giant-flowering marsh-mallows of Bobbink & Atkins, now catalogued as H. moscheutos hybrids, were first offered in 1909 at retail and in 1911 to the trade. They are stated to be hybrids of H. coccineus and H. moscheulos, the first cross being made in 1905, first plant flowered in 1906. The colors range from white to crimson, sometimes with an eye. The culture of such a various group as hibiscus cannot be described in detail. In general, the species present no special difficulties. They are strong and profuse growers, and mostly thrive under a variety of conditions. The herbaceous perennial species are late summer and fall bloomers, with hollyhock-like flowers. They send up new strong shoots or canes each year. Many of them are perfectly hardy in the North, but even these profit by a mulch covering. Others are tender in the North, and the roots should be taken up after frost and stored in a dry, warm cellar. Keep them just moist enough to maintain life in them. Many times the roots of these herbaceous species are set in large pots in the spring, and they then make excellent specimens. All the species require a deep rich soil and a good unfailing supply of moisture. The only popular glasshouse species in this country is H. rosa-sinensis, a showy and floriferous summer bloomer, of many varieties.
cultivation: Tropical hibiscus plants need to be kept in warm temperatures, and bloom best in temperatures ranging from 15 to 35 degrees. They like regular water, but may tolerate low levels of water. Ensure good drainage so water does not sit by the roots. Water more in warm weather, and in colder weather, only water the plant if it looks dry. Hibiscus thrive with plant food. If you are potting your flower, make sure the pot excellent drainage. Spraying plants and under leaves with strong jets of water helps protect against aphid and whitefly infestations. Care for individual species can vary a great deal, so see species listed below for additional information.
propagation: Mainly by rooting cuttings or seed.
pests_and_diseases: Whitefly, aphids, scales, etc. Hibiscus species are used as food plants by the larvae of some Lepidoptera species including Chionodes hibiscella, Hypercompe hambletoni, the Nutmeg moth, and the Turnip Moth.

description: Sapodilla is the name applied in the United States to Achras Sapota, Linn., of the family Sapotaceae, generally considered one of the best indigenous fruits of the American tropics. The tree is commonly cultivated, as well as naturalized, on the Florida Keys, and the fruit is offered in south Florida markets. Botanically the sapodilla is closely related to the mamey sapote (Lucuma mammosa), the ti-es (L. nervosa) and the star-apple (Chrysophyllum Cainito). The tree is evergreen, stately, with a dense rounded or conical crown sometimes attaining a height of 50 to 60 feet, horizontal or drooping branches, and stiff, glossy leaves thickly clustered at the ends of the young branchlets. The wood is hard and very durable. The bark contains a milky latex known commercially as chicle, which is secured by tapping the trunk, and is exported in considerable quantities from Mexico to the United States, where it forms the basis of chewing-gum. The leaves are 2 to 5 inches long, glabrous, of rich green color. The small inconspicuous flowers are produced upon short finely pubescent pedicels in the leaf-axils toward the ends of the branchlets. The fruit is very variable in form, commonly round, oval, globose-depressed, or conical, and 2 to 3 1/2 inches in diameter. The skin is thin, rusty brown, somewhat scurfy, giving the fruit a striking resemblance to an Irish potato. The flesh is yellowish brown, translucent, soft and melting when fully ripe, sweet and delicious, but when green containing tannin and a milky latex, so that it must not be eaten until it has become quite mellow. The seeds vary from none to ten or twelve, and are hard, black and shining, obovate, flattened, about 3/4 inch long, easily separated from the pulp. The flavor is difficult of description, likened to that of a pear by some writers, and with a peculiar character common to several sapotaceous fruits. It is considered one of the best fruits by some early writers. The tree is considered indigenous in Mexico, Guatemala, and possibly Salvador and northern Honduras. Common names include nispero (Spanish-speaking countries), naseberry (British West Indies), sapoti/sapota (Brazil), Breiapfelbaum (German), sapotillier (French), and mispelboom (Dutch). It is cultivated globally, thriving up to elevations of 3,000 feet in some parts of southern India, and over 8,000 feet in Ecuador. Its culture in Florida is limited to the southern part of the state. Mature trees can withstand temperatures of 28° F. The branches are tough and not easily broken by hurricanes. In California, it grows very slowly and fruiting is hindered by cool winters. The soil best adapted seems to be rich sandy loam, but it thrives on light clay and shallow sandy soil. Trees rarely come into bearing until six to eight years of age if seedlings, and may reach 20 to 30 feet. They should be set 25 to 30 feet apart and require very little pruning. Trees bear heavily, often producing two crops a year. The fruit can be shipped successfully and kept in good condition for ten days or more if picked hard. It is mainly consumed fresh, usually eaten out of hand, and sometimes used for sherbet. Sapodillas are not strictly tropical and mature trees can withstand temperatures of 26° to 28° F for several hours, though young trees are more tender. It seems equally at home in humid and relatively dry environments. The slow-growing sapodilla makes a satisfactory container or greenhouse specimen. It is an attractive upright, slow-growing, long-lived evergreen tree, distinctly pyramidal when young, developing a dense and rounded or sometimes open crown with age. It is strong and wind-resistant and rich in a white, gummy latex. In the tropics it can grow to 100 feet, but grafted cultivars are substantially shorter. The leaves are highly ornamental, 3 to 4-1/2 inches long and 1 to 1-1/2 inches wide, medium green, glossy, alternate and spirally clustered at the tip of forked twigs. Flowers are small, inconspicuous and bell-like, approximately 3/8 inch in diameter, borne on slender stalks in the axil of the leaves, with several flushes throughout the year. The fruit is round to egg-shape, 2 - 4 inches in diameter. The skin is brown and scruffy when ripe. The flesh varies from yellow to shades of brown and sometimes reddish-brown, and may be smooth or of a granular texture. The flavor is sweet and pleasant, ranging from a pear flavor to crunchy brown sugar. Fruits can be seedless, but usually have from 3 to 12 hard, black, shiny, flattened seeds. Chicle, the latex obtained from the bark, has been used as a chewing gum base for many years.
cultivation: The sapodilla prefers a sunny, warm, preferably frost free location. They are highly wind tolerant and can take salt spray. Sapodillas are well adapted to many types of soil, thriving in very poor soils but also flourishing in deep, loose, organic soil, light clay, sand or lateritic gravel. Good drainage is essential, as the tree does poorly in low, wet locations. It is highly drought resistant and tolerant of soil salinity. The tree tolerates dry conditions remarkably well; most mature trees receive no watering, but irrigation in dry season will increase productivity. Newly planted trees need small and frequent feedings to become established, using fertilizers with 6-8% nitrogen, 2-4% available phosphoric acid and 6-8% potash. First year applications should be made every two to three months beginning with 1/4 pound and gradually increasing to one pound. Thereafter, two to three applications per year are sufficient, in amounts proportionate to the increasing size of the tree. Sapodillas require very little pruning. For frost protection, although mature trees will take several degrees of frost, it is prudent to provide overhead protection if possible and plant them on the south side of a wall or building. Plants can also be covered with sheeting when significant frost is likely. It is often difficult to tell when a sapodilla is ready to pick. If the skin is brown and the fruit separates from the stem easily without leaking of the latex, it is fully mature but must be kept at room temperature for a few days to soften. It is best to wash off the sandy scruff before ripening. It should be eaten when firm-soft, not mushy. Firm-ripe sapodillas may be kept for several days in the home refrigerator, for 6 weeks at 35° F, and fully ripe fruits frozen at 32° F keep perfectly for a month. The fruit is mainly consumed fresh.
propagation: The sapodilla is most commonly propagated by seed, which remain viable for many years if kept dry. Easily germinated, they take five to eight years to bear. Since seed may not come true, vegetative propagation is desirable. Veneer grafting with seedlings as rootstock is the best method. Air layering and rooting of cuttings have not been successful. Experiments in Florida have shown that it can readily be budded, using as stocks seedlings of the same species. Seeds should be planted in shallow flats of light sandy soil, covering them to the depth of 1/2 inch. In warm weather germination takes place within a month. Young seedlings can be potted off and carried along in pots for the first year or two, then set out in the open ground. If to be budded, they may be planted in nursery rows about 3 feet apart, 18 inches apart in the row. In south Florida, May has proved to be a favorable season for budding; in strictly tropical regions the work can probably be done at any time, provided the stock plants are in active growth. Budwood should be chosen from young branches which have begun to lose their greenish color and assume a brownish tinge, and should be carefully examined for well-developed eyes. Shield-budding is the method used, similar to mango; buds should be cut slightly more than an inch in length, and the wood removed if it comes out readily. After making the incision in the stock, the bud should be inserted and tied as promptly as possible, as latex soon collects. Waxed tape should be used for wrapping. After three or four weeks the stock may be headed back, and the wrap loosened, leaving the eye exposed to start growth. The best seedlings should be selected for propagation based on desired characteristics like seedlessness, fruit size, prolificacy, or ripening time.
pests_and_diseases: The tree seems to be remarkably free from insect pests and fungous diseases, and in Florida requires very little attention. In general, the sapodilla tree remains quite healthy with little or no care. Insects and diseases usually don't cause sufficient damage to necessitate control measures, although the Wooly White Fly can sometimes be a problem. Oil sprays in winter are suggested.

description: Theobroma cacao, also known as cacao tree and cocoa tree, is a small (4–8 m or 15–26 ft tall) evergreen tree in the family Sterculiaceae (alternatively Malvaceae), native to the deep tropical region of the Americas. Its seeds are used to make cocoa powder and chocolate. The tree is found growing wild in the low foothills of the Andes at elevations of around 200–400 m (650–1300 ft) in the Amazon and Orinoco river basins. It requires a humid climate with regular rainfall and good soil, growing best with some overhead shade as an understory tree. The leaves are alternate, entire, unlobed, 10–40 cm (4–16 in) long and 5–20 cm (2–8 in) broad, described as poisonous and inedible. The flowers are small, 1–2 cm (1/2–1 in) diameter, with pink calyx, produced in clusters directly on the trunk and older branches, and are pollinated by tiny _Forcipomyia_ midges. The fruit, called a cacao pod, is ovoid, 15–30 cm (6–12 in) long and 8–10 cm (3–4 in) wide, ripening yellow to orange, and weighs about 500 g (1 lb) when ripe. The pod contains 20 to 60 seeds ("beans") embedded in a white pulp. Each seed contains a significant amount of fat (40–50% as cocoa butter) and theobromine. A tree begins to bear when it is four or five years old. A mature tree may have 6,000 flowers in a year, yet only about 20 pods. About 300-600 seeds (10 pods) are required to produce 1 kg (2.2 lb) of cocoa paste. It is a perennial tree, reaching 25 ft in height and 10 ft in width, suitable for USDA Zones 11 to 12, and prefers part-sun.
pests_and_diseases: Various plant pests and diseases can cause serious problems for cacao production. These include: Insects (Cocoa mirids or capsids, _Conopomorpha cramerella_ "Cocoa pod borer"); Fungi (_Moniliophthora roreri_ "Frosty Pod Rot", _Moniliophthora perniciosa_ "Witches' Broom", _Ceratocystis cacaofunesta_ "Mal de machete" or "Ceratocystis wilt", _Verticillium dahliae_, _Oncobasidium theobromae_ "Vascular streak dieback"); Oomycetes (_Phytophthora_ spp. "Black Pod", especially _Phytophthora megakarya_); Viruses (CSSV); Mistletoe; and vertebrate pests (Rats, squirrels, woodpeckers, etc.).

description: Cudrania tricuspidata, also known as cudrang, mandarin melon berry, silkworm thorn, zhe or che, Chinese Che, Silkworm Thorn, and Chinese mulberry, is a shrub or small tree native to East Asia, occasionally grown for its fruit. It can grow to 20, rarely to 60 ft., with slender, thorny branches. The deciduous trees can eventually grow to about 6m (25 ft.) in height, but often remain a broad, spreading bush or small tree if not otherwise trained when young. Immature wood is thorny but loses its thorns as it matures. Female trees are larger and more robust than male trees. The alternate leaves resemble those of the mulberry, but are smaller, thinner, and pale yellowish-green. They are typically trilobate but can also be unlobed. The che leafs and blooms late in spring. It is dioecious, with male and female flowers on different plants, appearing in June. Both types are green and pea-sized; male flowers turn yellow when pollen ripens, while wind-pollinated female flowers develop many small stigmas. Male plants occasionally have a few female flowers that set fruit. The fruit is a collective fruit, 1 to 2 inches in diameter, resembling a round mulberry crossed with a lychee. Ripe fruits are attractive red or maroon-red with juicy, rich red flesh and 3 to 6 small brown seeds. While firm, they are almost tasteless, but when fully soft ripe, they develop a delicious watermelon-like flavor with sugar content similar to a ripe fig.
cultivation: The che requires minimal care and tolerates drought and poor soils, similar to the mulberry. It can be grown in most parts of California and other regions, withstanding temperatures of -20° F. Ches need a warm, sunny location and should not be planted near sidewalks due to fruit staining. They are quite wind-resistant. One planting method is to place a male and female plant about 1 ft. apart in a single site, pruning to a combined volume of approximately 25% male and 75% female. Trees are relatively undemanding but perform best in warm, well-drained soil, ideally a deep loam. Although somewhat drought-resistant, ches need watering in dry seasons; a deep watering every two weeks is recommended in summer-dry California to prevent defoliation and unripe fruit drop. An annual application of a balanced fertilizer (e.g., 10:10:10 NPK) in late spring maintains satisfactory growth, with nitrogen being the most likely needed element in California. Regular pruning is necessary to control shape; branches from the previous season should be pruned to half their length, and branchlets on remaining parts trimmed about 50%. Summer pruning of the male plant is also necessary when planted with a female. To grow as a tree, lateral branches need pruning, and the leading branch may require staking. Trees grafted onto Osage orange (Maclura pomifera) rootstock tend to be more robust and grow more upright. Ches begin to bear early, with mature trees producing up to 400 pounds of fruit, ripening around November in California. Unlike mulberries, ripe fruits must be individually picked as they do not separate easily. Full ripeness is indicated by a darker red shade with some blackening of the skin. Fruit keeps for several days in a refrigerator in a covered dish and can be eaten fresh or cooked. Cooking with tart fruits improves the taste. Blending and straining yields a delicious che "nectar".
propagation: The che is readily grown from seed, though plants can take up to 10 years to bear. Seeds should be sown immediately after extraction from the fruit. Plants are often propagated from softwood cuttings taken in midsummer and treated with rooting hormone. The che is also easily grafted to Osage orange rootstock using either a cleft or whip-and-tongue graft.
pests_and_diseases: No pests or diseases have been noted. The ripe fruit is attractive to birds, and deer will browse on both the fruit and foliage.

description: Abelias are popular garden shrubs from 1-6 m tall; the species from warm climates are evergreen, and colder climate species deciduous. Graceful, arching branches which are densely clothed with oval, usually glossy leaves 1/2 to 1-1/2 in. long; having bronzy new growth. The leaves are opposite or in whorls of three, ovate, glossy, dark green, 1.5-8 cm long, turning purplish-bronze to red in autumn in the deciduous species. The flowers appear in the upper leaf axils and stem ends, 1-8 together in a short cyme; they are pendulous, white to pink, bell-shaped with a five-lobed corolla, 1-5 cm long, and usually scented.
