Tubular or bell-shaped flowers come in clusters at ends of branches or among leaves. Flowering continues over a long and continuous late spring to fall period. The small, plentiful blossoms are enough to be showy, mostly during summer to early fall. After blooms drop, they usually leave purplish or copper-colored sepals that give color into the fall. Abelia is a genus of about 15-30 species and many hybrids in the honeysuckle family Caprifoliaceae, in the part of that family split off by some authors in the segregate family Linnaeaceae. The Angiosperm Phylogeny Group considers Linnaeaceae to encompass such genera as Linnaea, Abelia, Dipelta, Kolkwitzia, and Zabelia. Named after Dr Clarke Abel, a British physician and plant collector. Abelias are adaptable in the garden, useful in shrub borders, as space dividers and visual barriers, and near house walls; lower kinds are good bank or ground covers.
cultivation: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil, in a sun. Moderate frost hardiness, prune selectively in winter to keep the shrub's graceful form, removing some of the basal shoots to make room for new growth, plus the cane ends. Take care to preserve the plant's naturally arching habit. Don't shear, the more stems you cut to the ground in winter or early spring, the more open and arching next year's growth will be. The cultivation of abelias presents no special difficulties. They do best in sunny, sheltered positions and prefer a well-drained soil enriched by peat or leaf- mold. A. floribunda is sometimes grown in pots and kept during the winter in the cool greenhouse; in this case a sandy compost of loam and peat or leaf-mold will be a suitable mixture.
propagation: Soft-tip cuttings in spring to summer, or half-hardened cuttings in late autumn to winter. Seeds are not often obtainable; they are sown in spring and germinate after a month or two; the seedlings begin to bloom usually in their third year.
pests_and_diseases: Anthracnose, Cercospora leaf spots, powdery mildew and fungal root rot. Abelia species are used as food plants by the larvae of some Lepidoptera species - see list of Lepidoptera which feed on Abelia.

description: Vigna is a genus in the plant family Fabaceae, named after Dominico Vigna, an Italian botanist of the 17th century. It comprises herbs, mostly grown for seeds and fodder, commonly known as the cowpea group. The usual cultivated species are annual bean-like rambling vines with three rhomboid-ovate stalked leaflets. Flowers are bean-like, white, pale to violet-purple, and pale yellow, borne on long axillary peduncles. Pods are slender, straight or slightly curved, a few inches to 3 1/2 ft. long, containing small, nearly round to kidney-shaped seeds. The genus includes 60 or more tropical species, showing great variation in stature, growth-habit, and seed color. Vigna can be distinguished from Phaseolus by its keel, which is bent inward at right angles but not coiled, and from Dolichos by the form and position of the stigma and shorter petioles of the first pair of aerial leaves. Three species commonly cultivated are the cowpea (V. sinensis), the catjang (V. Catjang), and the asparagus bean (V. sesquipedalis). While cowpea and catjang are primarily used for animal food and green-manuring, the peas are also a good human food. Some Vigna species, like V. strobilophora and V. vexillata, are also useful for ornament.
pests_and_diseases: Vigna species are used as food plants by the larvae of some Lepidoptera species including Turnip Moth.

description:
Small deciduous tree with spines, grows fast, popular from S Europe to China. Serrated leaves are oval to lance-shaped. Gives tiny (5mm) cream-colored flowers in late spring, which are followed by plum-like fruit (1.5-3 cm) which become dark red when ripe, and has one hard stone in it, like that of the olive.

**Growth Habit**: The jujube is a small, deciduous tree, growing to 40 feet tall in Florida, but smaller in size in California. The naturally drooping tree is graceful, ornamental and often thorny with branches growing in a zig-zag pattern. The wood is very hard and strong. Jujube cultivars vary in size and conformation, with some being very narrow in habit and others being more widespread. One cultivar, the So, seems to be fairly dwarfing in habit. After 30 years of growth in an average site, trees can be 30 feet tall with a crown diameter of up to 15 feet. Plants send up suckers (often with intimidating spines) from their roots, and these suckers can appear many feet from the mother plant. Currently, these root suckers must be controlled by mowing or hoeing.

**Foliage**: The small, ovate or oval leaves are 1-2 inches long and a shiny bright green. In the autumn, the leaves turn bright yellow before falling. There are usually two spines at the base of each leaf. Some spines may be hooked while others are long daggers. Virtually thornless cultivars are known. As the growing season commences, each node of a woody branch produces one to ten branchlets. Most of these are deciduous, falling from the plant in autumn.

**Flowers**: The inconspicuous, 1/5 inch diameter, white to greenish-yellow flowers are somewhat fragrant and produced in large numbers in the leaf axils. The flowering period extends over several months from late spring into summer. However, individual flowers are receptive to pollen for only one day or less. Pollination needs of the jujube are not clearly defined, but appear to be done by ants or other insects and possibly by the wind. Most jujube cultivars produce fruit without cross-pollination. The jujube is well protected from late spring frosts by delayed budding until all chance of cold weather has passed.

**Fruit**: The fruit is a drupe, varying from round to elongate and from cherry-size to plum-size depending on cultivar. It has a thin, edible skin surrounding whitish flesh of sweet, agreeable flavor. The single hard stone contains two seeds. The immature fruit is green in color, but as it ripens it goes through a yellow-green stage with mahogany-colored spots appearing on the skin as the fruit ripens further. The fully mature fruit is entirely red. Shortly after becoming fully red, the fruit begins to soften and wrinkle. The fruit can be eaten after it becomes wrinkled, but most people prefer them during the interval between the yellow-green stage and the full red stage. At this stage the flesh is crisp and sweet, reminiscent of an apple. Under dry conditions jujubes lose moisture, shrivel and become spongy inside. Tests in Russia indicate a very high vitamin C content. The fruit has been used medicinally for millennia by many cultures. One of its most popular uses is as a tea for sore throat.

**Adaptation**: The jujube can withstand a wide range of temperatures; virtually no temperature seems to be too high in summertime. Winter dormancy allows it to withstand temperatures to about -28° F, yet it requires only a small amount of winter chill in order for it to set fruit. The plant revels in summer sun and heat, with the lack of either limiting fruit production more than winter cold. Yet jujubes have fruited in the Puget Sound and low Cascade regions of Washington State as well as in Pennsylvania. Fruiting of some cultivars has also been reported in northern Florida. The Indian jujube, which is more sensitive to frost, is grown in Florida, but the fruit is considered inferior. Jujube trees are not particularly suitable for container culture, but can be grown in this manner in a large container.
cultivation:
Tolerant of a wide range of temperatures and water, but won't give good fruit without summer heat and water during fruit development. Very drought tolerant if fruit is not important. Does well in desert with some water, can take extreme heat, down to -15°C, exceptionally -20°C, temperatures.

**Location**: Jujubes should be given a warm, sunny location, but are otherwise relatively undemanding. Given adequate heat and sun, the trees will thrive without any special care. They should not be planted in the shade of other trees.

**Soils**: Jujubes tolerate many types of soils, but prefer a sandy, well-drained soils and do less well in heavy, poorly drained soil. They are able to grow in soils with high salinity or high alkalinity.

**Irrigation**: One of the outstanding qualities of the jujube tree are its tolerance of drought conditions. Regular watering, though, is important to assure a quality fruit crop.

**Fertilization**: Fertilizer requirements have not been studied, but jujubes appear to do well with little or no fertilization. Light broadcast applications of a balanced fertilizer such as 8-8-8 NPK at two-month intervals during the growing season would probably speed growth. Do not fertilize until the newly planted tree has several months to get established.

**Pruning**: Unpruned trees produce as well as trees that have been pruned. Extensive winter pruning, however, will keep the plants in better health and produce more easily obtainable fruit.

**Planting**: Jujubes should be set out 10 to 15 feet apart since they require high light intensities for good production. Upon setting out new, bare root trees, top the plant to 3 or 4 feet and remove all side branches to leave only a whip. New, stronger branches will emerge from each bud just below the point where the old branches were pruned.

**Harvest**: The crop ripens non-simultaneously, and fruit can be picked for several weeks from a single tree. If picked green, jujubes will not ripen. Ripe fruits may be stored at room temperature for about a week. The fruit may be eaten fresh, dried or candied. Fresh fruit is much prized by certain cultures and is easily sold in Chinese, Korean, Vietnamese, and Indian markets. Tree dried fruit stores indefinitely and may have good marketing potential as it dries on the tree without the use of a sulfur preservative.
propagation:
Most Chinese cultivars in the U.S. are grafted or budded onto a thorny rootstalk which produces many suckers from the roots. There is evidence that jujube cultivars will root on hard or soft wood cuttings. However, successes have been limited to date with this process of plant reproduction. Jujubes also can be propagated from seed, although they do not come true. Most jujube cultivars produce fruit without cross-pollination, but seeds from such self-pollination are usually not viable (such as from the Li or Lang cultivars) - Source: CRFG.

Seed - best sown in a cold frame as soon as it is ripe. Stored seed requires 3 months warm then 3 months cold stratification. Germination should take place in the first spring, though it might take another 12 months. Prick out the seedlings into individual pots when they are large enough to handle and grow them on in a cold frame for at least their first winter. Plant out in early summer. Root cuttings in a greenhouse in the winter. Best results are achieved if a temperature of 5 - 10°c can be maintained. Cuttings of mature wood of the current season's growth, November to January in a frame. Division of suckers in the dormant season. They can be planted out direct into their permanent positions if required.
pests_and_diseases: No serious pests in the USA. Witch's brooms are a wide problem in China and Korea.

description: Barley is a cereal grain derived from the annual grass Hordeum vulgare. It is a widely adaptable crop, popular in temperate areas as a summer crop and tropical areas as a winter crop. Its germination time is 1 to 3 days. Barley likes to grow under cool conditions but is not particularly winter hardy. It is more tolerant of soil salinity than wheat. It is not as cold tolerant as winter wheats, fall rye, or winter Triticale, but can be sown as a winter crop in warmer areas like Australia. It has a short growing season and is relatively drought tolerant. Common barley is H. sativum, Jess, and is believed to have originated from H. spontaneum, which grows wild from Asia Minor and Caucasian countries to Persia and Beloochistan, as well as in Syria, Palestine, and Arabia Petraea. Common barley has a 4-rowed ear or head, with 2-rowed and 6-rowed races also existing, likely all domestic forms of one parent stock.
cultivation: Barley is a widely adaptable crop, grown as a summer crop in temperate areas and a winter crop in tropical areas. It likes to grow under cool conditions but is not particularly winter hardy. It is more tolerant of soil salinity than wheat. It has a short growing season and is relatively drought tolerant.
pests_and_diseases: This plant is known or likely to be susceptible to barley mild mosaic bymovirus as well as bacterial blight. Barley can be susceptible to many diseases but plant breeders have been working hard to incorporate resistance. The devastation caused by any one disease will depend upon the susceptibility of the variety being grown and the environmental conditions during disease development.

description: The Raisin tree (Hovenia dulcis) is a deciduous perennial tree originating from China, growing to a height of 25-30 feet (or up to 70 feet in the wild) with a singular trunk and a rounded head. It is cold-hardy to about -10° F. It features cordate, glossy green leaves up to six inches long. Small, self-fruitful, cream-colored flowers bloom in late spring in racemes. The edible 'raisins' are actually swollen, knobby, translucent reddish-brown flower stalks (peduncles) that develop a pear-like flavor as sugars increase and fall to the ground when ripe. The actual fruit is an inedible brown pod. It thrives in full sun and requires regular water.
cultivation: Raisin trees prefer full sun to hasten flowering and ripening, and can provide summer shade. They tolerate a wide range of soil conditions but thrive in sandy loam. Regular moisture is beneficial, though they are somewhat drought-tolerant. Light to moderate fertilization in mid-spring is suggested. The tree tends to self-prune by dropping lower branches.
propagation: Propagation is primarily by seed, which requires scarification due to an impermeable seed coat. Methods include nicking with a file, soaking in concentrated sulfuric acid for two hours, or soaking in hot tap water (140° F) for three consecutive days. Freezing the seed has also shown some success. Treated seeds are planted in potting soil, covered with plastic wrap, and placed in bright light, germinating within a week to a month. Seed-grown plants typically bear fruit in 7-10 years, potentially as early as 3 years. The plant can also be propagated by softwood cuttings taken in late summer and by root cuttings. Grafting has seen little research.
pests_and_diseases: Raisin trees are apparently free of any significant pests and diseases. Deer may browse the foliage, but gophers do not seem to be attracted to the roots.

description: The acerola is typically found in dry, thorn-woodlands as a deciduous tree. It grows in San Diego County, coastal Southern California and in more extreme areas with protection. There are trees in Riverside, Calif. and San Bernardino County. In general, acerola has poor cold tolerance, with young plants typically killed at temperatures below 30° F. Trees can survive brief exposure to 28° F with loss of leaves. Trees are sensitive to wind (shallow root systems). The acerola is drought tolerant, and will adopt a deciduous habit; irrigation results in leaf and flower flush. Plants can easily adapt to pot culture in well-draining, limed soil. Large, relatively fast growing bushy shrub or small tree (to 15 feet). Can be pruned to any desired shape, but grows best as a managed shrub. Multiple or single trunks which can be trained. Occasionally, bushes appear to be composed of canes. Branches are brittle, and easily broken. Leaves may be irritating to some people. The root system is shallow, and trees can be toppled by wind, but they can be uprighted and recover over time. Acerola leaves are dark to light green, glossy when mature, obviate to lanceolate, with minute hairs which can be irritating. Foliage will drop during water stress, but recovers well with flush and flowering. The flowers are sessile or on short-peduncled cymes, with small pink to white flowers with five petals. Up to 90% of flowers fall from tree, but "Blossom Set" can be used to counter this effect. Flowering can occur throughout the year, but is typically in cycles associated with rain. Irrigation can be used to induce flowering. Flowering occurs primarily on old growth. Pollination rarely observed, but thought to be by the solitary bee, Centris. Honeybees do not appear effective (contested). Cross-pollination may or may not be required depending on variety or strain (contested). In available cultivars, fruit does set without obvious pollinators or need for cross-pollination. Fruits are round to oblate, cherry-like but with 3 lobes. They are bright red (rarely yellow-orange) with thin skin, easily bruised. The pulp is juicy, acid to sub-acid occasionally nearly sweet, with a delicate flavor and apple notes. The fruit is very high in Vitamin C, up to 4,000 mg per 100 g fresh weight, but typically around 1,500 mg C. Green fruits have twice the Vitamin C level of mature fruits. Fruits develop to maturity in less than 25 days. Seeds typically three with fluted wings, forming a triangle. Many aspects of seed viability have not been studied.
cultivation: The plant prefers full sun for fruit development, giving rise to the problem of winter protection in harsher climates. Shaded trees fruit, but at reduced fruit densities, and the plants themselves become somewhat spindly. Due to its shallow and smaller root system, acerola can be interplanted with other crops more closely than many trees. Acerolas grows in marl, limestone, clay and other heavy soils as long as it drains well; waterlogging of roots will cause plant death. Soil pH should be 6.5-7.5 as acid soils do not promote vigorous growth. Liming of trees and working into the soil is a common practice and necessary for high productivity. The acerola does best with 1000-2000 mm of water. However, as mentioned, acerola is drought-tolerant. Irrigation can be used to cause flowering and can regulate flower cycles. Under good constant irrigation, acerola will flower all year, with between 1 and 3 flowering peaks. Acerola does well with both overhead and drip irrigation. Acerolas requires a good, balanced fertilization schedule, and regular (once a year) liming of soil. Foliar sprays are very effective, and are used commercially. Mineral nutrition is very important, with good levels of boron and iron required. The plant will tolerate heavy pruning, but requires time for recovery. In more tropical areas, plants do not seem overly affected by pruning. Plants are pruned commercially with citrus pruners. Can be kept as a small bush (e.g. 5 ft) and will produce well. As with most frost-sensitive plants, the acerola will need some protection when grown in areas were frost can occur. Growing with overhead protection or growing next to a wall or building may be sufficient, but the plant may also be covered with heavy cloth or plastic sheeting draped over a frame for added protection. Container grown plants can be moved too a frost secure area. The fruit deteriorates rapidly once removed from tree; sensory differences can be noted within 4 hours. The fruit undergoes rapid fermentation, and is typically unusable by 3-5 days. Unrefrigerated fruit develops mold quickly The best uses are direct eating, jams and jellies, and syrups. Juices, which are popular in Brazil, do not hold their sensory characteristics for extended periods. The fruit has also been used for baby food, as a supplement source for Vitamin C, as an ice cream and pop-sickle ingredient, and in many home recipes. Frozen fruit falls apart when thawed. In the tropics, there are typically 3 harvest periods per year. In more temperate areas, one and possibly 2 harvests occur. With regular irrigation, some fruit production may occur through much of the year. Eight year old trees can yield from 30 to 60 pounds of fruit. From seed, plant can fruit in the 2nd or 3rd year; cuttings may fruit in the first year. Productivity increases over a 15 to 20 year period, and then levels or declines. Forty year old productive trees are known in southern Florida.
propagation: Acerolas can be propagated by seed, cutting, grafting, and other standard methods. The plant does not appear stringent in its requirements. Seed viability can be very low; in some groves, seedlings are never observed. Cuttings are considered the simplest method of propagation and, with the use standard IBA hormone, give near 100% success rates. Grafting onto rootstocks has not been systematically studied, although grafts onto Byrsonima crassifolia rootstock have been successful.
pests_and_diseases: The Acerola is susceptible to root-knot nematode (Meloidogyne spp.) which causes serious problems with young trees and slower losses of productivity in older trees. It is also attacked by a variety of common insects, such as aphids, whitefly and scale. In other areas of the world (Mexico, Caribbean) weevils (Anthonomus spp.) are serious pests, and can limit fruit production. In tropical areas, Cercospora fungi can be a major cause disease.

description: Cucumbers (Cucumis sativus) are herbaceous annual vines that creep on the ground or climb trellises, fences, and other supporting frames using thin, spiraling tendrils. They have large leaves that form a canopy over the fruit. The fruit is typically cylindrical, elongated, and tapers at the ends, reaching up to 60 cm long and 10 cm in diameter in some varieties. They are extensively cultivated as field and garden crops in all civilized countries, used as a table salad eaten raw, and are widely pickled in various sizes. Varieties are grown for fresh eating (slicers) and pickling (picklers), which are very similar. The plant is native to the tropics and is tender to frost.
cultivation:
Cucumbers require a warm location and should be planted after the danger of frost has passed. For early crops, a sandy soil rich in well-rotted stable manure is preferable. Seeds can be sown in hills 3 feet apart with rows 6 feet apart, or by machine in drills 6 feet apart, using an abundance of seed due to potential early insect injury. Plants can be started under glass to hasten maturity, sowing seeds in pots, baskets, or inverted sods. Ideal conditions for rapid and healthy growth include night temperatures between 60° and 65°F, rising to 100°F in bright sunshine, ample moisture, and sufficient ventilation without drafts. Transplanting seedlings is difficult if roots are disturbed. Glass-covered frames can be used over field-planted seeds. Marketable fruits are typically ready in seven to eight weeks from seed and continue bearing until frost. Commercial growers usually allow two or three plants per hill, or one plant every 18 to 24 inches in drills. During peak season (hot days, moist, warm nights), cucumbers need daily picking when well-filled out and nearly cylindrical. Immature fruits are furrowed, while overripe ones become swollen in the middle and are not first quality.

For greenhouse cultivation, cucumbers are a significant commercial crop. Seeds are planted in 4- to 6-inch clay pots about two weeks before setting in the greenhouse, often over manure heat in a warm house. A night temperature not below 60°F is essential, with day temperatures up to 90°F in bright sunshine. Abundant moisture and food are crucial. Mulching greenhouse vines with 3-4 inches of strawy manure, with water applied directly to the manure, prevents soil compaction and provides nutrients. Pruning and training are important; plants are twined around strings attached to overhead wires, and lateral branches are cut off.

Plants often need at least 25 square feet, but can be grown on walls and trellises to save space, planted 1-3 feet apart along fences. Bush varieties are more compact. Row covers can protect seedlings from pests but must be removed for pollination. Warm air is needed for pollination, usually by bees. Some varieties, like English cucumbers, must not be pollinated and are grown in greenhouses to avoid bees. Misshapen fruit can result from poor pollination or uneven watering. Harvesting while young encourages further fruit production.
propagation: Cucumbers are grown from seed. Seeds require warm soil to sprout. For an early start and extended fruiting period, seeds can be sprouted indoors. Outdoors, seeds should be planted 1 to 2 weeks after the average date of the last frost, 1 inch deep in the soil.
pests_and_diseases:
Cucumbers are affected by serious insect pests and fungous diseases. The most serious and difficult insect pest is the striped cucumber beetle, which feeds on the undersides of leaves soon after seedlings emerge. Common remedies for chewing insects are often ineffective due to the beetle's activity and difficulty in thoroughly poisoning leaf undersides. Arsenate of lead in strong concentrations is the most satisfactory remedy. Hammond's Slug Shot dusted lightly or a mixture of a teaspoonful of paris green with two pounds of flour can also deter them. Young plants can be protected with small wire or hoop frames covered with fine netting. Strewing tobacco stems under plants or using kerosene emulsion (though often ineffective) can also help.

Cucumber blight (Pseudoperonospora cubensis) is a significant fungal disease. It can be repulsed by thorough spraying with bordeaux mixture, and plants should be kept covered with bordeaux throughout their growth, requiring at least three or four sprayings. Late sprayings are often necessary but can be difficult due to dense vine growth.

In greenhouses, cucumbers are liable to damage from mites, aphis, root-gall, and mildew. For mites, syringe the plant and pick off infested leaves. For aphis, use tobacco fumigation and pick infested leaves. For root-gall, use thoroughly frozen soil. For mildew, improve sanitary conditions and use sulfur.

description: The Cape Gooseberry (_Physalis peruviana_) is also known as Goldenberry, Husk Cherry, Peruvian Ground Cherry, or Poha. It is a stronger grower than P. pubescens, standing partially erect and attaining a height of 1 1/2-3 ft., though it can reach 2-6 ft. (up to 6 ft. with support). It is a tender perennial, native to Brazil, and has been cultivated for centuries. The plant is a soft-wooded, somewhat vining plant with purplish, ribbed branches covered with fine hairs. Its heart-shaped, nearly opposite leaves are 2-1/2 to 6 inches long and slightly velvety. Bell-shaped, nodding yellow flowers with dark purple-brown spots in the throat form in the leaf axils, cupped by a purplish-green, hairy calyx. After the flower falls, the calyx expands into a straw-colored husk enclosing the fruit, which takes 70 to 80 days to mature. The fruit is a berry with smooth, waxy, orange-yellow skin and juicy pulp containing numerous small yellowish seeds. Fruits drop when ripe and continue to mature from green to golden-yellow. Unripe fruit is said to be poisonous to some people. It is self-pollinated, with pollination enhanced by gentle shaking or light water spraying. It is an annual in temperate regions and a perennial in the tropics, frost-tender (killed at about 30°F).
cultivation: The plant prefers a sunny, frost-free location, sheltered from strong winds, and does well next to a south-facing wall or in a patio. It grows in any well-drained soil, performing best on sandy to gravelly loam, with good crops on rather poor sandy ground. Consistent watering is needed for a good fruit crop, but it cannot tolerate 'wet feet'; plantings should be on a gentle slope or mounded if drainage is an issue. Irrigation can be reduced as fruits mature, and plants become dormant during drought. Cape gooseberries thrive on neglect; moderate fertilizer encourages excessive vegetative growth and depresses flowering, with high yields attained with little or no fertilizer. Very little pruning is needed unless training to a trellis; pinching growing shoots induces more compact plants. In frost-prone areas, overhead protection, planting next to a wall/building, or covering individual plants with plastic sheeting over a frame can provide protection. Potted specimens can be moved to frost-secure areas. Fruit is harvested when it falls to the ground, then held until fully ripe. Matured and prepared fruits can keep for several months. Ripe fruit can be eaten raw, used in salads, cooked dishes, desserts (e.g., with apples or ginger), dipped in chocolate, or rolled in sugar. Its high pectin content makes it good for preserves and jams, and it dries into tasty 'raisins'.
propagation: The plant is widely grown from seed, with 5,000 to 8,000 seeds per ounce, sometimes mixed with pulverized soil or ashes for uniform sowing. High humidity is essential for good germination. It can also be propagated from 1-year-old stem cuttings treated with a rooting hormone. Cuttings flower early and yield well but are less vigorous than seedlings.
pests_and_diseases: Cape gooseberries are susceptible to several diseases, including Alternaria spp. and powdery mildew. They are also prone to root rots and viruses, especially when grown in poorly drained soil. Common insect pests include cut worm, stem borer (Heliotis suflixa), leaf borer (Epiatrix spp.), fruit moth (Phthorimaea), Colorado potato beetle, flea beetle, and striped cucumber beetle (Acalymma vittata). Greenhouse-grown plants may be attacked by white fly and aphids. Stored fruit can be adversely affected by Penicillium and Botrytis molds.

description: Hardy kiwifruit (Actinidia arguta) is an edible, berry-sized fruit of a woody vine, similar to kiwifruit but often sweeter and can be eaten whole without peeling. Its exterior is smooth and leathery, contrasting with the fuzzy exterior of larger kiwifruits. Common names include Kiwi Berry, Baby Kiwi, Dessert Kiwi, Cocktail Kiwi, Bower Vine, Tara Vine, and Yang-tao. In its native forests, it's a climbing vine (liana) that can reach one hundred feet high, but in cultivation, it requires a trellising system. The plant has a more delicate appearance than regular kiwifruit. Leaves are elongated, 2 to 5 inches long, attached to the stem on red petioles, usually serrated, and less leathery and fuzzy than regular kiwifruit. Flowers are about one-half inch in diameter, white to cream colored, somewhat fragrant, and produced as singlets to triplets in leaf axils from early May to June. Plants are dioecious, requiring both male and female plants for fruit production, though self-fruiting females exist. The fruit is generally green, fuzzless, and grape-sized, with small black seeds, emerald green color, and a typical rayed pattern when cut open. While typically green, some cultivars have red in the skin, flesh, or both. Hardy kiwifruits are generally sweeter than regular kiwifruit, with sugar levels ranging from 14% to 29%. Cultivars can also differ in fruit aroma and skin bitterness. Commercial cultivation is growing in the United States due to the plant's ability to thrive in harsher climates than regular kiwifruit.
cultivation: The fast-growing, climbing vine is very hardy, capable of surviving slow temperature drops to -32°C (-25°F). However, they need to acclimate to cold slowly, as sudden temperature plunges can cause trunk splitting. Vines require a frost-free growing season of about 150 days, unaffected by late winter or early autumn freezes, for a good crop, but are not damaged by late freezes. They can be grown in low-chill areas, though all cultivars need winter chilling, with exact amounts varying by cultivar. All cultivars grown in both high and low chill areas have produced equally well. Late winter freezing temperatures will kill exposed buds. Plants can be successfully grown in large containers. Location: Vines tolerate some shade but prefer a sunny location with a trellising system and protection from strong winds. Site Preparation: Hardy kiwi plants need a substantial trellis, patio cover, or other permanent support. For a trellis, a single wire or T-bar system can be installed using 4x4 redwood posts (8 ft, buried 2 ft and cemented) with a 2x6 crossarm (4 ft long) for T-bars. Wires are run across posts and anchored tautly to cemented deadmen. For patio covers, plants are trained up a corner post and allowed to form an umbrella-like spoke work of shoots. Soils: Hardy kiwi prefer well-drained, somewhat acid (pH 5 - 6.5) soils. Neutral soils are acceptable, but leaves may show nitrogen deficiency if soils become too basic. Plants will not tolerate salty soils. Irrigation: Hardy kiwi plants need large volumes of water throughout the growing season but must be in well-drained soils. Regular watering in summer heat is essential; never allow drought stress, which causes drooping, browning, or defoliation. Water-related problems are a common cause of plant death. Fertilization: Based on regular kiwifruit, hardy kiwi are heavy nitrogen feeders, requiring abundant application during the first half of the growing season. Late season nitrogen enhances fruit size but can lead to poor storage. In basic soils, a citrus and avocado tree fertilizer should be broadcast and watered in well in early March, followed by supplemental additions until early summer. In other areas, use a high nitrogen fertilizer with trace elements unless specific deficiencies are known. Mulching with manures and/or straws is beneficial, but avoid direct contact with the vine to prevent crown rot. Pruning: Winter pruning is essential for best fruit production, typically based on a "cane replacement" method adapted to the trellising system (single wire, T-bar, or patio cover). One stem is trained up to a wire at six feet and allowed to grow along it. When growth ends in "pig-tailing," it's cut behind the entanglement, and a new shoot is allowed to grow from a leaf base. After two years, multiple shoots emerge from the lateral mainline. During the growing season, each lateral cane sends out a new shoot about 1/3 of the way from its start. The next winter, the older cane is pruned off at its connection with the previous summer's new shoot, repeating annually. Harvest: Ripening depends on the cultivar and local climate. Cordifolia ripens in early September, while Anna (Ananasnaja) may need until late October/early November to sweeten. Hardy kiwifruits drop or come off easily when ripe and are usually picked at the mature-ripe stage to ripen off the vine, similar to kiwifruit.
propagation: Propagation can be done via cuttings for true offspring. Growing from seed is possible, but fruit quality and sex are uncertain. In regions where regular kiwifruit grows, hardy kiwi scions can be grafted directly onto kiwifruit rootstock. Otherwise, one must root their own from hardwood or greenwood cuttings or purchase established plants. Seed: Sow in spring in a greenhouse. It's recommended to give seeds 3 months of stratification; sow in a cold frame as soon as ripe in November or upon receipt. Fresh seed germinates in 2-3 months at 10°C; stored seed may take longer. Once large enough, prick seedlings into individual pots and grow in light shade in the greenhouse for at least their first winter. Plant out into permanent positions in late spring or early summer, after the last expected frosts, when plants are 30cm or more tall. Most seedlings are male. Seedlings are susceptible to damping off and require good ventilation. Cuttings: Softwood cuttings can be taken as soon as ready in spring in a frame. Half-ripe wood cuttings can be taken in July/August in a frame with a very high success rate. Ripe wood cuttings can be taken in October/November in a frame.
pests_and_diseases: Hardy kiwi plants are relatively free from problems, possibly due to their limited widespread planting. One unusual issue is that the trunks have a catnip-like aroma, attracting cats that rub against them. This can be problematic for small plants, as cats may rub off new spring shoots. Garden snails can also be a problem for younger plantings. Other pests include deer, which browse on leaves, and gophers, which attack roots. Scale insects can cause damage if their populations become too extensive. Greenhouse thrips may also damage the fruit.

description: This plant provides flavor and scent with its leaves and flowers. The Earl Grey tea is scented with this plant. Monarda didyma Linn (M. Kalmiana, Pursh. M. coccinea, Hort.). Oswego Tea. Bee-balm. Fragrant Balm. St. acutely 4-angled: lvs. thin, ovate- lanceolate, acuminate. Vars. alba and rosea, Hort., are offered, but the latter should be compared with the next species. In 1893 John Saul advertised M. Kalmiana as if horticulturally distinct, calling it the finest of monardas. Var. salmonea, Perry. Large heads of delicate shade of salmon-pink.—Suited to moister positions than the others.
pests_and_diseases: Very sensitive to mold. Do not overwater.

description: The pineapple (Ananas comosus) is a herbaceous perennial, 2-1/2 to 5 ft. high with a spread of 3 to 4 ft., characterized by a short, stout stem and a rosette of waxy, straplike leaves. The long-pointed leaves are 20-72 inches in length, usually needle-tipped, and generally bear sharp, upcurved spines on the margins, appearing all green or striped with red, yellow, or ivory. At blooming time, the stem elongates, producing an inflorescence of small purple or red flowers. The oval to cylindrical, compound fruit develops from many small fused fruits, is juicy and fleshy with a fibrous core, and has a tough, waxy rind that can be dark green, yellow, orange-yellow, or reddish when ripe. The flesh ranges from nearly white to yellow, and fruits can be up to 12 inches long, weighing 1 to 10 pounds or more. It is a bromeliad, perennial, edible fruit originating from Southern Brazil and Paraguay, thriving in sun with moderate water. It tolerates brief exposures to 28°F and is suitable for USDA Zones 25-27 or greenhouse/house culture. Ananas is a genus in the Bromeliaceae family, allied to billbergias, grown mostly for the rosette of rigid leaves and the strange, often colored head of fleshy flowers. The ripe head is composed of the thickened rachis, in which the fleshy berry is imbedded, and the fleshy persistent bracts; in the pineapple, the flowers are abortive. Leaves are leafy crown or topknot, long and sword-shaped, stiff, and more or less rough-edged.
cultivation: Pineapples are tropical or near-tropical plants that tolerate brief exposures to 28°F, though prolonged cold above freezing retards growth and makes fruit more acidic. They are drought-tolerant, producing fruit with yearly precipitation rates from 25-150 inches. Plants should be located in the warmest, sunniest part of a garden or on the south side of a home. The ideal soil is a friable, well-drained sandy loam with high organic content and a pH of 4.5 to 6.5; sulfur can be used to increase acidity. Waterlogging must be avoided. Adequate soil moisture is crucial for good fruit production. Nitrogen is essential for fruit size and yield, applied every four months (e.g., urea solution spray). Magnesium and iron (foliar sprays of ferrous sulfate) are also important, especially in high pH soils. Frost protection is necessary; plants are small enough to cover, but cold adversely affects fruit quality. Ripeness is judged by a dull, solid sound when tapped. Harvested fruit should be stored at 45°F or above for no longer than 4-6 weeks. Fruiting can be forced in mature plants using acetylene gas, calcium carbide solution, or foliar sprays of a-naphthaleneacetic acid or B-hydroxyethyl hydrazine. Plants typically produce for about four years, potentially longer in cooler climates. For commercial cultivation, land preparation involves clearing and deep plowing, with plants spaced 12 inches apart in beds. Fertilization is critical, using dried blood, ground bone, and nitrate of soda for nitrogen, and various sulfates or carbonates of potash. Acid phosphate should be used sparingly or avoided. A common fertilizing plan involves cottonseed meal in the bud after planting, followed by blood and bone and low-grade sulfate of potash applications in October, February, June/July, and October, with amounts adjusted based on plant growth. Cultivation involves light surface agitation with a scuffle-hoe, avoiding leaf breakage. Shelters are highly advantageous, protecting plants from extreme temperatures and frost, which can freeze plants at 32°F. The same stalk does not bear a second time, but a new shoot may arise from the same root and bear fruit; better results are usually secured by severing the sucker or crown and growing a new plant.
propagation: Pineapples are propagated by new vegetative growth, specifically slips (emerging from the stalk below the fruit), suckers (originating from leaf axils), crowns (growing from the top of the fruits, also called topknot), and ratoons (developing from underground stem portions), or small offsets from the base. While slips and suckers are preferred for commercial planting, crowns are the primary material for home gardeners, obtained from store-bought fruit by twisting them free. After removing bottom leaves, crowns are dried for two days before planting or starting in water; dividing crowns is not recommended in marginal conditions. Plants are typically set out during summer months. A black plastic ground cover is beneficial for weed control, heat absorption, and moisture conservation. Crowns are planted about 2 inches deep, while suckers and slips are planted 3 to 4 inches deep. Suckers are commonly used for planting, and ratoons, along with some suckers, are left in the field to replace harvested plants. Slips take approximately one year longer than suckers to mature a crop. These vegetative parts are treated as cuttings, being rooted in sand with bottom heat, or in the South, set directly in the field. Raising plants from seed is primarily for breeding purposes and takes 10-12 years.
pests_and_diseases: Pineapple plants can be affected by several pests and diseases. Mealybugs (Dactylopius citri and other species), spread by ants, are a common problem, attacking the base of leaves, buds, slips, and fruit eyes; controlling ants helps manage mealybugs, and tobacco dust in leaf axils can be a remedy. In commercial areas, nematodes, mites, and beetles can be damaging, though less so in California. Diseases include Heart-rot (bitter-heart), whose cause is unknown but is more prevalent in rainy seasons, causing a water-soaked appearance and bitterness in the fruit. Sanding occurs when sand blows into the plant's bud, especially after planting during dry spells; cottonseed-meal or a mixture with tobacco stems can form a protective plug. Spike (longleaf) manifests as leaves failing to expand at the base, often due to improper commercial fertilizers or untoward soil conditions; avoiding acid phosphate, kainit, and large quantities of cottonseed-meal, and using sheds, can help. Blight (wilt) is a sporadic disease where leaves turn red, wilt, and the plant dries up, caused by a soil-inhabiting fungus attacking roots; removing infected plants or trimming and replanting healthy parts is recommended. Red-spider (Stigmaeus floridanus) attacks the tender white base of leaves, leading to rot; tobacco dust in the bud can control them. Scale insects (Diaspis bromeliae) are troublesome in dry areas and greenhouses, causing yellow spots on leaves; resin wash, resin compound, or whale-oil soap sprays are remedies.

description: The cherimoya is a fairly dense, fast-growing, evergreen tree, briefly deciduous in California from February through April. The tree can reach 30 feet or more, but is fairly easily restrained. Young trees "harp," forming opposite branches as a natural espalier. These can be trained against a surface, or pruned off to form a regular free-standing trunk. Growth is in one long flush, beginning in April. The roots commence as taproot, but the slow-growing root system is rather weak, superficial, and ungreedy. Young plants need staking. The attractive leaves are single and alternate, 2 to 8 inches long and up to 4 inches wide. They are dark green on top and velvety green on the bottom, with prominent veins. New growth is recurved, like a fiddle-neck. Axillary buds are hidden beneath fleshy leaf petioles. The fragrant flowers are borne solitary or in groups of 2 or 3 on short, hairy stalks along the branches. They appear with new growth flushes, continuing as new growth proceeds and on old wood until midsummer. The flowers are made up of three fleshy, greenish-brown, oblong, downy outer petals and three smaller, pinkish inner petals. They are perfect but dichogamous, lasting approximately two days, and opening in two stages, first as female flowers for approximately 36 hours. and later as male flowers. The flower has a declining receptivity to pollen during the female stage and is unlikely to be pollinated by its own pollen in the male stage. The cherimoya is subtropical or mild-temperate and will tolerate light frosts. Young growing tips are killed at 29° F and mature trees are killed or severely injured at 25° F. If cherimoyas do not receive enough chilling, the trees will go dormant slowly and then experience delayed foliation. The amount of chilling needed is estimated to be between 50 and 100 hours. The tree grows well in the coastal and foothill areas of southern California, doing best at a slight elevation, 3 to 15 miles from ocean. It is worth attempting in sunny, south-facing, nearly frost-free locations from San Francisco Bay Area to Lompoc, and may survive to fruit in a very few protected Central Valley foothill locations from Chico to Arvin. Resentful of the excessive dry heat of the interior, it is not for the desert. Cherimoyas are not recommended for container culture. The compound fruit is conical or somewhat heart-shaped, 4 to 8 inches long and up to 4 inches in width, weighing on the average 5-1/2 to 18 ounces, but the largest fruits may reach 5 pounds in weight. The skin, thin or thick, may be smooth with fingerprint-like markings or covered with conical or rounded protuberances. The sweet, juicy, white flesh is melting, subacid and very fragrant. The fruit is of a primitive form with spirally arranged carpels, resembling a raspberry. Each segment of flesh surrounds a single hard black bean-like seed. The fruit size is generally proportional to the number of seeds within. They ripen October to May.
cultivation: Cherimoyas prefer a sunny exposure, buoyant marine air and cool nights. In southern California do not plant where heat collects on barren hillside or against a wall, since the leaves and fruit may sunburn badly. In the north, do the opposite: plant against a south facing wall to collect heat and encourage early bud-break and fruit ripening. The trees need protection from constant ocean or Santa Ana winds which may damage them and interfere with pollination and fruit set. The cherimoya performs well on a wide range of soil types from light to heavy, but seems to do best on a well-drained, medium soil of moderate fertility. The optimum pH ranges from 6.5 to 7.6. Cherimoyas need plenty of moisture while they are growing actively, but should not be watered when they are dormant. The trees are susceptible to root rot in soggy soils, especially in cool weather. Commence deep watering biweekly in April. Drip irrigation is also an excellent way to supply water. It is best to avoid poor water to prevent salt build-up. Drought-stressed trees will drop their leaves, exposing the fruit to sunburn. Cherimoyas should be fertilized on a regular basis. Apply a balanced fertilizer, such as 8-8-8 NPK, in midwinter, then every three months. Increase the amount of fertilizer each year until the trees begin to bear fruit. Mature trees require an annual application of 4 ounces of actual nitrogen per inch of trunk diameter. Cherimoyas also respond to organic amendments. It should be kept in mind that yellow leaves may mean that the soil too dry or the weather too cold, not always a need for fertilizer. Cherimoyas have rather brittle wood. Prune during the dormant period to develop strong branches that can support the heavy fruit. Train the tree to two scaffold branches at 2 feet of trunk, pruning them to a 2 foot length. Save only the strongest single shoots, preferably those at 60 to 90 degree angle, and remove the others. In the following years, remove two-thirds of the previous year's growth, leaving six or seven good buds, at time of new growth. This will keep fruiting wood within reach of the ground. Thin out crossing branches. Young trees are very frost sensitive. Wrap the trunk and scaffold with sponge foam for protection, or cover the entire tree. In cooler areas plant next to a south-facing wall or under the eaves to trap house heat. Since natural pollinators are not present in California, the flowers must be pollinated by hand. This is best done in mid-season of bloom, over a period of two to three months. In early evening, collect in a small bottle the anthers and pollen from the interior of fully open male flowers with a #2 or #3 artists brush. Anthers will be tan colored and the white pollen falling from them will be obvious. The pollen has its highest viability at the time it is shed and declines significantly with time. Immediately apply freshly collected pollen with a small brush to the flowers in partially open, female stage. If no female stage flowers are available, pollen may be saved in the sealed container under refrigeration overnight. Pollen may then be applied to female stage flowers in the morning. In large scale operations the pollen may be mixed with inert Lycopodium spores, PVC, starch or talc powder and applied with aspirator-type Japanese apple-pollinators, to save time and pollen. Pollinate every two or three days, and only flowers easily reached inside the tree, to avoid sunburned and wind-damaged fruit. If pollination efforts are quite successful, it may be necessary to thin the fruit. Too much fruit may result in small size and adversely effect future yields. The fruit turns a pale green or creamy yellow color as they reach maturity. Color change is not marked in cool weather. They should be picked when still firm and allowed to soften at room temperature. Ripe fruit will give to soft pressure. Overripe fruit will be dark brown. Fruit left on the tree too long will usually crack or split and begin to decay. The fruit should be clipped rather than pulled from the tree. Cut the stem close to the fruit so it won't puncture other fruit during storage. Store mature fruit above 55° F to prevent chilling injury to the skin and flesh. Ripe fruit will deteriorate quickly but can be stored at temperatures lower than 55° F for short periods.
propagation: Since there are no recognized rootstocks for cherimoyas, seedlings are universally utilized. Seeds from the White cultivar (Dr. White) are thought by some to produce superior rootstocks, however there does not appear to be a great deal of objective data to support this position. Seeds remain viable for two to three years if kept dry and protected from weevil and fungi. With 70° F bottom heat, seed will germinate in about 21 days, but will require about 40 days under normal ambient growing conditions. Seedlings should be transplanted to deep containers (approximately 18") when they are 3" tall to promote development of the tap root. In frost-free areas, it is recommended that seedlings for spring grafting be planted in their ultimate location in the fall and grafted in the ground the following spring. Grafting is most successful in January through May provided previous years leaves have not been shed from the potential scionwood. During this period no scion preparation is required other than removal of leaves. All normal grafting techniques appear to be equally successful. However in topworking, nurse branches are desirable if not essential for success. To bud, collect budwood in July store refrigerated for 10 days in plastic. Petioles will drop exposing dormant buds. Bud at once using chip bud technique and wrap well against dehydration. Grafted plants will bear in two to three years.
pests_and_diseases: Mealybugs and snails are the main pests of cherimoyas. Keep ducks or apply copper strips to the trunks for control of snails. Mealybugs are brought by ants which can be controlled to some extent by maintaining fresh Tanglefoot on masking tape around the trunk. The masking tape is important to prevent damage to the tree. Skirt the tree to prevent ant access from the ground or weeds. No chemicals are registered for use on Cherimoyas. Cherimoyas are susceptible to Armillaria (Oak Root Fungus) and Verticillium. Do not plant in old vegetable gardens, or near tomatoes, eggplant or asters. Crown rot can kill trees damaged by frost or growing in saturated soil, as well as from trunks hit by frequent, superficial lawn sprinkling.

description: The coconut, Cocos nucifera, is the most important of cultivated palms. Its nearest relatives, whether or not regarded as in the same genus, are natives of tropical America. For this and for other reasons which have been presented by Cook, it must be believed that the coconut is a native of America, and that it was carried westward across the Pacific in prehistoric times. While the nut will float and retain its power of germination for a considerable time, its propagation from island to island in known cases has practically always been the deliberate work of men, and it is probable that men were also responsible for its crossing the Pacific. It was a cultivated plant in Polynesia and Malaya, and in many places the chief crop, at the time of the discovery of this part of the world by Europeans. But it reached Ceylon recently enough so that its introduction is a matter of fairly reliable legend. It is now grown in all tropical countries except the interior of continents. Its cultivation extends somewhat beyond the tropics, both north and south, but its growth at these extremes, in Florida, India and Madagascar, is not thrifty enough to give it any industrial importance. Within the last two decades, the rise in the price of oils and the discovery of new uses for coconut-oil have caused a tremendous increase in the area devoted to the plantation and cultivation of coconuts. Cocos nucifera, Linn. Coco Palm. Coconut Tree. Caudex 40-100 ft. high, flexuous, thickened at the base: Lvs. 12-18 ft. long; Ifts. linear-lanceolate, 2-3 ft., coriaceous, flaccid: petiole 3-5 ft., stout. Seashores within the tropics and at Miami and Palm Beach, Fla. Indigenous to Cocos or Keeling Isls. of the Indian Ocean, but recently thought to be native of Trop. Amer.— Produces the coconuts of commerce. Var. aurea, Hort., is a form remarkable for its orange-yellow sheaths, petioles and midribs.
cultivation: In the first years after the coconuts are transplanted, it is good policy to raise catch-crops between the trees. But these crops should be so chosen that they will not compete with the coconut for light or water; and from the profit they pay, a return should be made to the soil of fertilizers at least sufficient to replace what they have removed. By the time the grove is four years old, the coconuts will shade the ground and it will no longer be possible to raise catch-crops on a large scale. Then, but not before this time, it is good practice to use the grove for pasture. The returns from live-stock should be at least sufficient to pay for keeping the plantation in good condition and cattle will themselves do a large part of the work in keeping down the other vegetation. Pasturing of other live-stock in coconut groves is in general not to be recommended. It is not customary anywhere in the tropics to give to coconut plantations such cultivation as is given to orchards in temperate countries. It has ever been believed that any but the most shallow cultivation would be detrimental by destroying the roots near the surface, and that machine-cultivation was likely to be top expensive to be profitable, in view of the time that it would have to be kept up before the coconut begins to pay returns. Limited experience in the Philippines indicates that real cultivation produces very much the same results with coconuts as it does with other crops. Coconuts respond, as do other crops, to the application of manures containing potash, nitrogen, and phosphorus. So far as the very limited evidence shows, the demand for these three fertilizing elements is in the order given. With ordinarily good treatment, coconuts come into bearing in seven or eight years. Single trees of standard varieties will bear fruit in five years, while others will require ten. If the coconut is treated as a wild crop, which is by no means uncommon, and little or no attention is given it after the first three years, it will be ten or fifteen years, as a rule, before a full crop is produced and even then the crop will be an inferior one. The coconut makes on the climate the characteristic demands of a typically tropical plant. It thrives where the mean annual temperature is 72° F. or higher, and where there are no great differences in temperature between seasons. Except where supply of ground water makes it independent of local rainfall, the coconut demands an annual rainfall of at least one meter (about 40 in.); and this precipitation should be well distributed through the year. In most of the best coconut countries, the rainfall is considerably more than one meter. The coconut can endure exceedingly drying conditions for short periods, and is accordingly adapted to the intense light of the seashore, to resisting strong winds, and to enduring salt water about its roots for short periods of time. Moreover, it will live through prolonged droughts. But long dry seasons cut down the crops; and the damage done by droughts lasts for as much as two or three years after the return of rain. A dry season of five or six months every other year will keep the crop at all times down to not more than 40 per cent of what it would be if the supply of water were constant. If there is an ample supply of soil-water, dryness of the atmosphere is favorable to the best production. Seacoasts usually have higher land back of them, and the ground-water from the higher country circulates through the soil toward the sea. Near the shore it comes near enough to the surface to be reached by the roots of the coconut. For this reason, coconuts thrive on the seashore under climatic conditions that prevent good development in the interior. This is the principal ground for the idea that coconuts thrive only near the sea. Around the bases of volcanoes in the interior, similar soil conditions are met with, and such localities are admirably adapted to this crop.
propagation: The coconut is produced only by seed. Nuts for this purpose should of course be selected from conspicuously good trees. They are usually planted in seed-beds, although, on a small scale, there are various other local methods of handling them during germination. The best treatment is to take them from the seed-bed when the plumule is not more than 6 inches high, which will usually be after about six months. To avoid the expense of keeping the groves clean while the trees are small, it is common practice to leave the nuts for a longer time in the seed-beds, but the transplanting of older seedlings, even with the greatest practicable care, sets them back for several months. In the Jaffna district of northern Ceylon, the nuts are transplanted from the first seed-beds to others in which they have more room, and are not put in their permanent places until they are three or four years old.
pests_and_diseases: With the increase in the industry in the tropical world, and with the increase in commerce, there have been created conditions favorable to the development and spread of pests. Twenty years ago, serious coconut pests were practically unknown, and only eight years ago, Prudhomme, in an excellent general treatment of the coconut industry, listed as serious pests only two or three insects and no other organisms. There are now known as serious pests various species of Rhynchophorus, known as palm weevils; Oryctes, called the rhinoceros beetle; a scale, Aspidiotus destructor, closely related to the San Jose scale; at least two fungi, and the organisms causing bud-rot. The latter have been determined in the West Indies to be Bacillus Coli, and in India to be a fungus, Pythium palmivorum. Besides these, there are a large number of minor or local pests, including weevils and other beetles, the larvae of moths and butterflies, insects of other groups, and fungi. Damage is also done in places by crustaceans, and by rats and other higher animals. Forests made up of one kind of tree practically do not exist in nature in the tropics; and when such forests are made, as has been done with the coconut, the prevention of devastation by pests will be accomplished only by greater care than is ordinarily demanded to protect the crops of temperate lands.

description:
The Mango (Mangifera indica) exists in two main races: Indian and Philippine. The Indian race is intolerant of humidity, has bright red new growth susceptible to mildew, and bears monoembryonic fruit with high color. The Philippine race tolerates excess moisture, has pale green or red new growth, resists mildew, and produces polyembryonic, pale green, elongated kidney-shaped fruit. Philippine types from Mexico are particularly hardy in California. Mango trees require a frost-free climate; flowers and small fruit can be killed below 40°F, and young trees damaged below 30°F, though mature trees can withstand brief drops to 25°F. They need warm, dry weather for fruit set and resent cool summer fog. Wet, humid weather promotes anthracnose and poor fruit set. Dwarf cultivars are suitable for containers or greenhouses.

Mango trees are large, tropical evergreen trees, typically erect with a broad, dome-shaped or tall, oval crown, reaching up to 70 feet (usually half in California) and living over 300 years. They have deep taproots (up to 20 ft) and extensive feeder roots. Leaves are alternate, petiolate, entire, coriaceous, oblong-lanceolate to elliptic, 6-16 inches long, deep green above, pale below, and red or wine-colored when young, emitting a turpentine odor when crushed. Growth occurs in periodic 'flushes'.

Flowers are small, yellowish or reddish, odorous, polygamous, and appear in dense terminal panicles (up to 2000 minute flowers, a foot or more in length). Both staminate and hermaphrodite flowers are present on the same panicle, with typically one fertile stamen. Pollinators are flies and hoverflies. Pollen cannot shed in high humidity or rain, and fertilization is ineffective below 55°F night temperatures. Mangos are monoecious and self-fertile.

The fruit is a large, fleshy drupe, extremely variable in size (from plum-sized to 4-5 pounds) and shape (kidney-shaped, ovate, round, slender, reniform, heart-shaped, elliptical). The leathery, waxy skin is inedible and contains irritating sap, commonly yellow or greenish-yellow, sometimes scarlet or crimson. The flesh is peachlike, juicy, rich, sweet, and high in sugars and acid, often compared to apricot, pineapple, or peach. Fibers radiate from the single large seed, more pronounced with hard water and chemical fertilizers. The stone is large, flattened, and fibrous in seedlings but fiber-free in grafted varieties. Seeds can be monoembryonic or polyembryonic. Some seedling varieties may have a turpentine taste. Mango trees tend to be alternate bearing.

Notable varieties include Mulgoba (broadly oval, yellow with crimson-scarlet blush), Haden (highly colored, good flavor), Paheri (oval, orange-yellow with scarlet blush, juicy, spicy), Amini (smaller, oblong-ovate, penetrating aroma, spicy), Cambodiana (elongated, light yellow or greenish yellow, more fiber), Bennett (oval to cordate, orange-yellow, fiber-free), and Sandersha (very large, elongated, orange-yellow, fiber-free). The species is native to N. India, Burma, and possibly Malaya.
cultivation:
Mango trees thrive in tropical and subtropical regions, requiring full sun and perfect air drainage in winter, doing best on slopes. A windbreak is recommended in exposed areas, and staking may be needed. In deserts, they need shade from other trees or a north-side planting; in gardens or near the coast, planting against a south wall or in paved areas provides maximum heat. In greenhouses, full light and free air movement are crucial to prevent disease.

They grow in almost any well-drained soil (sandy, loam, or clay), avoiding heavy, wet soils, with a preferred pH of 5.5 to 7.5. Deep soil is necessary for their extensive root systems. The most crucial climatic factor is a well-defined dry season during blooming and ripening; damp, cloudy weather during bloom is prejudicial. Ideal annual precipitation is 30-60 inches, with minimal rain during flowering.

Irrigation should begin when the weather warms (February in the desert, April at the coast) and continue every one to two weeks (more often in light soils, nearly continuously in the desert) until fruit harvest. Watering can be discontinued when rains are sufficient. Greenhouse plants are watered until harvest, then reduced to a minimum, increasing again after one to two months to initiate a new bloom cycle.

Regular nitrogen fertilizer applications are needed for healthy growth and flower production, along with chelated micronutrients like iron. A feeding program similar to citrus is satisfactory, but avoid fertilizing after midsummer. Organic fertilizers are preferred as trees are susceptible to fertilizer burn, especially young trees which respond well to fish emulsion. Sandy soils require more fertilizer. Well-rotted stable or sheep manure can be used for young trees, but fertilizers (e.g., 3% nitrogen, 10% phosphoric acid, 10% potash for Cuban soils) should be applied carefully to mature trees (15-25 lbs/year) and withheld in fall, winter, or during flowering.

Healthy trees need little pruning, but pruning to stimulate new growth can promote uniform annual bearing. Removing some flower clusters during heavy bloom years may alleviate alternate bearing. Pruning to control size can be done in late winter or early spring without fruit loss, mainly to encourage a symmetrical, well-branched, non-dense head. Sap and debris can cause severe dermatitis, so avoid burning prunings.

Young trees (first two years) require overhead frost protection. For taller trees, methods include lath shelters, orchard heating, lights under the canopy, or foam/straw trunk wraps. Do not prune dead parts until all frost danger has passed. Young plants are sensitive to freezing temperatures, but mature trees can withstand 27-28°F for short durations.

Fruit matures 100 to 150 days after flowering. For best flavor, allow fruit to ripen on the tree, though winter-maturing fruits in coastal California may need indoor ripening. Ripening fruit changes color and softens. Commercial marketability requires 13% dissolved solids. Harvest when the first fruit shows color, picking when fully mature but before softening on the tree, using shears to leave a short stem section. Do not store below 50°F. Fruit ripens best stem end down in trays at room temperature, covered with a dampened cloth to prevent shriveling. Ripening occurs in June from January bloom in interior California, and October from April bloom on the coast, with less time needed for greenhouse fruit. In Florida, mangoes are shipped from late July to late September.

Regarding specific varieties, Mulgoba has irregular fruiting habits and does not bear young, but has been shipped commercially. Alfonso is preferred commercially over Paheri due to superior keeping qualities. Amini trees are more productive. Cambodiana trees are much more regular in fruiting. Bennett's dull orange-yellow color is a market disadvantage. Sandersha is valued for its regularity in bearing and is excellent for cooking.
propagation:
Growing mangos from seed is a gamble, as supermarket fruits may have been sterilized or chilled too long. Viable seeds should be removed from the husk and planted (before drying) with the hump at soil level, germinating in two to four weeks, ideally with bottom heat. Polyembryonic seedlings should be carefully separated after sprouting, and thinned to one shoot if multiple appear. Seedling mangos will bloom and bear fruit in three to six years. Seeds should be planted soon after removal as viability is short.

The primary vegetative methods are inarching (grafting by approach) and budding. Inarching, common in India, is slow but certain, involving joining a branch from the desired variety to a 10-12 month old seedling (stem < 0.5 inch diameter). Make 3-inch slices on both, bind with waxed tape, and cover with wax. Union takes several months, after which the seedling top is removed above the union and the scion severed from the parent tree below. Budding is faster and more practical for nurseries. Seedling stocks are grown from seeds (husk removed, planted in 5-inch pots, barely covered), and budding is done when plants have a stem diameter of a lead pencil or greater and are flushing. Budwood should be round, straight, smooth, from the second flush (discarding the most recent), and as dormant as possible (grayish cast, no wine-colored leaves). Buds are cut 1-1.5 inches long. A T or inverted T incision is made in the stock, the bud inserted, and bound with waxed tape (muslin boiled in beeswax and rosin). April and May are propitious months in south Florida; in tropical regions, it can be done anytime stock plants are ready. After 3-4 weeks, the stock top is lopped above the bud. Large seedling trees can be top-worked by cutting main branches, allowing sprouts, and then budding the sprouts. Cambodiana seedlings have been propagated by budding.

Grafting can be successful in April and September, but better results are typically seen from May through August. Small, pencil-diameter plants graft well with a whip graft. On larger trees, a crown groove bark graft allows multiple scions. Fully grown trees can be topworked using crown or groove bark grafts, or by hard pruning and whip grafting sprouts later. Plastic bagging with a few drops of moisture improves graft success. Grafting in the second year, using cleft, side, or tongue (splice) grafts in midsummer, is effective when scion and stock are swelling for new growth. Leaves below the graft should remain, but suckers must be removed. Pencil-sized scions of hardwood with three or four nodes should be used and covered with a loose, punctured white paper bag for shade. When topworking, do not dehorn the entire tree; leave at least two fully leafed branches.

Marcottage is feasible in humid climates or greenhouses but yields few plants. Budding, though rare in California, can be done using a shield bud in an inverted T when the tree begins a new growth flush. Cuttings are rarely successful, though ethylene treatment may improve rooting.

Mangos are suitable for container or greenhouse culture, starting with established named cultivars, especially fine Indian types. A large tub with casters is recommended. Cultivar selection depends on the planting location; California-selected seedlings suit coastal counties, while Florida cultivars are generally better for the desert and Central Valley.
pests_and_diseases:
Common pests in greenhouses and orchards include scale, mealybugs, and mites. Thrips frequently cause leaves to turn rusty brown in greenhouses. Gophers are attracted to mango roots. Major insect pests also include various fruit-flies (e.g., Mediterranean, Queensland, mango, Mexican fruit-flies), whose larvae infest fruit, rendering it unfit. In India, the mango hopper and mango weevil are significant pests. In Florida, red-spider and thrips cause foliage injury. A large bark-boring beetle (Plocaederus ruficornis) causes damage in the Philippines.

Pest control methods include Malathion for insect pests, sulfur for mites, and kerosene emulsion for scale insects. Grove cleanliness, including gathering fallen fruit, helps reduce fruit-fly and weevil populations. Control of fruit-flies is difficult, with varied success from sweetened arsenical sprays and ongoing research into natural parasites. Natural enemies like coccinelid beetles are beneficial for scale control. Weekly hosing down helps control mites in greenhouses, and regular pesticide sprays may be needed for anthracnose and mealybugs.

The flower panicles, young fruit, and leaves are susceptible to powdery mildew (Oidium mangiferae), especially in rainy weather or frequent fog. This can often be controlled with a spray of powdered kelp at bud break, sodium bicarbonate, or fungicide sprays. Trees planted in pavement openings seldom develop mildew.

Bacterial spot (Colletotrichum oleosporides) distorts and blackens developing leaves and disfigures fruit, potentially spreading to new growth. The primary fungous disease is blossom-blight or anthracnose (Colletotrichum gleosporioides), which thrives in moist weather and can destroy flowers. Anthracnose can be controlled with bimonthly applications of copper spray or captan, starting when a growth flush begins and continuing until flowers open, then resuming when fruits begin to form. Prevention also includes spraying with Bordeaux mixture (5 lbs copper sulfate, 5 lbs unslaked lime to 50 gallons water) during bloom and fruit development. Maintaining a dry atmosphere in a greenhouse with a fan for air movement and ventilators helps prevent anthracnose. Amini seems slightly more resistant to blight.

Mango trees are very sensitive to root loss caused by digging, transplanting, or gopher damage. "Soft nose," a physical disorder characterized by shriveling at the fruit apex, is associated with excessive nitrogen in the soil. Exposed fruits can also suffer from sunburn in high temperatures.

description: The Olive (Olea europaea) is a small evergreen tree or shrub in the family Oleaceae, native to the coastal areas of the eastern Mediterranean Basin, western Asia, northern Africa, and northern Iran. It is short and squat, rarely exceeding 8-15 m in height, with a typically gnarled and twisted trunk. Its silvery green, oblong leaves measure 4-10 cm long and 1-3 cm wide. Small white flowers are borne in racemes on the previous year's wood. The fruit is a small drupe, 1-2.5 cm long, harvested green or when ripened to a rich purple color, and is of major agricultural importance as the source of olive oil and as a food/condiment. The cultivated olive has been grown since prehistoric times in Asia Minor, spreading to Mediterranean countries, and later to America, Australia, and South Africa, thriving particularly in California. The wild form, oleaster, has small rigid leaves and little flesh, used as a stock for grafting. Olives prefer deep, well-drained, fertile soils and a clear, dry atmosphere, requiring a greater annual sum of heat than the vine and being sensitive to winter cold below 14-19°F. Common cultivated varieties include Mission, Ascolano, Manzanillo, and Sevillano, with a trend towards large pickling fruit varieties that are also rich in good quality oil. Other varieties mentioned include Obliza, Salonica, Regalis, Empeltre, Columbella, Nevadillo bianco, Oblonga, Pendulina, Uvaria, Nevadillo (a good oil olive), Picholine (excellent for pickling in California), Redding Picholine (small, useless fruit), and Frantoio (Italian oil olive, grows well in California but small fruit).
cultivation: Olive trees prefer calcareous, light, well-drained, and fairly fertile soils, flourishing on limestone slopes and crags, and in coastal climates. Rich soils can predispose them to disease and poorer oil. They thrive in hot weather, tolerating drought well due to extensive root systems, but mature trees can be injured by temperatures below -10°C (or 14-19°F). A clear, dry atmosphere is favorable, as summer rains or fogs can lead to black scale. Olives require a slightly greater annual sum of heat than the vine. Trees can live for centuries and remain productive with correct and regular pruning, which also keeps them to limited dimensions and low for easy fruit gathering, preserving flower-bearing shoots. Planting distances vary widely, from 15 to 72 feet, often 20-36 feet, and they can be planted in solid orchards or as avenue/border trees. Irrigation is often necessary, typically heavy winter and spring irrigations, supplemented before blossoming and when fruit is nearly full size. Pruning in the first two years forms a single trunk, gradually removing lower branches to create a clean trunk of 2-3 feet. Annual winter pruning maintains symmetry and thins out old or unproductive branches. Some varieties, like Pendolino, require cross-pollination. Spaces between trees are regularly fertilized. Harvesting occurs in autumn and winter: green olives from late September to mid-November, blond from mid-October to late November, and black from mid-November to early February. For green pickles, olives are gathered just before full size and color change; for ripe pickles, when uniformly black; and for oil, maximum quantity from fully mature, soft olives, with finest oil from olives gathered just before softening. Olives are typically hand-picked to avoid bruising, though special rakes can be used for oil olives. The naturally bitter fruit is subjected to fermentation or cured with lye or brine to make it palatable. Pickling involves cleaning, sizing, removing bitterness with soda or potash lye, hardening, and preserving in salt brine. Oil making involves partially drying, crushing, and pressing the pulp, followed by washing and clarification. Specific varieties like Nevadillo bianco, Oblonga, Pendulina, and Uvaria need favorable conditions for pickling-sized fruit, while Nevadillo thrives for oil production, and Frantoio grows well in California.
propagation: Olives are propagated in various ways, with cuttings or layers being preferred methods. Cuttings are typically taken in January and February when the tree is dormant. Nurserymen often use 'tips' (mature shoot ends 4-5 inches long) planted in sand under glass with artificial heat, then transplanted. Larger cuttings (14 inches long, ¾-1½ inches diameter) from older wood can be rooted without a greenhouse, with rooting facilitated by splitting or scratching the bottom. In Spain, large branches (truncheons) are cut, incised, and placed horizontally in trenches to root. Branches of about 1 meter in length, planted deeply in manured ground, also vegetate. Shorter pieces can be laid horizontally in shallow trenches to produce sucker-like shoots. Suckers from the stump or trunk base with old wood attached are also used, though yields from suckers or seeds are generally poor, requiring budding or grafting for optimal performance. In Greece, grafting cultivated trees onto wild trees is common. In Italy, embryonic buds are carefully excised and planted to form vigorous shoots. Large branches are occasionally 'marched' to obtain young trees. Propagation by seeds is possible, yielding stronger root systems and more fruitful trees, but seedlings require grafting and take longer. To facilitate germination, the oily pericarp is softened by slight rotting, soaking in hot water or an alkaline solution, or by treating stones with a 10% caustic soda solution. Clipping the apex of the pit can significantly hasten germination. Large-seeded varieties like Mission are hard to germinate, while small-fruited ones like Redding and Chemlali are better.
