Seedlings are transplanted to nursery rows in their second or third year and budded or grafted the following spring, ready for the orchard 1-2 years later. Grafting methods include side- or whip-grafts in early spring, or twig-budding after sap flow on one-year-old seedlings. Older seedlings can be crown-grafted or twig-budded higher up. Old trees are often grafted to change to more desirable varieties. The Redding Picholine is noted as a seedling.
pests_and_diseases: Olive trees are susceptible to several pests and diseases. A fungus, Cycloconium oleaginum, can infect trees, causing damage, though it is generally not important and rarely causes slight weakening. A bacterium, Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. oleae (also referred to as Bacterium savastanoi), induces tumor growth (olive-knot) on shoots, leaves, twigs, branches, and the trunk, weakening trees and potentially being fatal, especially to young trees in rich, moist, warmer districts. Its spread may be controlled by quarantine and eradication. Lepidopterous caterpillars feed on leaves and flowers. More serious damage is caused by olive-fly attacks to the fruit, though the noxious olive-fly and olive-moth of Europe are not present in California. The black scale bug (Saissetia oleae) is very injurious in moister coastal regions, reducing fruit quality and being difficult to control; its main predators are wasps. In dry summer air, the scale is absent or not injurious. The curculio beetle eats the edges of leaves, leaving sawtooth damage. Rabbits can cause considerable damage by eating the bark of olive trees, especially young ones, potentially killing the tree if the bark is removed circumferentially. Fungous decays of roots and stem are noted but usually cause little damage. Some large-fruited varieties like Macrocarpa and Polymorpha are liable to a disease causing spotting or softening of the flesh. The Nevadillo variety is sensitive to cold and subject to dry-rot of the fruit.

description: Hydrangea (Greek, hydor, water, and aggeion, vessel; alluding to the cup-shaped fruit) is a genus of about 35-75 species of ornamental woody flowering plants, with the greatest diversity in eastern Asia, N. and S. America, and the Himalayas. They are chiefly grown for their showy white, pink, or blue flowers. Most are deciduous shrubs 1-3 m tall, some are small trees, and others are lianas reaching up to 30 m, rarely vines climbing by rootlets. They have opposite, petioled, serrate (sometimes entire, rarely lobed) leaves without stipules. Flowers are produced from early spring to late autumn in terminal flowerheads (corymbs or panicles) at the ends of stems. Flowerheads often contain small fertile flowers in the middle and large, sterile bract-like flowers (with enlarged showy sepals) around the edge, though some species or varieties have all fertile flowers of the same size or all sterile and enlarged. Flowers are typically white, but in species like H. macrophylla, they can be blue, red, pink, or purple, with the color depending on soil pH (acidic soils produce blue, neutral soils pale cream, and alkaline soils pink or purple). Hydrangeas accumulate aluminum from acidic soils, which contributes to their blue color. Botanically, flowers are perfect, with 4-5 calyx-lobes and petals, usually 10 stamens, an inferior or half-inferior ovary, and 2-5 short styles. The capsule is 2-5-celled, dehiscent at the base of the styles, with many minute seeds. All parts of the plant contain cyanogenic glycosides, making them mildly toxic if eaten, though poisoning is rare. Hardiness varies by species, with H. paniculata being the hardiest, and others like H. involucrata, H. opidoides, H. sargentiana, H. heteromalla, and H. davidii being more tender.
cultivation: Hydrangeas are popular ornamental plants, widely grown for their large flowerheads, with H. macrophylla having over 600 named cultivars. They grow best in rich, porous, and somewhat moist soil, thriving in partly shaded positions but flowering more freely in full sun with sufficient moisture. They are well adapted for shrubbery borders, and species like H. paniculata and H. opidoides are showy as single lawn specimens. H. opuloides can be used for ornamental hedges in warmer climates. Pruning varies by species: some are best pruned annually when new leaf buds appear to prevent legginess, while others only flower on 'old wood', meaning new wood from pruning will not produce flowers until the following season. Most species should be pruned in fall or early spring, cutting back previous year's branches to one to three pairs of buds. Severe pruning can produce enormous panicles that may need support. H. paniculata var. grandiflora can be grown as a small standard tree with specific pruning and care. In colder climates, standard plants may need to be lifted and stored, and hardy hydrangeas can be protected by tying branches and covering with earth. H. opuloides, being tender to frost, is often grown as a pot-plant in the North, requiring specific winter storage, repotting, and watering. They can also be planted outdoors for summer and then lifted for winter storage. Pink hortensias may turn blue in certain soils with the addition of iron filings or alum. H. opuloides is also valuable for forcing for Easter. H. petiolaris is a climbing plant that grows well in shade but flowers freely only in full sun.
propagation: Hydrangeas are readily propagated by cuttings of half-ripened or nearly ripe wood under glass in summer, as well as by hardwood cuttings, layers, suckers, or division of older plants. H. quercifolia is best propagated by suckers or layers of growing wood put down in summer. Propagation by seeds is rare; seeds are very small and should be sown in fall in pans or boxes, only slightly covered with soil. For forcing H. opuloides, pot-plants can be grown in one year from cuttings inserted in February or March in a propagating-house with slight bottom heat, followed by potting, outdoor growth in plunged pots, multiple transplantations, and liberal watering and manuring. Alternatively, they can be planted out in rich, full-sun soil with liberal watering and liquid manure. For winter, they should be repotted into 8-inch pots in late September, kept shady, then exposed to sun, and moved to a cool greenhouse after first frosts. For Easter flowering, transfer to a warmer house (50°-60°F) in early January, with free watering and weekly liquid manure until flower-buds develop. After flowering, plants are pruned and repotted or planted out, or new plants are raised from cuttings.
pests_and_diseases: BACTERIAL DISEASES: Bacterial blight (Pseudomonas solanacearum), Bacterial leaf spot (Pseudomonas cichorii). FUNGAL DISEASES: Bud and flower blight (Botrytis cinerea), Interveinal chlorosis (Lack of iron uptake due to alkaline soils), Fungal leaf spots (Cercospora hydrangeae), (Corynespora cassicola), (Phyllosticta hydrangeae), (Septoria hydrangeae), Powdery mildew (Erysiphe polygoni), Root rot (Pythium spp.), Rust (Pucciniastrum hydrangeae), Southern blight (Sclerotium rolfsii), (teleomorph: Athelia rolfsii (Curzi) Tu & Kimbrough). VIRAL DISEASES: Phyllody (Hydrangea ringspot virus), Ringspots (Hydrangea ringspot virus), (Tobacco ringspot virus), (Tomato spotted wilt virus), (Tomato ringspot virus).

description: Tropical and subtropical trees and shrubs, which may be evergreen or deciduous. Branches may be spiny in some species, with double thorns - one hooked and one straight per node. Shiny green alternate leaves, usually with 3 prominent veins from leaf base. Small flowers are not significant, but come in axillary clusters, in cream, white, yellow, greenish tones. Small fleshy fruit are sometimes edible. Best known species is _Ziziphus jujuba_ , the [jujube](/wiki/Jujube "Jujube"), cultivated in China since antiquity.
cultivation: Best grown in a sunny spot in well drained deep soil that retains moisture and has good drainage. Needs strong wind protection. Water regularly during growing season. Prune tips if you want to keep it compact.
propagation: From seed or by rooting cuttings. Named fruiting cultivars can be obtained from grafting.
pests_and_diseases: The larvae of some Lepidoptera species eat Ziziphus species, they include Bucculatrix zizyphella, which feeds only on Ziziphus, as well as Endoclita malabaricus.

description: Lupins, or lupines, are members of the genus Lupinus in the legume family (Fabaceae), comprising 200 to 600 species primarily found in South America, western North America, the Mediterranean region, and Africa. They are mostly herbaceous perennial plants, 0.3-1.5 m (1-5 ft) tall, but some are annuals, a few are shrubs up to 3 m (10 ft) tall, and one species (Lupinus jaimehintoniana) can be a tree up to 8 m high. They have characteristic palmately divided leaves, usually with 5–28 leaflets, often bearing silvery hairs. Flowers are produced in dense or open whorls on an erect spike, 1-2 cm long, with a typical peaflower shape (standard, wings, and keel), and can be blue, white, yellow, or variegated. The fruit is a pod containing several seeds. Like other legumes, lupins fix nitrogen from the atmosphere into ammonia via rhizobium-root nodule symbiosis, allowing them to thrive in infertile soils. Some species have long central tap roots or proteoid roots. Lupins contain secondary compounds like isoflavones and toxic alkaloids such as lupinine and sparteine.
cultivation: Lupins are popular ornamental plants in gardens, with numerous hybrids and cultivars available. They are generally of easy cultivation in any garden soil, but are said not to succeed in soil containing lime. Some species, like Garden Lupin (Lupinus polyphyllus) and Rainbow Lupin (L. × regalis), are common garden flowers. However, others, such as the Yellow Bush Lupin (L. arboreus) and Lupinus polyphyllus in New Zealand, are considered invasive weeds when growing outside their native range. It is rumored that soaking lupin seeds in water can improve growth.
propagation: Lupins are propagated by seed. Perennial species can also be propagated by division. It is recommended to sow seeds directly where the plants are desired, as they do not tolerate transplanting well once established.
pests_and_diseases: The most significant diseases affecting lupins include anthracnose, wilting, and root rot diseases caused by Fusarium and other pathogens, as well as some bacterial and viral diseases.

description: Hypericum is a genus of about 400 species of flowering plants in the family Clusiaceae, commonly known as St. John's-wort, hypericum, or tutsan. It has a nearly worldwide distribution, absent from tropical lowlands, deserts, and polar regions. These plants vary from annual or perennial herbaceous herbs (5–10 cm tall) to shrubs and small trees (up to 12 m tall). Leaves are opposite, simple oval, 1–8 cm long, either deciduous or evergreen, entire, and dotted with pellucid or opaque glands. Flowers are typically bright yellow, rarely pink or purplish, ranging from 0.5–6 cm in diameter, with five (rarely four) petals, usually appearing in terminal cymes. The fruit is generally a dry capsule that releases numerous small seeds, though some species produce fleshy, berry-like fruits. Hypericum species are cultivated for their ornamental value, bright yellow flowers, and interesting foliage and habits. While most species are tender in northern regions, some, like H. aureum, H. prolificum, H. lobocarpum, H. calycinum, H. patulum Var. henryi, H. kalmianum, and H. ascyron, have proven hardy with protection. They are generally short-lived and should be renewed when showing signs of exhaustion. Larger varieties are suitable for shrubbery borders, while low-growing types like H. calycinum are excellent as ground-cover due to their rapid spread by suckers. Low, tufted, or prostrate species are well-suited for rockeries.
cultivation: Hypericum thrives in any good loamy soil, and also in sandy soil if sufficiently moist. They are suitable for dry, poor soils. Most species prefer partly shaded situations and bloom longer if not exposed to full sun, although some, such as Hypericum calycinum, will also tolerate full sun.
propagation: Hypericum can be propagated by seeds, cuttings, division, and suckers. Seeds should be surface sown between January and May and placed in a cold frame; pre-soaking in distilled water may increase germination rates. Softwood cuttings can be taken from the tips of current growth in August, rooted in sandy compost, and placed in a cold frame. Shrubby species can also be propagated by greenwood cuttings under glass in summer. Creeping kinds, such as H. calycinum, and some herbaceous species can be propagated by division and suckers.
pests_and_diseases: Hypericum is remarkably resilient to pests and diseases. Very occasionally, plants may be infected by rust.

description: The fig (Ficus carica) is a picturesque deciduous tree, typically 10-30 ft tall, with muscular, twisting branches that spread wider than they are tall. Its wood is weak and the sap contains irritating milky latex. It can also grow as a multiple-branched shrub or be espaliered. The bright green leaves are large (up to 1 ft), single, alternate, and deeply lobed, rough hairy on top and soft hairy underneath. The tiny flowers are hidden inside the green "fruits" (synconium), which are botanically a hollow, pear-shaped receptacle containing many minute seeds. Common figs have all-female flowers requiring no pollination. Other types include caprifigs (male and female, requiring Blastophaga grossorum wasp), Smyrna figs (needing cross-pollination by caprifigs for fruit perfection, best for drying), and San Pedro figs (first crop independent, second crop dependent on pollination). The "fruit" bears a breba crop in spring on old growth and a main crop in fall on new growth. It has a tough peel (green, brown, or purple) that often cracks when ripe, revealing a white inner rind and a seed mass with jelly-like flesh. Edible seeds are numerous and hollow unless pollinated, with pollinated seeds providing a nutty taste to dried figs. The fig is a warm-temperate fruit, capable of withstanding 10° to 20° of frost under favorable conditions, and is a long-lived, ornamental tree. Commercial figs are often dried. Key botanical varieties include the inedible Capri fig (Ficus carica var. sylvestris), the Smyrna fig (Ficus carica var. smyrniaca), and self-fertilizing table/preserving varieties (Ficus carica var. hortensis). Figs are classified by shape (round, turbinate, pyriform, obovate), skin color (green, yellow, brown, purple, black), and flesh color (white, opaline, red) and texture (melting, spicy, juicy, coarse, dry). Popular varieties include Celeste (hardy, excellent for canning), Turkey/Brown Turkey (hardy, prolific, dwarf growth), Brunswick (large, good for fresh market), and Magnolia (vigorous, prolific, good for canning, but prone to splitting in rainy weather). Related species mentioned include Ficus sycomorus (Sycamore fig), Ficus religiosa (Peepul tree), and Ficus elastica (India rubber tree).
cultivation: Figs grow best and produce high-quality fruit in Mediterranean and dry warm-temperate climates, thriving where temperatures do not fall below 18°F and summers are not cold, windy, or excessively foggy. Rain during fruit development can cause splitting. They require full sun exposure all day to ripen palatable fruits and are suitable for USDA Zones 10-12 and Sunset Zones 4-9, 12-31, warmer 32, and pots. Chilling requirements are less than 300 hours. Fully dormant trees are hardy to 12°-15°F, but active growth can be damaged at 30°F. In borderline climates or northern/middle parts of the United States, figs can be grown outdoors with frost protection: plants can be lifted in early November and stored, bent to the ground and covered with earth or pine boughs, planted against a wall for heat radiation, or grown as a bush by pruning the trunk to ground level and allowing multiple stems. Tub or pot plants can be brought indoors during winter. Keep roots as dry as possible during winter. Exposed trunks and branches should be whitewashed to prevent sun damage. Figs can be planted 10x15 or 12x20 feet apart in thoroughly plowed, pulverized clay or heavy, uniformly moist soils, avoiding light sandy soils prone to nematode damage. Planting occurs in January/February in the lower South, or after spring opens in colder sections, ensuring roots do not dry out. Figs are best grown in a bush form with three or four trunks to minimize loss from severe winter weather. For container-grown plants, replace most of the soil every three years and keep the sides of the tub shaded. Pruning should be minimal once the tree form is established, focusing on removing suckers, dead/injured branches, and shaping in early seasons. Avoid heavy winter pruning, which causes loss of the following year's crop, as fruit is borne on terminals of previous year's wood. It is better to prune immediately after the main crop is harvested, or with late-ripening cultivars, summer prune half the branches and prune the remainder the following summer. If radical pruning is done, whitewash the entire tree. For table figs, trees are headed low (18 inches) to facilitate picking, or higher for drying figs, which are allowed to ripen and fall. Young fig trees should be watered regularly until fully established. Mature trees in dry western climates need deep watering at least every one or two weeks; desert gardeners may need more frequent watering. Mulch the soil to conserve moisture, as drought-stressed trees will not produce fruit, are more susceptible to nematode damage, and leaves will turn yellow and drop. Regular fertilizing is usually necessary only for potted trees or when grown on sands. Excess nitrogen encourages rank growth at the expense of fruit production. Fertilize if branches grew less than a foot the previous year, applying 1/2 - 1 pound of actual nitrogen, divided into three or four applications from late winter/early spring to July. Shallow cultivation is performed weekly or bi-weekly from spring until July 1, followed by cover-crops. Figs must be allowed to ripen fully on the tree before picking; they will not ripen if picked immature. A ripe fruit will be slightly soft and starting to bend at the neck. Harvest gently to avoid bruising. Fresh figs keep for only 2-3 days in the refrigerator. Some varieties are delicious when dried, taking 4-5 days in the sun or 10-12 hours in a dehydrator, and can be stored for six to eight months. Caprification, involving the fig-wasp (Blastophaga grossorum) and Capri figs, is essential for Smyrna fig varieties.
propagation: Figs are very easily propagated from hardwood cuttings. Cuttings of well-ripened wood from the previous season's growth, 4 to 5 inches long (or foot-long pieces less than 1 inch diameter with two-year-old wood at the base), cut through the nodes, are taken in the fall or winter. They can be started with bottom heat or planted directly in nurseries. Single-eye cuttings can also be used. Dip cuttings in a rooting hormone and allow them to callus one week in a moist place at 50-60°F. Summer cuttings can also be made but do best if defoliated and winterized in refrigeration for 2-3 weeks before potting; leafy shoots require a mist bed. New varieties are obtained from seeds, particularly fertile seeds from imported Smyrna figs or occasionally from common varieties. Budding is best done by the annular or ring method. Cleft-grafting is possible in February in California with extreme care to exclude air. Particularly rare cultivars may be propagated on rootstocks, or older trees can be topworked by whip, cleft, or crown grafting, or chip or patch budding. Rooted cuttings should be planted in 22 to 30 feet squares, depending on soil capacity and ultimate tree size. Keep roots moist until planted. Never transplant or disturb a young tree while it is starting new growth in spring, as this is likely to kill it. Cut the tree back to 2 ft high upon planting and whitewash the trunk.
pests_and_diseases: Figs are generally robust, with California trees noted for having very few insect enemies and not being subject to disease. However, several issues can arise. Root pests include gophers, who can easily kill a large plant (control: plant in a large aviary wire basket). Nematodes (root-knot) are a serious problem in light sandy soils, attacking roots, forming galls, and stunting trees; drought-stressed trees are more susceptible. Fruit damage can be caused by birds. Mitadulid and Carpophilus dried fruit beetles can enter ripening fruit through the eye, introducing fungi and rots; they frequently breed in fallen citrus fruits, so control involves keeping a clean orchard by destroying fallen fruits and not growing near citrus trees. The very dangerous fig moth (Ephestra cautella) can infest fruit with eggs and larvae, leading to destruction of affected cargoes, though it is not yet in California. Euryphid mites cause little damage but are carriers of mosaic virus from infected to clean trees. Mosaic virus, formerly considered benign, probably causes crop reduction. Symptoms resemble potassium deficiency—leaves are marbled with yellow spots, and the veins are light colored. Symptoms are often not apparent until the tree is older or when it becomes heat or water-stressed. Control involves not purchasing infected trees and isolating those which show symptoms. Fungal and bacterial diseases include fruit fermentation, known as 'fig-sour,' which can occur in some districts and seasons, sometimes linked to over-irrigation, lack of vitality, or tender/juicy varieties. Botrytis causes a blast of branch terminals, which dry out and turn charcoal-like; the attack usually starts from half-grown fruits damaged by the first frost of winter and enters the main stem as a reddish expanding necrotic zone. The infection is generally self-controlling and stops in the spring; it can be prevented by removing mummies and frost-damaged fruits. Fig canker is a bacterium which enters the trunk at damaged zones, causing necrosis and girdling and loss of branches; it usually starts at sunburned areas, so it is important to keep exposed branches whitewashed. Rhyzopus smut attacks ripened fruits on the tree, causing a charcoal black coating inside the fruit, and is worst on cultivars with large, open eyes. Most ripe fruit losses are from Endosepsis (Fusarium) and Aspergillus rot, which are introduced by insects, even pollinating wasps. The fruit appears to burst, or a ropy, mucus-like exudate drains from the eye, rendering the fruit inedible. The best control is to destroy all crop for one year, apply diazinon granules beneath trees to eliminate insect vectors, and destroy adjacent wild trees. Penicillium fungus will attack dried fruits in storage but can be controlled by keeping them dry, or sulfuring before storage.

description: The apple is a fruiting deciduous tree, typically 3 to 12 meters tall, with a broad, often densely twiggy crown. It can also be a round-headed tree or large bush. Its leaves are alternately arranged simple ovals, 5 to 12 cm long, with serrated margins, or oval, ovate, or orbicular-ovate, pointed at the apex, rounded at the base, soft, dull, with irregularly serrate margins on stout petioles. Foliage is often clustered on short shoots/spurs and slender axial growths. White to pink-tinged blossoms appear in spring, often large and showy, in close clusters of 5-6 per cyme, with 5 acuminate sepals, 5 obtuse petals (often pink outside), and about 20 stamens with yellow anthers. The fruit matures in autumn, typically 5 to 9 cm in diameter, containing five carpels with one to three seeds each. The fruit is very various, with a cavity about the stem, homogeneous flesh, and persistent calyx. Varieties include sylvestris, pumila (source of most pomological apples), astracanica, Niedzwetzkyana (red-tinged leaves, deep pink flowers, purplish fruit flesh), and apetala (bloomless). Horticultural forms include aurea (yellow-variegated leaves), plena (double flowers), and pendula (weeping habit). Native to southwestern Asia and adjacent Europe, it is a perennial plant and a hybrid. The seeds are toxic, and possibly the leaves. It thrives in sun and moist conditions, producing flowers, edible fruit, and attracting bees. It belongs to the Rosaceae family.
cultivation: Apples are widely cultivated in temperate climates. They are easily grown in most fertile, moisture-retentive, well-drained loamy soils, and also in heavy clay soils if well-drained. Rolling, inclined, or elevated lands are preferred for better drainage. The plant prefers a sunny position but tolerates partial shade, though fruiting may be reduced. It tolerates a pH range of 6-7, preferring 6.5-6.8. Ideal conditions include warm summer temperatures, freedom from spring frosts, wind protection (especially from cold north/east winds), and 600-800mm annual rainfall. About 1000 hours of winter temperatures below 7°C are needed for flower production. Trees can be grown against walls (south/west for most, east for tougher cultivars, north for early culinary). Best results are achieved with deep ground preparation before planting, followed by deep plowing for the first 1-3 years to encourage deep root growth and ameliorate soil, then shallow tillage to conserve moisture. Tillage should start in spring and cease by midsummer/August to allow for cover-crop growth. On good lands with sufficient moisture, a sod-mulch treatment (mowing grass and leaving it on the ground) can replace tillage. Planting can occur in fall or spring, with a standard spacing of 40 feet apart, though some varieties or regions may allow closer planting. While generally best to dedicate land solely to apples, fillers (other trees, shorter-lived apple varieties, or dwarf apples) can be used with careful management. Apples are self-incompatible and require cross-pollination, typically by honeybees, orchard mason bees, or bumblebees. Pollination groups (A-H) dictate compatibility based on flowering times. Fertilizers are typically not needed until trees are bearing on strong, well-handled lands. If growth is insufficient or foliage lacks color, nitrogen (nitrate of soda, sulfate of ammonia, blood, tankage at 200-300 lbs/acre) may be required. Potash and phosphoric acid (e.g., 300 lbs muriate of potash/acre) can be applied if trees show vigorous growth. Orchards in full bearing should receive liberal annual fertilization. In the East, trees are profitably bearing from 12 years for about 30 years. Lime applications (around 1,000 lbs/acre every 4-5 years) have shown good results. Companion planting with chives/alliums can prevent/cure scab, Equisetum spp. spray helps against scab, and climbing nasturtiums repel woolly aphis. Avoid storing apples with potatoes as it causes flavor loss in apples and imparts a bitter flavor to potatoes/carrots; growing near potatoes also increases blight susceptibility. Wrapping apples in maple leaves helps preservation. Apples store better if grown in a sward with high clover. Foxgloves and wallflowers improve growth and fruit quality. Dandelions can cause earlier fruit ripening due to ethylene gas. The fruit is a good wildlife food source.
propagation: Apple cultivars are primarily reproduced by grafting, either by budding or root-grafting. Common rootstocks include apple seedlings, often grown from seeds obtained from cider mills. Budded trees are preferred in the East, while root-grafted trees are favored in the upper Mississippi Valley to secure own-rooted trees of known hardiness. In Russia, Pyrus baccata seedlings are used as stocks to prevent root-killing and promote earlier fruit-bearing. Dwarf apples are created by grafting onto Paradise and Doucin stocks, which are naturally dwarf forms of the common apple. Trees are typically planted when two or three years old. Seeds are used to produce rootstocks or new varieties, but as a hybrid, seeds do not breed true. Seeds are best sown ripe in autumn in a cold frame, germinating in late winter. Stored seeds require 3 months of stratification at 1°C. Seedlings should be pricked out into individual pots and can be planted out in late summer with winter protection, or kept in a cold frame until late spring of the following year. Cuttings of mature wood can be taken in November in a frame.
pests_and_diseases: The primary enemies of the apple are the apple-worm (larva of the codlin-moth) and apple-scab. Other common issues include mildew, aphids, fireblight, Gymnosporangium rust, black spot, canker-worms, case-bearers, and bud-moths. Young trees' soft bark can be fed upon by mice and deer, especially in winter. Plants are also susceptible to honey fungus. These pests and diseases are controlled by spraying: arsenical poisons for the apple-worm (should occur as soon as the last petals fall) and lime-sulfur or bordeaux mixture for apple-scab (as soon as buds are well burst). In severely infected areas or with susceptible varieties, an initial scab spray may be necessary before buds swell. For insects like canker-worms, case-bearers, and bud-moths that appear before flowers open, arsenical poison should be added to the fungicide during early spraying. A general rule is to spray with a combined fungicide and insecticide mixture when buds burst and again after petals have fallen. Less spraying for fungous diseases may be needed in the plains country. Companion planting with chives/alliums and Equisetum spp. spray can also help prevent/cure scab, and climbing nasturtiums repel woolly aphis.

description: The genus Ipomoea, also known as Morning glory or Moonflower, is the largest in the family Convolvulaceae, with over 500 species. It occurs throughout tropical and subtropical regions and comprises annual and perennial herbaceous plants, lianas, shrubs, and small trees, with most species being twining climbing plants. Many species have spectacular, colorful flowers and are often grown as ornamental plants, attracting large Lepidoptera and hummingbirds. The genus also includes important food crops such as Sweet Potato (I. batatas) and Water Spinach (I. aquatica).
cultivation: Many species are often grown as ornamental plants due to their spectacular, colorful flowers.
pests_and_diseases: Many herbivores avoid Ipomoea due to high alkaloid content, which makes the plants unpalatable or toxic. However, Ipomoea species are used as food plants by the caterpillars of certain Lepidoptera. Diseases affecting Sweet Potato (I. batatas) may also infect other members of this genus.

description: Walnuts (genus Juglans) are deciduous trees, 10–40 meters tall (about 30–130 ft), with pinnate leaves (200–900 millimetres long, 5–25 leaflets) and chambered pith. The 21 species in the genus range across the north temperate Old World from southeast Europe east to Japan, and more widely in the New World from southeast Canada west to California and south to Argentina. The Latin name, Juglans, derives from Jupiter glans, "Jupiter's acorn," and the word walnut derives from Old English wealhhnutu, literally "foreign nut." Walnuts refer to Juglans regia (English/Persian walnut) and its fruit, and by extension, other species of the genus Juglans. They are broadly categorized into three horticultural groups: Persian/English (J. regia), North American (J. nigra), and East Asian (J. Sieboldiana and allies). The butternut (J. cinerea) is also part of this genus. Walnut kernels are rich in food properties and pleasing to the taste, with most species being fairly rapid growers that develop into shapely trees. The Persian walnut (J. regia) is the primary commercial species, known for its large nut and thin shell, and is native from the Balkans to southwest China. Black walnut species are more difficult to crack. Walnut trees produce male cylindrical catkins (about 10 cm long) from leafless shoots of the previous year, and female flowers appear in clusters at the peak of the current year's leafy shoots. The wood is hard, and the Persian walnut is widely cultivated for its delicious nuts.
cultivation: Walnuts require sun and moderate water; they are deciduous and edible. Commercial walnut cultivation, particularly of J. regia, is extensive in California, covering 45,000-50,000 acres, and is often a specialized crop. Other significant cultivation areas include Shahmirzad orchard in Iran (700-750 ha) and 230,700 ha of walnut-fruit forest in Kyrgyzstan. Optimal conditions include deep, rich alluvial loam with humus, good drainage (6-8 feet deep), and irrigation. Coastal regions with high humidity, frequent fogs, and moderate temperature ranges are favorable, while inland areas are susceptible to sun-scald, though new varieties are being developed for these conditions. Clean culture with winter cover-crops (e.g., melilotus clover, vetch, rye) is common, planted after harvest (Sept/Oct) and plowed under in March/April. Irrigation is typically by furrow system (1-6 applications per season), ensuring water penetrates 6-8 feet; pre-harvest irrigation aids in shuck opening and clean nut drop. Pruning is generally minimal, focusing on removing lower limbs, but annual thinning can enhance yield. Interplanting young groves with crops like lima beans, other vegetables, small-fruits, alfalfa, or occasionally apricots and peaches is practiced, with vegetables preferred to avoid dwarfing effects from tree crops. Optimal tree spacing is now considered 60 feet apart for larger varieties on rich soils. Harvesting involves shaking nuts from trees and gathering them from the ground. Nuts are then washed, dried, culled, sacked, and bleached (using electrolyzed salt-brine or a solution of chloride of lime and sal soda with sulfuric acid) to achieve an attractive light tan color, followed by further drying and grading based on size, shape, meat color, and quality. Walnuts are light-demanding, benefit from wind protection, and are drought-hardy. Interplanting with nitrogen-fixing plants like Elaeagnus or Alnus species can improve tree growth. For nut production, selecting compatible cultivars for pollination is crucial, and 'late flushing' varieties are important in northern latitudes to prevent spring frost damage. The Black Walnut (J. nigra) is also widely cultivated, primarily for its valuable wood, as its nuts have a smaller kernel and an extremely tough shell. Hinds' Black Walnut (J. hindsii) is widely used commercially as a rootstock for J. regia trees.
propagation: Walnut propagation has shifted from seedlings to budding and grafting in commercial quantities over the last 10-15 years. Popular varieties for propagation include Placentia, Eureka, Franquette, El Monte, and Prolific, with Placentia and Eureka dominating southern California nurseries. Black walnuts are widely used as rootstocks; J. Hindsii (northern California species) is favored for its vigor and resistance to adverse soil conditions. Eastern black walnuts (J. nigra) are considered less vigorous, and southern California black walnuts (J. californica) are less used due to profuse suckering and slower growth. Royal hybrid (J. nigra x California species) is also used as a rootstock, believed to be resistant to excessive soil moisture. The Paradox hybrid (J. regia x black species) is vigorous but not practical for wholesale. Nursery propagation typically involves crown-grafting in place: one-year-old black walnut roots are grafted in the second spring using a whip-graft, tied, waxed, and protected. Young trees are staked, and one-year-old trees (6-10 feet high) are preferred for planting, usually cut back to 5 feet, or 18 inches in some inland areas to train a single sprout. Walnut species freely interpollinate, leading to natural hybrids like Paradox and Royal, which are often used as rootstocks. Hinds' Black Walnut (J. hindsii) is widely used commercially as a rootstock for J. regia trees, which implies grafting as a propagation method for the Persian walnut.
pests_and_diseases: The primary insect and fungous issues affecting walnuts are walnut aphis and walnut blight or bacteriosis (Pseudomonas juglandis). Walnut aphis can be controlled with tobacco sprays, though damage is usually occasional. Currently, there is no known control for walnut blight, but selecting blight-resistant varieties offers future promise. Minor losses are attributed to red-spider, codlin-moth, and melaxuma. In California commercial production, J. Hindsii and Juglans x Paradox rootstocks are utilized for their resistance to Phytophthora and, to a limited extent, oak root fungus, although trees grafted on these rootstocks can still succumb to black line. The Butternut (J. cinerea) is currently endangered by an introduced disease called butternut canker, which is caused by the fungus Sirococcus clavigignenti.

description: The Loquat (Eriobotrya japonica), also known as Japanese medlar or Nispero, is a large evergreen shrub or small tree, typically growing 10-30 ft high, with a rounded crown, short trunk, and woolly new twigs. It is native to SE China and Japan, and is a perennial. Its boldly textured, elliptical-lanceolate leaves are 5-12 inches long, dark green and glossy above, whitish or rusty-hairy beneath, thick, stiff, and evergreen. Small, white, sweetly fragrant flowers with a rusty-wooly texture bloom in panicles in fall or early winter. The fruit, ripening in spring (March to June in California), grows in clusters and is oval, rounded, or pear-shaped, 1-3 inches long, with smooth or downy, yellow, orange, or red-blushed skin. The succulent, tangy flesh can be white, yellow, or orange, and ranges from sweet to subacid. Each fruit contains 1-9 large brown seeds. Loquats are normally pollinated by bees, with some cultivars being self-infertile. The tree is drought tolerant, edible, and can be grown as an ornamental or for fruit. It adapts to subtropical to mild-temperature climates, tolerating lows of 12°F, but extreme heat or cold can damage flowers (killing temperature for flower bud is about 19°F) and fruit. It thrives in full sun and is wind tolerant. The fruit is eaten fresh or used in pies, jams, jellies, and preserves.
cultivation: Loquats are wind tolerant and grow best in full sun, but also do well in partial shade. They can be used to shade patios or as espaliers. They thrive on a variety of moderately fertile soils, from light sandy loam to heavy clay and limestone, provided there is good drainage. Trees should be planted 20-24 feet apart in orchards. While drought tolerant, regular, deep watering, especially during blossom swelling and harvest, improves fruit quality; standing water should be avoided. Loquats benefit from regular, light nitrogen fertilizer applications (e.g., 1 lb. of 6-6-6 NPK three times a year for 8-10 ft trees), but excessive nitrogen reduces flowering. Some recommend fertilizing only once a year in midwinter to control growth. Pruning should occur just after harvest to maintain a low head for easier fruit thinning and harvest, remove crossing branches, and thin dense growth to allow light into the tree's center. Loquats respond well to severe pruning. Fruits should ripen fully on the tree (about 90 days from full flower opening) before harvesting, as unripe fruits are excessively acidic and do not ripen properly off the tree. Harvested fruit can be stored in the refrigerator for 1-2 weeks. Thinning flowers and young fruits can enhance fruit size and modify alternate-bearing patterns. Bagging fruit clusters can protect against sunburn and bird damage.
propagation: Seeds are generally used for ornamental purposes or as rootstock. For rootstock, seeds are washed and planted in flats or pots soon after removal from fruit; seedlings are transplanted when 6-7 inches high and are ready for top-working when the stem is 1/2 inch thick at the base. Loquats are propagated by various grafting methods, including shield-budding, side-veneer grafting, and cleft-grafting. Budding is preferred in the U.S. for commercial planting. Loquat seedling rootstock results in larger trees, while quince rootstock produces dwarfed, early-bearing trees with easier picking. Loquat cuttings are difficult to root. Grafted trees bear fruit in 2-3 years, compared to 8-10 years for seedling trees. Seeds should be planted immediately after removal from fruit, about 1 inch deep in light loam. Seedlings are moved to nursery rows when 6-7 inches tall. Budding is best done in October or November, using young, smooth, brown wood. Shield-budding, similar to citrus, is common, with buds cut 1.5 inches long. Buds are tied with raffia or waxed tape for about three weeks to form a union, then unwrapped or loosely rewrapped to encourage growth. The stock is cut back above the bud, and adventitious buds are removed. Cleft grafting uses a pencil-diameter scion of well-matured wood.
pests_and_diseases: In California, loquats face few pests, though black scale infestations may occasionally appear. Fruit flies are a serious problem in affected areas. Birds will peck at ripe fruit, causing damage, and deer browse on foliage. Fire blight, caused by Erwinia amylovora, is a major enemy, especially in areas with late spring/summer rains or high humidity, spread by bees during flowering. Control involves preventive fungicides or bactericides and removal of scorched branches, cutting well into live wood, with prunings burned or sealed for disposal. Crown rot (Phytophthora) and cankers (Pseudomonas Eriobotrya) are occasional problems. Pear blight (Bacillus amylovorus) and loquat scab (Fusicladiurn eriobotryae) can be troublesome in California orchards, and a borer is reported in Japan.

description: Cannabis sativa is an annual plant in the Cannabaceae family. It is an herb that has been used throughout recorded history by humans as a source of fiber, for its seed oil, as food (see hemp), as a drug, as medicine, and for spiritual purposes. Each part of the plant is harvested differently, depending on the purpose of its use.
cultivation: The flowers of the female plant are arranged in racemes and can produce hundreds of seeds. Male plants shed their pollen and die several weeks prior to seed ripening on the female plants. Although genetic factors dispose a plant to become male or female, environmental factors including the diurnal light cycle can alter sexual expression. Naturally occurring monoecious plants, with both male and female parts, are either sterile or fertile but artificially induced "hermaphrodites" (a commonly used misnomer) can have fully functional reproductive organs. "Feminized" seed sold by many commercial seed suppliers are derived from artificially "hermaphrodytic" females that lack the male gene, or by treating the seeds with hormones or silver thiosulfate. A Cannabis plant in the vegetative growth phase of its life requires more than 12–13 hours of light per day to stay vegetative. Flowering usually occurs when darkness equals at least 12 hours per day. The flowering cycle can last anywhere between five to ten weeks, depending on the strain and environmental conditions. In soil, the optimum pH for the plant is 6.3 to 6.8. In hydroponic growing, the nutrient solution is best at 5.2 to 5.8, making Cannabis well-suited to hydroponics because this pH range is hostile to most bacteria and fungi. Cultivars primarily cultivated for their fiber, characterized by long stems and little branching. Cultivars grown for seed from which hemp oil is extracted. Cultivars grown for medicinal or recreational purposes. A nominal if not legal distinction is often made between industrial hemp, with concentrations of psychoactive compounds far too low to be useful for that purpose, and marijuana. Cannabis is very adaptable to soil and climatic conditions. It prefers a rich loamy soil with plenty of humus but it succeeds in ordinary garden soil and also in calcareous soils. When grown for fibre, it requires a mild temperate climate with at least 67cm annual rainfall, with abundant rain whilst the seeds are germinating and until young plants become established. Cannabis thrives on rich, fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline, well-drained silt or clay loams with moisture retentive subsoils, it does not grow well on acid, sandy soils. Of the many types of hemp, some are adapted to most vegetated terrains and climates. Cannabis is reported to tolerate an annual precipitation range of 30 to 400cm, an average annual temperature range of 6 to 27°C and a pH in the range of 4.5 to 8.2. Plants require little cultivation, except for weeding during early stages of growth. Hemp grows rapidly and soon crowds out weeds. After the plants are 20 cm tall, weeding is abandoned. Hemp tends to exhaust the soil of nutrients, though some nutrients are returned to the soil after plants are harvested. As Cannabis sativa has been cultivated for over 4,500 years for different purposes, many varieties and cultivars have been selected for specific purposes, as fibre, oil or narcotics. Drug-producing selections grow better and produce more drug in the tropics; oil and fibre producing plants thrive better in the temperate and subtropical areas. Many of the cultivars and varieties have been named as to the locality where it is grown mainly. However, all so called varieties freely interbreed and produce various combinations of the characters. The form of the plant and the yield of fibre from it vary according to climate and particular variety. Varieties cultivated particularly for their fibres have long stalks, branch very little, and yield only small quantities of seed. Varieties which are grown for the oil from their seed are short in height, mature early and produce large quantities of seed. Varieties grown for the drugs are short, much-branched with smaller dark-green leaves. Between these three main types of plants are numerous varieties which differ from the main one in height, extent of branching and other characteristics. At least one variety has been selected for its virtually insignificant content of the narcotic principles. This form is monoecious whereas most other forms are dioecious. There is also said to be a tall Chinese form that has no narcotic effect. Certain varieties do not form many side-shoots and these are the forms most commonly grown for their fibre. Plants grown in warmer climates tend to be best for medicinal use, whilst those grown in more northerly latitudes produce the better fibre. The seed is very attractive to birds and is often included in bird seed mixtures.
propagation: Seed - sow in early spring in the greenhouse. When they are large enough to handle, prick the seedlings out into individual pots and plant them out into their permanent positions in early summer. Seeds germinate well at low temperatures, but not below 1°C. The seed can also be sown outdoors in situ in mid spring.
pests_and_diseases: A good companion plant for cabbages and other brassicas, it repels the cabbage white butterfly and also secretes a volatile essence from its roots that inhibits pathogenic micro-organisms in the soil.

description: Bactris gasipaes is a species of palm native to the tropical forests of South and Central America. It is a palm which can typically grow to 20 m or taller, with pinnate leaves 3 m long on a 1 m long petiole. The fruit is a drupe with an edible pulp surrounding the single seed, 4-6 cm long and 3-5 cm broad. The rind (epicarp) of this wild palm can be red, yellow, or orange when the fruit is ripe depending on the variety of the palm. B. gasipaes is well known by the native populations where it usually grows up, and they having been used for centuries as food. They frequently eat the fruit stewed in salted water. However, it may be peeled and dressed with salt and honey, used to make compotes and jellies, or also used to make flour and edible oil. Many species of birds feed on this wild palm, notably macaws, parrots and parakeets, all members of the family Psittacidae. Some of these are endemic species, which are in danger of extinction. According to the Standard Cyclopedia of Horticulture, Bactris gasipaes, HBK. (Guilielma speciosa, Mart.) is a stem about 60 ft. high, single or cespitose, with rings of subulate-compressed black spines, 1 in. long, the rings about as far apart as the diameter of the stem. Leaves are 6 ft. long, curving; segments are dark green above, pale green below, very numerous, approximate, 1½ ft. long, 1¼ in. wide, linear-lanceolate, long-acuminate, bristly or minutely prickly along the margins. The fruit is orange or reddish, nearly 2 in. long, ovoid, found in the Lower Amazon.
pests_and_diseases: Pest Susceptibility: Mite (Tetranychus mexicanus), ant (Atta spp.), (Rhychophorus palmarum), (Strategus aloeus). Disease Susceptibility: Anthracnose (Colletotrichum gloesporioides), ascomycotic fungi (Helminthosporium sp.), (Phytophthora sp.), Mycosphaerella leaf spot disease (Mycosphaerella sp.), brown rot (Monilia sp.).

description: The Pomegranate (Punica granatum) is a large deciduous shrub or small tree, typically growing 2-4m, but can reach 6-9m. A dwarf variety, var. nana, is 1-2m tall. It is usually deciduous, with a red-brown bark that becomes gray, stiff, angular, and often spiny branches, and a tendency to sucker from the base. Pomegranates are long-lived, with some specimens over 200 years old. The glossy, leathery leaves are narrow and lance-shaped, 3-8cm long, arranged in opposite pairs. Flowers are attractive, usually scarlet (occasionally pink, white, or variegated), 3cm across, with five (or more) crumpled petals and a persistent red, fleshy, tubular calyx. They are self-pollinated and cross-pollinated by insects. The fruit is nearly round, 6-12cm in diameter, crowned by the calyx, with a tough, leathery skin typically yellow overlaid with pink or red. The interior contains compartments with sacs filled with sweetly acid, juicy, red, pink, or whitish pulp (aril) and one angular seed. High temperatures are essential for best fruit flavor. Bearing can begin one year after planting, but 2.5-3 years is more common, with fruit maturing 5-7 months after bloom. Native to southwestern Asia, it prefers a semi-arid mild-temperate to subtropical climate with cool winters and hot summers. It can be injured by temperatures below -10° to -12°C, and the dwarf variety below -5°C. It adapts well to container culture and can be grown as an ornamental or for its edible fruit.
cultivation: Pomegranates thrive in the sunniest, warmest part of the yard or orchard for best fruit production, though they will grow and flower in part shade. They do best in well-drained ordinary soil but also tolerate calcareous or acidic loam and rock-strewn gravel. Once established, they are drought-tolerant, but irrigation is needed for good fruit production. New plants should be watered every 2-4 weeks during the dry season. They tolerate moderately saline water and soil. For fertilizing, young trees are given 60-120g applications of ammonium sulfate or other nitrogen fertilizer the first two springs; thereafter, little fertilizer is needed, but they respond to an annual mulch of rotted manure or compost. For pruning, plants can be cut back to 0.5-1m high, allowing 4-5 evenly distributed shoots to develop about 30cm from the ground to form a short, well-defined trunk. Suckers and other shoots should be removed. For the first three years, branches should be judiciously shortened annually to encourage new shoots and prevent straggly development. After the third year, only suckers and dead branches are removed. Fruits are ripe when they have a distinctive color and make a metallic sound when tapped. They must be picked before over-maturity to prevent cracking. Pomegranates have a long storage life, best maintained at 1°-5°C and 80-85% relative humidity for up to 7 months, improving in juiciness and flavor during storage.
propagation: Pomegranates can be raised from seed, but the resulting plants may not come true to type. Cuttings root easily, and plants grown from them bear fruit after approximately 3 years. Cuttings, 30-50cm long, should be taken in late winter from mature, one-year-old wood. The leaves should be removed, and the cuttings treated with rooting hormone before being inserted about two-thirds of their length into soil or another warm rooting medium. Air-layering is also a viable method, but grafting is generally not successful.
pests_and_diseases: Pomegranates are relatively free of most pests and diseases. Minor issues include leaf and fruit spot, and foliar damage caused by whiteflies, thrips, mealybugs, and scale insects. Rodents seldom bother the roots, but deer may browse on the foliage.

description: The Apricot is a tree and fruit somewhat intermediate between the peach and the plum. It is a round-headed grower with dark, peach-like bark and broad, almost circular leaves. The fruit is peach-like in shape and color, with a smoother skin, rich yellow flesh, and a large, flat, smooth stone. The flesh is commonly less juicy than that of the peach, and often of higher quality. It blooms in early spring, is a perennial, and typically grows to 8-12 meters in height. It is native to Siberia to China, requires sun exposure and moderate water, and is known for its pink or white flowers, edible fruit, and ability to withstand temperatures down to -30°C, thriving in USDA Zones 3 to 9. There are three main species: Prunus Armeniaca (common apricot), Prunus Mume (Japanese apricot, grown more for flowers), and Prunus dasycarpa (purple or black apricot, little cultivated).
cultivation: Apricots are as hardy as peaches and thrive in similar localities, but demand very strong, deep, dry, loamy, or gravelly soil, being particularly intolerant of wet feet. For best results, plantations should be on elevated land near large bodies of water or with a somewhat backward exposure to retard blooming and avoid spring frosts. Clean culture is essential; for the first few years, hoed crops can be grown between rows, but after that, trees need the entire land. Tillage should stop late summer/early fall, followed by a cover-crop to mature wood and improve soil. Pruning is similar to plums, with fruit-buds on spurs and last season's growth. It is a rampant grower and profuse bearer, requiring continuous thinning of surplus shoots and shortening of new growth to maintain low branching, prevent overbearing, and avoid small fruit and alternate bearing. In coast regions, winter pruning is effective; in warmer regions, main pruning is done in summer after fruit gathering to check wood growth and promote fruit. Hand-thinning of fruit is often necessary for size and quality. In California, clean tillage is used for moisture conservation, with irrigation applied after fruit-gathering or periodically in regularly irrigated regions. Apricots can also be trained on walls, ideally not facing east or south, with an overhanging cornice for frost protection.
propagation: Apricots are propagated by budding or grafting desired varieties onto peach or plum rootstock. Peach rootstock generally provides a better union and stand, while plum stock (Myrobalan and Domestica, with preference for the latter) results in a hardier, longer-lived tree less susceptible to borers. Apricot seedlings can also be used for budding. On its own root, the apricot is less successful due to specific soil requirements. The peach root is the primary choice, defining the extent of apricot cultivation in frost-free areas. Trees are typically allowed one summer's growth in the nursery before planting.
pests_and_diseases: The most serious enemy of the apricot is the curculio, which also attacks plums and peaches, showing a particular fondness for apricots. Control methods include spraying with arsenate of lead in combination with self-boiled lime-sulfur (first application when calyces shed, second 2-3 weeks later) or jarring the trees early in the morning for 3-6 weeks after blossoms fall. The foliage is sensitive to arsenical sprays. Apricots are also susceptible to injury by scale insects (lecanium group) and a shot-hole fungus, which perforates leaves and causes pustules on fruit, making it unfit for canning or resulting in low-grade dried product. This fungus can be controlled with copper-class fungicides. Other susceptibilities include bacterial canker and blast, bacterial spot, crown gall, brown rot, Alternaria spot and fruit rot, powdery mildew, nematodes, and various viral diseases, including graft-transmissible problems.

description: The tamarind (Tamarindus indica) is a slow-growing, long-lived, evergreen tree, native to Tropical Africa and Sudan, that can reach 80-90 feet in height with a spread of 20-35 feet. It is well adapted to semiarid tropical conditions and is drought tolerant. Young trees are susceptible to frost, but mature trees can withstand brief periods of 28-30°F. It has dense, feathery, bright green pinnate foliage, with leaves that may be shed briefly in very dry areas. The inconspicuous, inch-wide, five-petalled flowers are yellow with orange or red streaks, blooming in early to late summer. The fruit consists of 3-8 inch long, brown, irregularly curved pods containing a juicy, acidulous pulp that turns brown or reddish-brown when ripe. The pulp has a sweet/sour flavor, is high in acid, sugar, vitamin B, and calcium, and contains 1 to 12 large, flat, glossy brown seeds. The pulp is widely used in chutneys, curries, meat sauces, for pickling fish, and to make cooling drinks and candies. It is also valued in medicine as a refrigerant, digestive, carminative, laxative, and antiscorbutic.
cultivation: Tamarinds should be planted in full sun, as they are highly wind-resistant and form a beautiful spreading crown. They tolerate a diversity of soil types but thrive best in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic soils. They do not tolerate cold, wet soils but are tolerant of salt spray. Young trees require adequate soil moisture until established, but mature trees are drought-tolerant and do well without supplemental irrigation; over-watering should be avoided. Young trees should be fertilized every 2-3 months with a 6-6-3 NPK fertilizer, starting with 1/4 lb and gradually increasing to 1/2 lb. Bearing trees can be fertilized 3-4 times a year with an 8-3-9 NPK fertilizer at rates of about 1/2 lb per application per year of tree age. Microelements, especially iron, may be needed in alkaline soils. Young trees are pruned to develop three to five well-spaced main scaffold branches, with only maintenance pruning thereafter to remove dead or damaged wood. Fruits mature in late spring to early summer and can be left on the tree for up to 6 months to reduce moisture content. Mature trees can produce about 350 lb of fruit annually. Ripe fruit in humid climates is susceptible to beetles and fungi, so it should be harvested and refrigerated.
propagation: Tamarinds are commonly propagated from seeds, which retain viability for several months if kept dry and germinate within a week when planted 1/2 inch deep in light, sandy loam or UC soilless potting media. Seedlings, however, are variable in quality and slow to bear fruit (6-8 years). For desirable selections, vegetative propagation methods such as veneer grafting, shield (T or inverted T) budding, gootee (stem-layering), and air layering can be used. Vegetatively propagated trees typically fruit in 3-4 years. Young trees should be planted slightly higher than ground level in holes larger than the root system, with a water basin built around them. Commercial orchards typically space trees 20 to 25 feet apart.
pests_and_diseases: In California, tamarinds are generally free of pests and diseases, though ants may spread black and olive scales. In India, the tree is attacked by a variety of pests including mealybugs, caterpillars, aphids, white flies, thrips, and various scales. Weevils and borers can also infest ripening pods or stored fruits. Specific insect pests mentioned include Caryoborus gonagra (a large gray-brown chrysomelid beetle found in seeds) and Charaxes fabius (a large black yellow-spotted butterfly whose larvae feed on leaves).

description: The Sapodilla (*Manilkara zapota*) is an evergreen, stately, slow-growing, long-lived tree, typically reaching 50-60 feet, with a dense rounded or conical crown. Its wood is hard and durable, and its bark contains a milky latex known as chicle, used as a chewing-gum base. The leaves are glossy, ovate-elliptic to elliptic-lanceolate, 2-5 inches long, and clustered at branchlet ends. Small, inconspicuous, white, tubular or bell-like flowers are produced in leaf-axils. The fruit is variable in form (round, oval, globose-depressed, or conical), 2 to 3 1/2 inches in diameter, with a thin, rusty brown, scurfy skin. The flesh is yellowish brown, translucent, soft and melting when fully ripe, sweet and delicious, but contains tannin and latex when green. It typically contains 3 to 12 hard, black, shining seeds, though some fruits can be seedless. The flavor is sweet and pleasant, often likened to pear or crunchy brown sugar. It is strong, wind-resistant, and distinctly pyramidal when young.
cultivation: Sapodillas prefer a sunny, warm, and preferably frost-free location, being highly wind tolerant and able to withstand salt spray. Mature trees can tolerate temperatures of 26° to 28° F for several hours, but young trees are more tender. They are well adapted to many soil types, thriving in very poor soils as well as deep, loose, organic soil, light clay, sand, or lateritic gravel. Good drainage is essential, as they do poorly in wet locations. The tree is highly drought resistant and tolerant of soil salinity. While mature trees tolerate dry conditions, irrigation in the dry season will increase productivity. Newly planted trees require small, frequent feedings of fertilizers containing 6-8% nitrogen, 2-4% available phosphoric acid, and 6-8% potash; starting with 1/4 pound and gradually increasing to one pound every two to three months in the first year. Thereafter, two to three applications per year are sufficient, proportionate to the tree's size. Sapodillas require very little pruning. For frost protection, overhead cover is prudent, and planting on the south side of a wall or building, or covering with sheeting, is suggested during significant frost. Harvesting is indicated when the skin is brown and the fruit separates easily from the stem without leaking latex. The fruit must be kept at room temperature to soften, washed to remove scruff, and eaten when firm-soft, not mushy. Firm-ripe fruits can be refrigerated for several days, kept for 6 weeks at 35°F, or frozen at 32°F for a month.
propagation: The sapodilla is most commonly propagated by seed, which remains viable for several years if kept dry and is easily germinated. Seedlings typically take five to eight years to bear fruit. Due to variation among seedlings in productiveness and fruit quality, asexual (vegetative) propagation is desirable to perpetuate desirable forms. Veneer grafting with seedlings as rootstock is considered the best method. Air layering and rooting of cuttings have not been successful. Seeds should be planted in shallow flats of light sandy soil, covered to a depth of 1/2 inch. Germination occurs within a month in warm weather. Young seedlings can be potted off after developing their second leaves and grown in pots for one to two years before being set out in the open ground. For budding, seedlings can be planted in nursery rows about 3 feet apart, 18 inches apart in the row. May is a favorable season for budding in south Florida; in strictly tropical regions, it can be done anytime if stock plants are actively growing. Budwood should be selected from young branches that have begun to lose their greenish color and show a brownish tinge, ensuring the eyes are well developed. Shield-budding is the method used, similar to mango, with buds cut slightly over an inch in length and wood removed if it comes out readily. The bud should be inserted and tied promptly with waxed tape to prevent latex accumulation. After three to four weeks, the stock can be headed back, and the wrap loosened to expose the eye and encourage growth.
pests_and_diseases: The sapodilla tree is remarkably free from insect pests and fungous diseases, generally remaining quite healthy with little or no care. Insects and diseases usually do not cause sufficient damage to necessitate control measures. However, the Wooly White Fly can sometimes be a problem, for which oil sprays in winter are suggested.

plant: Feijoa (Acca sellowiana, synonym Feijoa sellowiana), also known as Pineapple Guava or Guavasteen
description: The Feijoa is an evergreen shrub or small tree, 1-7 m (15-25 ft) in height, originating from the highlands of southern Brazil, parts of Colombia, Uruguay, and northern Argentina. It is a slow-growing plant with pale gray bark and spreading branches. The evergreen, thick, leathery leaves are opposite, 1 to 2-1/2 inches long, smooth soft green on top and silvery underneath. Showy, bisexual flowers, about 1 inch, appear from May through June, featuring long, bright red stamens and white petals tinged with purple, which are mildly sweet and edible. The fruit matures in autumn, ranging from 3/4 to 3-1/2 inches long, with a waxy, dull blue-green to grayish green skin. It emits a strong perfume and has a thick, white, granular, watery flesh and a translucent central pulp enclosing 20-40 small seeds. The flavor is a combination of pineapple and guava or pineapple and strawberry, often with overtones of wintergreen or spearmint. It has a gritty texture.
cultivation: Feijoas prefer cool winters and moderate summers (80°-90°F), adapted to areas where temperatures stay above 15°F. Flower production is poor with fewer than 50 hours of chilling. They should be planted away from hot, reflected sun, tolerating partial shade and slight salt spray. They grow in a wide variety of well-drained soils with a pH between 5.5 and 7.0. While drought-resistant, regular deep watering is necessary for quality harvests, especially during flowering and fruiting, with mulching to protect shallow roots. Fertilization should be light, with a complete fertilizer like 8-8-8 NPK every two months, and bone-meal or well-rotted manure annually. Pruning is not required for productivity but a light summer pruning after harvest encourages new growth and increases yields. Heavy pruning for hedges reduces flower and fruit production. Fruits ripen 4-1/2 to 7 months after flowers and are best harvested by allowing them to fall from the tree onto a tarp to prevent bruising. They can be stored in the refrigerator for about a week.
propagation: Propagation is usually by seed, though seedlings are not always true to type. Seeds are separated by fermenting the seedy pulp in water for 4 days, then strained and dried, retaining viability for over a year. Germination takes 3 weeks, and plants fruit in 3-5 years. Vegetative means are necessary for named varieties. Young wood cuttings (4 inches long) root in 1-2 months with bottom heat and mist. Layering, though tedious, is a certain method, where branches covered with soil root in six months. Whip-grafting and veneer-grafting are successful under glass using seedling feijoas as stocks.
pests_and_diseases: The feijoa is remarkably pest and disease-resistant. It is occasionally attacked by black scale (Saissetm oleae) in California and fruit flies where they are a problem. No fungus diseases have been observed on mature plants, but young plants are susceptible to damping-off fungus during germination.

description: The Sapodilla is an evergreen, stately, slow-growing, long-lived tree, indigenous to the American tropics (Yucatan, Belize, Guatemala). It can reach heights of 50 to 100 feet in the tropics, forming a dense rounded or conical crown. The wood is hard and durable, and its bark contains chicle, a milky latex used as a chewing-gum base. Leaves are ornamental, 3 to 4.5 inches long, glossy, and clustered at branchlet tips. Flowers are small, inconspicuous, and bell-like, appearing in flushes throughout the year. The fruit is round to egg-shaped, 2-4 inches in diameter, with a rusty brown, scurfy skin resembling an Irish potato. The flesh is yellowish-brown, translucent, sweet, and melting when ripe, with a flavor likened to pear or crunchy brown sugar. It typically contains 3 to 12 hard, black, shiny seeds. Mature trees can withstand temperatures of 26-28°F for several hours, but young trees are more tender. It is wind-resistant and tolerant of humid and relatively dry environments, making it suitable for container or greenhouse cultivation in cooler climates.
cultivation: Sapodillas prefer a sunny, warm, and preferably frost-free location, being highly wind tolerant and able to withstand salt spray. They adapt well to various soil types, thriving in rich sandy loam, light clay, sand, or lateritic gravel, but require good drainage. They are highly drought-resistant, though irrigation during dry seasons can increase productivity. Newly planted trees need small, frequent feedings of fertilizer (6-8% nitrogen, 2-4% available phosphoric acid, 6-8% potash) every 2-3 months in the first year, increasing amounts with tree size. Mature trees require 2-3 applications per year. Very little pruning is needed due to their compact growth. For frost protection, overhead cover or planting on the south side of a wall/building is recommended, and plants can be covered during significant frost. Trees typically bear fruit 6-8 years after planting from seed, producing heavily, often with two crops annually. Fruits are ready to pick when the skin is brown and they separate easily from the stem without leaking latex; they should be ripened at room temperature until firm-soft and can be stored in a refrigerator for several days or frozen for a month. They are primarily consumed fresh.
propagation: Sapodillas are most commonly propagated by seed, which remain viable for many years if kept dry. Seeds germinate within a month in warm weather and seedlings take 5-8 years to bear fruit. Due to variation in seedlings, vegetative propagation is desirable for perpetuating desirable traits. Veneer grafting with seedlings as rootstock is considered the best method. Budding, specifically shield-budding, has also proven successful, with May being a favorable season in south Florida. Budwood should be chosen from young, brownish branches with well-developed eyes. Air layering and rooting of cuttings have not been successful.
pests_and_diseases: The sapodilla tree is remarkably free from insect pests and fungous diseases, generally requiring little care. Insects and diseases typically do not cause sufficient damage to necessitate control measures, though the Wooly White Fly can occasionally be a problem, for which winter oil sprays are suggested.

description: Oaks are trees and shrubs belonging to the genus Quercus, native to the northern hemisphere, encompassing both deciduous and evergreen species. They are characterized by spirally arranged, often lobed leaves, catkin flowers, and a nut fruit called an acorn, which matures in 6-18 months. With over 200 known species, oaks are highly valued for their strong, durable timber used in construction and furniture, their bark for tanning and cork production, and in some cases, edible acorns. They are stately trees, often planted in parks and avenues, known for their noble habit and beautiful foliage that can display a range of colors throughout the seasons.
cultivation: Oaks grow slowly, typically not bearing acorns until they are about 20 years old. Acorns require stratification to stimulate sprouting; white oaks generally need immediate stratification, while red oak acorns can be stratified for up to two years. Oaks generally thrive in moderately moist, rich soil, including heavy clay, though specific species may prefer moister or drier, rocky/sandy conditions. Black and red oaks are usually easier to transplant than white oaks. Their lifespan typically ranges from 200 to 600 years, with some species living up to 1,000 years.
propagation: Oaks are primarily propagated by seeds (acorns) sown immediately after gathering in the fall, particularly for white oaks which sprout roots upon ripening. Red and black oak seeds, if not sown immediately, should be stratified and sown early in spring. For shipping, acorns should be packed in earth, moss, or sawdust. Varieties are commonly grafted onto potted stock in early spring or August, with Quercus Robur being a preferred stock. Evergreen species can also be propagated by layers and cuttings.
pests_and_diseases: Significant threats to oaks include Sudden Oak Death (Phytophthora ramorum), a water mould capable of killing trees within weeks, and Oak Wilt (Ceratocystis fagacearum), a lethal fungal disease, especially for red oaks. Other dangers include wood-boring beetles and root rot, which can affect older trees and may not be externally visible until the tree falls. Additionally, oak leaves serve as a food source for the larvae of various Lepidoptera species.

description: Schefflera arboricola (syn. Heptapleurum arboricolum) is a plant in the Araliaceae family. It is an evergreen shrub which can reach to 3-4 m tall. The trailing stems are weak and frequently scramble over other vegetation. The leaves are palmately compound, and have 7-9 leaflets. Leaflets are 9-20 cm long and 4-10 cm across in the wild, but normally smaller when cultivated. Flowers are produced in a 20 cm panicle of small umbels, each umbel 7-10 mm diameter with 5-10 flowers.
cultivation:
A common houseplant, popular for its ability to tolerate neglect and poor growing conditions. If given great care, it can grow up to 10 inches per year. Many cultivars exist with a variety of leaf colors and patterns, with variegation ranging from creamy-white to yellow edges or centres, and dwarf forms especially selected for. It can be useful as a bonsai as well.

The plant prefer bright light, but will grow well in lower light as well. This tropical plant prefers to be kept moist, but never let it sit in water, or the roots will rot. Soil should be moist but not wet.
propagation: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and give bottom heat. Air-layering can be done in spring. Plant seeds in spring and maintain temperature at 66-75F (19-24C).
pests_and_diseases: Scale insects, thrips, mealybugs.

description: The Raisin tree (Hovenia dulcis) is a deciduous tree, typically reaching about 30 feet in height, though it can grow to 70 feet. It has a singular trunk and a rounded head, with lower branches often dropping off naturally. It is a perennial, originating from China, and is cold-hardy to about -10° F. The tree has a moderate growth rate. Its foliage consists of large, cordate, glossy green leaves borne alternately. Small, self-fruitful, cream-colored flowers bloom in late spring, clustered in masses. The edible 'raisins' are not true fruits but swollen, knobby, translucent reddish-brown flower stalks (peduncles) that develop a pear-like flavor and fall to the ground when ripe. The actual fruit is an inedible brown seed pod.
cultivation: Raisin trees thrive in full sun, which aids flowering and ripening, and can be strategically placed for summer shade and winter sun. They tolerate a wide range of soil conditions but do best in sandy loam. While somewhat drought-tolerant, regular moisture is preferred. A light to moderate fertilization in mid-spring is likely beneficial. The tree tends to self-prune by dropping its lower branches as it grows.
propagation: Propagation is primarily from seeds, which have an impermeable coat. Methods to overcome this include scarifying the seed coat (nicking with a file or soaking in concentrated sulfuric acid for two hours, followed by thorough washing), soaking in hot tap water (140° F) for three consecutive days, or freezing. Treated seeds should be planted in potting soil, covered with clear plastic wrap, and placed in bright light, germinating within a week to over a month. Seed-grown plants typically bear fruit in 7-10 years, though 3 years is possible under optimal conditions. The plant can also be propagated by softwood cuttings taken in late summer and by root cuttings. Little work has been done on grafting.
pests_and_diseases: Raisin trees are apparently free of any significant pests and diseases. Deer may browse the foliage, but gophers do not seem to be attracted to the roots.

description: Skimmia is a genus of four species of evergreen shrubs and small trees in the Rue family, Rutaceae, native to warm temperate regions of Asia. They are ornamental woody plants grown chiefly for their bright red berries and handsome foliage. The leaves are clustered at the ends of the shoots, simple, lanceolate, 6-21 cm long and 2-5 cm broad, with a smooth margin. All parts of the plant have a pungent aroma when crushed. Flowers are small, 6-15 mm diameter, with 4-7 petals, in dense panicle clusters. The fruit is a fleshy drupe, red to black, 6-12 mm diameter, containing a single seed. They are densely branched, usually low shrubs with medium-sized, generally oblong acute leaves, small white flowers in terminal panicles, and showy bright red, rarely black, berry-like fruit. They are tender, not reliably hardy as far north as Washington, D.C., though S. Fortunei is somewhat hardier than S. japonica. They are handsome shrubs for borders of evergreen shrubberies and are particularly valuable for planting in cities as they are smoke-enduring. Their bright red fruits are retained through the whole winter if not eaten by birds.
cultivation: Skimmias are of rather slow growth and thrive best in a sandy loamy soil, but also grow well in strong clay. They prefer a partly shaded situation and are grown in shade, with moist, well-drained, humus-rich soils. They are tolerant of both drought and air pollution. For well-fruited specimens, it is necessary to plant staminate (male) plants among the pistillate (female) ones, as they are polygamous and mostly unisexual. They are sometimes cultivated in pots in a sandy compost of peat and loam.
propagation: Propagation is by seeds sown in fall or stratified, and by cuttings under glass with gentle bottom heat. Seeds sown in the fall and grown along in a coolhouse during winter can be planted out in a good loam the following spring for vigorous growth, and can be lifted the following October.
pests_and_diseases: Skimmias are fed on by aphids, Horse Chestnut Scale, Garden Leafhopper, and Southern Red Mite.

description: The common bean, also known as Green bean, Snap bean, String bean, or Dry bean, is widely cultivated. It produces fleshy, tender pods that can be eaten fresh, or varieties can be allowed to ripen for fresh or dried beans. Pods come in green, yellow (wax beans), or purple, with purple pods turning green when cooked. Plants are either smaller, self-supporting erect bush types that produce sooner, or taller pole types (vines) that yield more over the season and require support. Generally, beans are herbaceous plants of the Leguminosae family, primarily from the Phaseolus tribe, characterized by epigeal germination where cotyledons appear above ground. Garden beans, a category that includes common beans, are noted for their thick, meaty pods that snap cleanly without a string.
cultivation: Common beans require regular watering; occasional deep watering is more beneficial than frequent light watering to prevent mildew. Fertilize plants when actively growing and again when pods begin to form. Pods typically develop 50-70 days after planting, and harvesting should occur every 5-7 days. Production ceases once pods are allowed to mature.
