Beans are grown as 'field beans' for dry-shelled seeds (often bush varieties) or 'garden beans' for succulent pods and immature seeds (both bush and pole varieties). They can be forced under glass at tomato temperatures, with bush varieties often preferred. Field beans thrive in warm, fairly good farm soil, similar to that used for corn or potatoes, and do not significantly deplete soil fertility due to nitrogen fixation. Strong loams or limestone soils are ideal, with sandy/gravelly loams also suitable, provided there is fair humus. Fertilization can include old stable manure, muriate of potash, acid phosphate, or a commercial fertilizer (2-3% nitrogen, 8% phosphoric acid, 3-4% potash). In northern states, planting typically occurs June 1-25, after early plowing and harrowing to preserve moisture and control weeds. Use good, hand-picked seed, not over one year old, and free from weevils and disease. Rows are generally 28-36 inches apart. For field beans, harvesting is done when pods ripen and leaves drop, either by a bean-puller or by hand, followed by curing in small heaps before threshing. Garden beans prefer warm, fertile soil, and prompt, clean picking of pods prolongs bearing. Pole beans specifically need fertile, warm soil; poles should be set 4 feet apart, with 4-5 beans planted 1-1½ inches deep around each. Frequent cultivation and hoeing, along with a top-dressing of fertilizer or manure, are necessary to maintain productivity.
propagation: Common beans are propagated from seeds. Plant seeds when the soil is warm, or start them indoors for an early advantage and transplant when soil temperatures are suitable. Seeds should be planted an inch deep in full sun and good, loosened soil to facilitate emergence of the heavy seeds. Bush types can be planted 1-3 inches apart in rows spaced 2-3 feet apart. Pole type planting varies with the support system; for instance, a wigwam of 3 or 4 eight-foot poles can support 6-8 beans, later thinned to 4 per pole. Single poles can be spaced 3 or 4 feet apart, or closer at 1-2 feet, with seeds planted in rows every 1-3 inches. Vines can also be sown along a sunny wall, fence, or trellis and trained with light strings supported by wires or heavy twine. Water the ground thoroughly before planting, and do not water again until seedlings emerge. For field beans, use good, hand-picked seeds, not over one year old, and free from weevils and disease. For pole beans, plant 4-5 seeds around each pole, 1 to 1½ inches deep, ideally with the seed eye downward.
pests_and_diseases: Common bean pests include Mexican bean beetles, aphids, cucumber beetles, and whiteflies. Mildew is a frequent disease. A significant pest is the bean-weevil, which attacks seeds both in the pod and when dry. Control for weevil-infested seeds involves carbon-bisulfid treatment in the fall: place beans in a tight receptacle, pour half a pint of bisulfid into a saucer on top of the beans, and seal for 24 hours or more. Bean blight is a difficult-to-control disease affecting field, garden, and lima beans; prevention includes avoiding seed from affected fields and not replanting beans in affected fields for several years. Bean anthracnose (pod-spot), characterized by dark or brownish spots on pods, is prevented by planting clean seed and carefully rejecting any pods showing disease signs. True bean rust is less common and less serious. Red spider mites can be a problem when forcing beans under glass.

description: Peonies are native to Asia, southern Europe, and western North America. They are specially attractive and important flower-garden perennials, prized for their showy spring and early summer bloom. Most are herbaceous perennial plants, typically 0.5–1.5 meters tall, but some resemble trees, growing up to 1.5–3 meters tall. They have large, alternate, pinnately compound or dissected leaves, mostly ternate, with deeply lobed segments. The flowers are large, often fragrant, terminal, and mostly solitary, though sometimes several, ranging from red to white or yellow. They have 5 persistent sepals and 5-10 conspicuous, broad petals, with numerous stamens. The carpels are 2-5 on a fleshy disk, becoming dehiscent follicles bearing an indurated style, and large, fleshy seeds. Peonies are known for their hardiness, permanence in the garden, large size, wide range of color and form, and fragrance. The blooming season for herbaceous peonies typically runs from mid-May to mid-July. They are very hardy, tolerating severe cold as far north as Duluth without ground covering, but in southern states, an extended growing season may result in less fine blooms. Single-flowering varieties are less popular than double ones, as they do not keep as long when cut and fade more rapidly on the plant.
cultivation: Peonies thrive in deep, rich, rather moist loam, though they can grow in all soil types. A well-drained clay subsoil is beneficial for bloom production, while lighter soil encourages tuber ramification for propagation. Prepare the bed by trenching the soil thoroughly 2 or more feet deep, incorporating a generous quantity of well-rotted cow-manure, as peonies are gross feeders. The ground should be kept well tilled, and an annual top-dressing applied in November, then forked into the earth the following spring. Peonies require a liberal supply of water at all times, especially during bloom. Liquid manure applied during the growing season when the ground is dry can enhance plant growth and bloom size. Plant the eyes 2 inches below the surface. When transplanting, it's advisable to remove all old earth to ensure fresh, unimpoverished soil around the roots. While small divided plants may produce imperfect flowers initially, thoroughly established plants can bloom for over twenty years if undisturbed. Inconspicuous wire supports are recommended during blooming to prevent heavy rains from beating down the flowers. Peonies do well in partial shade, which can prolong and intensify bloom color. Herbaceous peonies grow 1 to 4 feet high, making them suitable for planting in front of shrubbery, along driveways, or as part of a distant vista. Their richly colored shoots in early spring offer a striking effect. When planted in a border with fall-blooming perennials like phlox and funkia, their glossy foliage remains effective. Old flowers should be cut off to prevent unnecessary seed follicle formation and plant exhaustion. Faded foliage should be removed in November to avoid interfering with the next year's shoots. Peony buds can be shipped long distances without water and arrive in good condition, making them popular for cut flowers. When cut in tight buds and wrapped in paraffin paper, some varieties can be cold-stored for over a month and open satisfactorily. For forcing, lift plants in September and place them in a coldframe. Under glass, maintain a uniform temperature of 55° to 60°F. With liquid manure, strong blooms can be produced in eight weeks, but plants require a two-year rest before being forced again. To achieve extra-fine blooms on double-flowering varieties, remove lateral buds as soon as they form. Retaining the first lateral bud instead of the terminal one can secure a later blooming period.
propagation: Propagation methods vary by peony type. Tree peonies are propagated by grafting, while herbaceous and Itoh peonies are propagated by root division. New peonies are also raised from seed. The easiest and most satisfactory method for herbaceous peonies is by division of their large, thick roots. Roots can be lifted and divided from mid-August until stalks reappear in spring, with early autumn being the best time for cut surfaces to callus and new rootlets to form before frost. A large stool should be chosen, its leaves cut off, and then separated into as many divisions as possible, ensuring each tuber has an 'eye'. Care must be taken to dig up all tubers, as any left behind may remain dormant for a season before producing a shoot. Tubers divided without an eye should also be planted, as they can similarly produce growth within two years. Peonies, like most tuberous plants, tolerate considerable exposure when dormant and can be safely shipped long distances. Grafting is used for herbaceous peonies to rapidly increase new and rare varieties. An eye of the desired variety is inserted into the tuber of a strong-growing variety (from which all previous eyes have been removed), typically in August. Grafted plants are then placed in frames for the winter and transplanted into nursery rows the following year. Propagating by seed is a more tedious process, primarily used for increasing distinct species and obtaining new varieties. Seeds should be gathered as soon as they are ripe, kept damp, and sown in November. A mulch during the first season helps keep the ground moist and prevents weeds. Typically, two years are required for seeds to germinate, and three more before a well-developed bloom can be expected.
pests_and_diseases: The most prevalent and destructive disease affecting peonies is botrytis blight, which attacks stems, buds, and leaves. Early in spring, young stems are attacked at the ground surface, turning black, wilting, and falling over; this issue can sometimes appear as late as the following season. The use of green manure seems to favor this disease, so only well-rotted manure or mineral fertilizers should be used. Later, young flower-buds are attacked, turning black and drying up, a condition known as 'bud-blast'. If buds are not attacked until they are well developed, they turn brown and fail to open, with petals becoming a dark brown rotten mass, referred to as 'bud-rot'. In very wet seasons, 80 to 90 percent of buds may be affected. Even flowers can be discolored by spots caused by this fungus. Leaves are usually the last to be attacked, showing large, irregular spots that become brown and dry. While specific control methods against botrytis blight and other peony diseases have not been fully devised, sanitary measures are considered the most practicable. Prompt and thorough removal of wilted stems and rotted buds, along with the complete destruction by fire of all leaves and stems in the fall, will help reduce the extent of diseases in the subsequent year.

description:
Celery (Apium graveolens) is a major garden vegetable, grown for its blanched leafstalks which are eaten raw and used in cookery. It is a biennial, sometimes annual, herbaceous plant with leaf-stalks 6-15 inches long, bearing 3 pairs and a terminal leaflet, coarsely serrated and ternately lobed or divided. Flower-stalks are 2-3 feet high, branched and leafy, with small, inconspicuous white flowers borne in compound umbels. Seeds are very small, flattened on the sides, broader than long. Wild plants have an acrid, pungent flavor. The genus Apium comprises 15-20 species of annual or perennial glabrous herbs. A. graveolens is the important horticultural species, with var. rapaceum being celeriac, a form with a thickened, turnip-like crown. Wild celery grows 1-2 feet high in marshy places near the sea in Europe, Africa, and Asia, and has run wild in parts of North America. Some individuals are allergic to celery.
Celery is grown in California, primarily in Orange County and along the Sacramento and San Joaquin Rivers. The Golden Self-blanching variety is most popular and profitable. Celery plants require a well-controlled irrigation system and good drainage, as they cannot tolerate drought at any stage of growth or standing water, and over-irrigation can lead to water-logging the soil and driving roots near the surface.
Celery can be classified into two general groups: green varieties and self-blanching varieties. Self-blanching varieties are more easily blanched and offer cultural advantages for commercial growers, especially for summer crops and late markets, allowing more plants per acre when using boards for blanching. However, light-colored varieties are generally considered inferior in quality to green sorts. Home gardeners often plant light-colored kinds for summer and green varieties for fall and winter. Notable varieties include White Plume, Golden Self-blanching (most popular, 14-20 inches tall, compact, stocky, easily blanched to creamy white), Rose-ribbed Golden Self-blanching, Giant Pascal (old green-stem, unsurpassed quality, 30 inches or more), Winter Queen, French Success, and Boston Market.
cultivation:
Celery thrives in muck soils (60% or more organic matter, loose, friable, with a water-table around 3 feet) but can be grown successfully on any friable soil with adequate moisture, plant-food, and vegetable matter. New muck lands require clearing, drainage, liming, and initial crops like corn. Other soils benefit from fall plowing with liberal manure. Soil preparation involves smoothing harrows and plank drags to conserve moisture.
Fertilizing: Stable manure is considered the best fertilizer, providing both organic matter and plant-food, applied near the surface (rotten manure after plowing, coarse fresh manure before). Market gardeners apply 30-40 tons/acre, or 10 tons on muck. Commercial fertilizers are also essential, with a high-grade formula (e.g., 4% nitrogen, 8% phosphoric acid, 10% potash) applied at 2 tons/acre. Side-dressings of nitrate of soda or complete fertilizer (100-200 lbs/acre) every three weeks are beneficial. Commercial fertilizers are heavily used in the South due to the scarcity and high cost of stable manure.
Planting in the field: Early crop plants are set out around May 10th in northern latitudes to avoid frost. Late crop seedlings are transplanted after June 20th, or early July, depending on the variety. Plants 3-5 inches high are preferred. Rows should be straight, using a marker. Planting distances vary: 'new celery-culture' uses close spacing (7-8 inches each way, or rows 1 foot apart with plants 4 inches in row) for self-blanching, suitable for greenhouses/coldframes with sub-irrigation. More common is 18 inches to 2 feet between rows, with plants 4-5 inches apart (for Golden Self-blanching with boards). For blanching with soil, 4-5 feet between rows is common. Green types are spaced 5-6 inches apart in the row. Transplanting should be done rapidly, with plant-beds watered 24 hours prior. In California, plants are transplanted 6 inches apart in furrows 3½ feet apart, at an average depth of 3 to 5 inches. After transplanting, the field is 'crowded' by moving earth away from young plants, followed by rolling to flatten ridges and pack the soil. When plants are 12-15 inches tall, 'splitting' is performed by drawing earth up to them to encourage tall, straight growth; this operation is repeated twice.
Tillage: Frequent, shallow tillage is necessary. Spike-tooth cultivators are used for horse tillage. Hand-weeding is required between plants. Mulching with 3-4 inches of aerated horse-manure conserves moisture, reduces labor, and provides nutrients. Irrigation (overhead systems in the North, sub-irrigation in the South) is crucial for vigorous growth and quality, preferably done in the evening or at night with thorough applications. Seedlings require heavy watering initially, which is then reduced, and the land must be well-drained.
Blanching: American markets demand creamy white stalks, achieved by growing plants in the dark to prevent chlorophyll development. Green varieties are blanched with earth, starting in early September, gradually ridging soil to the full height of the stems. Early crops are blanched with boards (10-12 inches wide, 14-16 feet long, secured with wire hooks) for 10 days to two weeks. Paper and tile are also used. In the South, blanching is entirely by boarding-up, using cypress boards with double rows planted 8-10 inches apart, and plants 6-8 inches apart in the row. In California, blanching is achieved by banking the celery with earth as it reaches maturity, with soil drawn higher with each banking.
Harvesting and marketing: Coldframe crops are harvested in July (sometimes late June). Late crops are ready in August. In California, harvest occurs from October through March, with the bulk in November, December, and January. Plants are lifted with forks or cut with a knife, then prepared for market by cutting just below the crown. Roots may be untrimmed and tied in bunches of a dozen, packed in standard crates (e.g., 24x24 inch base). Some regions trim roots into tapering cubes. Twine or colored tape is used for bunching. Michigan growers use small crates of thin lumber.
Storage: A large percentage of the late crop is placed in city cold-storage (roots on, minimal trimming). In the North, celery is commonly stored in trenches (10-14 inches wide, deep enough for tops to protrude 2-3 inches), covered with trough-shaped boards, and later with earth or manure for insulation. Coldframes, well-ventilated house cellars, and specialized cement/brick structures or pits are also used for storage.
Southern cultivation (Florida): Sowing occurs in July-September during warm, rainy weather. Seed-beds (18-36 inches wide) are covered with heavy burlap after sowing to conserve moisture, cool soil, and protect from rain. After germination, cheese-cloth screens (flat or roof-like) are used for shade and air circulation. Plants are watered through the cheese-cloth until strong enough for direct sunlight.
propagation:
Celery is propagated from seed. Great care is needed to procure high-quality seed from reliable dealers to avoid pithy stalks, poor stands, low vitality, or seed shoots. Some growers import seed or buy large quantities to test its merit over several years, storing it in sealed jars for up to six years. Celery seed is very small (approx. 70,000 seeds per ounce), with about 10,000 plants expected per ounce due to germination rates and seedling fragility. Seeds are slow to germinate and should be planted in fine, constantly moist (but not wet) soil.
For the early crop, seeds are sown in artificially heated frames or greenhouses, typically not before March 1st, in drills 2 inches apart and covered with no more than ⅛ inch of earth. A mixture of muck, sifted coal-ashes, sand, and bone-meal is recommended for starting plants under glass. Beds are covered with burlap until germination, then require plenty of light, sunshine, and fresh air. Germination is favored at 70-75°F, with 10° lower maintained after plants emerge. Seedlings are transplanted when rough leaves appear into beds or flats, spaced 1.5-2 inches apart. Flats can be 2 inches deep, half-filled with rotten manure and topped with rich soil to promote strong root development. Young plants are sensitive to low temperatures and require careful nursing. Spraying with bordeaux mixture can prevent fungous diseases.
For the late crop, seeds are sown in open ground or protected beds as early in spring as the soil can be prepared. Moisture control is important, often achieved with overhead irrigation or moist burlap covers. Beds may be shaded with brush or lath screens. Rows can be a foot or more apart, and thinning may be necessary to produce stocky plants. Seedlings are typically transplanted directly to the permanent bed or field once they reach about 3 inches in height. If plants are 5 inches or taller before transplanting, their tops should be cut back.
In the South (Florida), sowing is done in July, August, and September. Seed-beds are chosen near the celery field, 18-36 inches wide, with rows sown across. Burlap is placed over the beds immediately after sowing to maintain moisture, cool the soil, and protect seeds from heavy rains. After germination, a cheese-cloth screen (flat or roof-like, 8-12 inches above the bed) is used for shade and air circulation. Plants are watered through the cheese-cloth until they are 2-3 inches high and well-greened, at which point they are strong enough for direct sunlight. In California, celery is propagated by sowing seeds in the open ground, and then transplanting the seedlings to the field.
pests_and_diseases: Loss from fungous diseases can be avoided by spraying seedlings several times with bordeaux mixture. Heart-rot and other diseases are likely to occur in hot, moist weather when plants are spaced too closely, leading to poor air circulation. The most important disease in California is late blight (Septoria petroselini var. apii), which is successfully handled by growers with bordeaux mixture. Celery does not have any serious insect enemies. Other significant diseases include blights (Cercospora apii and Septoria petroselini var. apii), leaf-spot (Phyllosticta apii), and rust (Piccinia bullata). The application of bordeaux mixture in the seedbed and frequent applications in the field help control these diseases and prevent losses in storage. Earlier applications of bordeaux mixture are considered most effective, and crop rotation is also desirable for disease prevention.

description: Passiflora edulis is a vine species of passion flower that is native to Paraguay, Brazil and northeastern Argentina. It is cultivated commercially in frost-free areas for its fruit and is widely grown. The passion fruit is round to oval, either yellow or dark purple at maturity, with a soft to firm, juicy interior filled with numerous seeds. The fruit can be grown to eat or for its juice, which is often added to other fruit juices to enhance the aroma. For eating right out of the fruit, the fruit should be allowed to wrinkle for a few days to raise the sugar levels and enhance the flavor. The bright yellow variety, also known as the Golden Passion Fruit, can grow up to the size of a grapefruit, has a smooth, glossy rind, and has been used as a rootstock for the Purple Passion Fruit. The dark purple passion fruit is smaller than a lemon, less acidic, and has a richer aroma and flavor. The purple passion fruit is subtropical and prefers a frost-free climate, with some cultivars tolerating temperatures into the upper 20's (°F). The yellow passion fruit is tropical or near-tropical and more intolerant of frost. Both forms need wind protection and at least 35 inches of annual rainfall. They are vigorous, climbing vines that cling by tendrils, growing 15 to 20 ft. per year, and are generally short-lived (5 to 7 years). The evergreen leaves are alternate, deeply 3-lobed, finely toothed, 3 to 8 inches long, deep green and glossy above, paler and dull beneath, often tinged with red or purple. Single, fragrant flowers, 2 to 3 inches wide, are born at each node on new growth, consisting of greenish-white sepals, white petals, and a fringelike corona of white-tipped, purple-based rays. Purple passion fruit is self-fruitful, while yellow form flowers are perfect but self-sterile, requiring cross-pollination, often by carpenter bees or hand pollination. The nearly round or ovoid fruit, 1-1/2 to 3 inches wide, has a tough, smooth, waxy rind ranging from dark purple to light yellow. Inside is an aromatic mass of sacs containing orange-colored, pulpy juice and up to 250 small, hard seeds. The flavor is appealing, musky, guava-like, and sweet/tart to tart. The yellow form generally has larger fruit, but the purple pulp is less acidic, richer in aroma and flavor, and has a higher juice proportion (35-38%). Hybrids exist with intermediate characteristics. The vine, especially the yellow form, is fast-growing and bears fruit in 1 to 3 years, with ripening occurring 70 to 80 days after pollination.
cultivation: Location: Plant passion fruit vines in full sun, or partial shade in very hot areas. They are rampant, so plant next to a chain link fence or strong trellis, or train into an arbor. Soil: They grow on many soil types, but light to heavy sandy loams with a pH of 6.5 to 7.5 are most suitable. Excellent drainage is necessary. Soil should be rich in organic matter and low in salts. If too acidic, apply lime. Due to shallow roots, a thick layer of organic mulch is beneficial. Irrigation: Regular watering keeps vines flowering and fruiting continuously. Water requirement is high when fruits approach maturity; dry soil can cause fruits to shrivel and fall prematurely. Fertilization: Vigorous growers require regular fertilizing. A 10-5-20 NPK applied at 3 pounds per plant 4 times a year is a good choice. Too much nitrogen promotes foliage at the expense of flowering. Watch for deficiencies, especially in potassium and calcium, and magnesium. Frost-damaged plants should be generously fertilized after warming. Pruning: Pruning is necessary to control growth, ease harvest, and maintain productivity. In warm winter climates, prune immediately after harvest. In cool winter areas, prune in early spring. Remove weak growth and cut back vigorous growth by at least one third. In very hot climates, allow a thick canopy to protect fruit from sunburn. Frost Protection: Due to their mass, vines are hard to cover, but leaf layers protect inner branches. Plants usually resprout even if frozen to the ground. Grow vines against a wall, deck, or patio for best protection. Overhead protection also helps. Harvest: Fruit turns from green to deep purple (or yellow) when ripe and falls within days. Pick when color changes or gather from the ground daily. Store washed and dried fruits gently in bags; they last 2 to 3 weeks at 50°F. Fruit is sweetest when slightly shriveled. Both fruit and juice freeze well. The flavor blends well with citrus and other fruits.
propagation: Passion fruit vines are usually grown from seeds. For the yellow form, seedling variation provides cross-pollination and helps overcome self-sterility. Seeds planted soon after removal from the fruit will germinate in 10 to 20 days. Cleaned and stored seeds have a lower and slower germination rate. Seeds should be planted 1/2 to 1 inch deep in beds, and seedlings may be transplanted when 10 inches high. If taller (up to 3 feet), the tops should be cut back and the plants heavily watered. Plants can also be propagated by layers or cuttings of matured wood with 3 to 4 nodes. Rooting may be hastened by hormone treatment. Cuttings should be well rooted and ready for setting out in 90 days. Grafting is an important means of perpetuating hybrids and reducing nematode damage and diseases by utilizing the resistant yellow passion fruit rootstock. Scions of healthy young plants are grafted to seedlings, ensuring the scion's diameter matches the rootstock's. Either a cleft graft, whip graft, or side-wedge graft may be made.
pests_and_diseases: In tropical areas, passion fruit vines are attacked by a host of pests and diseases. The purple passion fruit is particularly susceptible to nematodes, while the yellow passion fruit is more nematode resistant. In California, problems are less severe, though plants can be afflicted with nematodes and viruses, as well as Fusarium and other diseases that thrive in cool soils. Nematodes are partially responsible for the short life of many passion fruit vines. Snails can also be a serious problem in California, often completely stripping a vine of leaves and bark, killing young plants or predisposing them to disease.

description: Roses are ornamental, perennial flowering shrubs or vines of the genus Rosa, family Rosaceae, with over 100 species. They are primarily grown for their beautiful, often fragrant flowers, and also for ornamental fruits (hips) and attractive foliage. They can be deciduous or evergreen, upright, climbing, or creeping, typically armed with sharp prickles. Leaves are alternate, pinnately compound, with 3-13 sharply toothed leaflets. Flowers are solitary or clustered, usually with five petals (rarely four), ranging in color from white, yellow, pink, red, purple, crimson, apricot, copper, orange, scarlet, lavender, gray, to brown. Many varieties are repeat bloomers. The flowers are followed by fleshy, berry-like rose hips, typically red, which can persist through winter and are rich in vitamin C. Roses are widespread in colder and temperate regions of the northern hemisphere, with most native to Asia. They range from tiny miniatures to climbers reaching 20 meters. Garden roses are diverse, including summer-flowering, climbing, and perpetual-flowering types, while wild single roses are valued for landscape planting due to their foliage, stem color, fruit, and general habit. Some varieties, like Rosa rugosa, are noted for dark green, leathery, glossy leaves and are useful for hedges. Hybrid Perpetuals have a limited flowering season, while Dwarf ramblers are hardy and continuous bloomers. Grafted roses offer stronger root-action and nematode immunity, while some varieties perform better on their own roots.
cultivation:
Roses are generally of easy cultivation and can grow in almost any soil type, except loose and very sandy ones, provided there is thorough drainage. They thrive best in ample space, good soil, and with liberal fertilizing, preferring a sunny position, ideally a gentle northerly slope, away from tall buildings and overhanging trees. The ideal soil is a rich, deep loam, but good rose beds can be made in clay, sand, or gravel, with soil composition varying by rose class (e.g., Hybrid Remontants prefer heavy clay, Tea roses prefer lighter, warmer ground). Roses are rank feeders and require liberal manuring.

Ground preparation is crucial, involving digging beds 2-3 feet deep, ensuring thorough drainage with a 6-inch layer of broken stone, bricks, or cinders at the bottom, connected to a drain-tile. The bed is then filled with a foot of well-rotted cow-manure and heavy clay loam, with sand and ground limestone added if needed to neutralize acidity. Beds can be any shape, but 4 feet wide is satisfactory for double rows planted 2.5 feet apart. When planting in a lawn, the subsoil should be loosened and mixed with well-decomposed stable manure, and the bed filled to surface level, settling to about 1 inch below the sod to retain rainfall.

Roses are readily transplanted and can be planted either on their own roots or budded on stock like Manetti. Dormant budded plants are often shipped for autumn planting before ground freeze or in early spring after being safely housed through winter. For budded roses, holes should be 1 foot deep and 15 inches wide, with the bud collar placed 2 inches beneath the surface to prevent suckers from the rootstock. Roots should be spread downwards, covered with fine soil (avoiding manure lumps in contact with roots), packed firmly, and then covered with 3 inches of coarse litter and manure. Pot-grown roses are planted in spring after frost danger, slightly deeper than they stood in the pot, and require staking. Tender Tea roses may need winter protection in cold frames or thorough winter mulch. Climbing roses should be planted 8 feet apart and trained on trellises, requiring manual training and tying to supports.

Pruning aims to secure strong, clean, flower-bearing canes and varies significantly by rose type and winter severity. Dead wood, weak, damaged, or diseased growth should always be removed, with cuts made at a 45-degree angle above a vegetative bud. Wild roses require minimal pruning, mainly thinning out weak and old wood, and long, vigorous shoots in climbing varieties should not be shortened as they are the most floriferous. Hybrid Perpetuals are pruned in late March, leaving 4-5 canes 3 feet high for mass effect or cutting healthy canes to 6-8 inches for quality. In autumn, they are cut back to 2 feet to prevent wind damage. Dwarf Tea roses require less vigorous pruning, while Bourbons need even less. Modern hybrids require deadheading to encourage new growth and flowers, and a 'hard' annual pruning (8-12 inches) in early spring in cold climates.

Regular cultivation and mulching (preferably with rotted manure) are vital, especially in hot summer weather. In spring, rough manure should be removed, and fine particles forked in (3 inches deep), then the surface raked smooth and stirred frequently until buds develop. Established plants benefit from light, frequent feeding with liquid manure or top-mulching with well-rotted cow or horse manure. Watering should be thorough but not excessive, copiously when plants are coming into bearing, less when cutting, and sparingly after cutting until new growth starts. In mid-continental regions, intense heat, low humidity, and temperature fluctuations necessitate strict horticultural practices. In California, roses require recurring periods of absolute rest, often achieved by withholding water semi-annually. Tea and Perpetual roses should be lifted and reset every five years.

Greenhouse cultivation involves specific house types with attention to ventilation, light, and heating. Normal temperatures are 60°F at night, 70°F on bright days, and 65°F on dark days. Ventilation is crucial, given freely on sunny days, guarding against drafts to prevent mildew. Humidity is important, with gravel or ash walks helping to maintain it. Flowers are cut depending on the variety, leaving two good eyes (or one for strong growth) when cutting. Pinching flowering shoots can control crop maturity. Cut flowers should be hardened in water at 38-42°F before grading.
propagation:
Roses are propagated by seeds, cuttings, grafting or budding, layering, and division.

**Seeds:** True species can be propagated by seeds, ideally from isolated plants to prevent hybridization. Hips should be gathered when ripe (early gathering is suggested), seeds washed out and sown immediately, or stratified (with moist sand or fermented) and sown in spring. They typically germinate in the first year, but if dried in hips over winter, they may not germinate until the second year. Seedlings are usually transplanted into nursery rows at one year old and are ready for permanent planting at two years. Natural propagation also occurs via seed dispersal by fruit-eating birds. Seeds can produce unknown results until the plant blooms.

**Cuttings:** Most species grow readily from cuttings. Hardwood cuttings, typically 6-inch lengths of ripened wood, can be taken in fall (October to March, December being ideal), stored over winter, and planted in spring with one eye above ground. For greenhouse propagation, strong non-flowering growths (blind-wood cuttings) or flowering stems are used, 2-3 inches long, with a clean cut below the eye, lower leaf removed, and top leaves trimmed. These are rooted in propagating beds with ample drainage (sand or screened coal-ashes) at 58-60°F in the sand and 54-60°F overhead, rooting in about four weeks. Once roots start, cuttings are carefully potted into small pots, watered, and shaded. Root cuttings may be possible for suckering roses (e.g., Cinnamomeae, Carolinae, Gallicae groups), taken in fall, stored in sphagnum or sand over winter, and sown 2 inches deep in spring.

**Grafting and Budding:** Many garden roses are grown on their own roots or budded/grafted onto stocks like R. Manetti, R. canina, R. multiflora, Maiden's Blush, Dog rose, or Banksia rootstock. Budding is done in the open air in July or August using a dormant shield-bud, and also under glass. Grafting, particularly root-grafting, is an easy method under glass, using whip- or splice-grafts with root sections as stock, or veneer-grafts. Commercial florists often use Manetti stock for forcing roses. Manetti stock is grown from hardwood cuttings, then disbudded or suckered below the graft point. Deep planting of Manetti is advisable for better stem union. Grafting involves making diagonal cuts on both stock and scion, ensuring a perfect union of the cambium layer, and tying with raffia. Grafted plants are then placed in a grafting-case at controlled temperatures (76-78°F initially, then 70-72°F) with careful ventilation and shade until hardened. Budding is preferred for less vigorous varieties, as some are worthless on their own roots. Standard or 'tree' roses are top-budded onto strong stocks.

**Layering:** Layering is less common but used for species difficult to root from cuttings (e.g., R. foetida, R. hemisphaerica). It is done in early spring using last year's wood, abrading the bark of the buried portion.

**Division:** Division is an easy method for suckering varieties (e.g., Rosa virginiana, R. nitida, R. palustris, R. spinosissima, Crimson Moss). Plants are grown thickly for 3-4 years, then lifted and torn apart.
pests_and_diseases:
Roses are susceptible to a variety of pests and diseases.

**Pests:**
Common insect pests include aphids (greenfly, Macrosiphum rosae, Myzus rosarum), which suck sap and weaken the plant; ladybirds are natural predators and should be encouraged. Other pests include red-spider mites (controlled by syringing with water under pressure), rose-midge (Neocerata rhodophaga, controlled by buying plants from unaffected areas, house cleanliness, removing infested parts, fumigation, or picking off buds), nematodes (eel-worms, controlled by using Manetti stock, lowland soil, or steam sterilization), thrips (controlled by nicotine preparations, tobacco extracts, or sweetened poison), rose slugs (Endelomyia rousae, Cladius pectinicornis, Emphytus cinctus, controlled by arsenate of lead spray, Hellebore, or nicotine solution), rose leaf-hoppers (Typhlocyba rosae, controlled by nicotine solution spray), rose leaf-rollers (Archips rosaceana, controlled by arsenate of lead spray early in the season or destroying caterpillars), rose chafers (Macrodactylus subspinosus, controlled by sweetened arsenate of lead or mosquito-netting), rose scale (Aulacaspis rosae, controlled by dormant lime-sulfur spray or cutting infested stems), rose curculio (Rhynchiles bicolor, controlled by hand-picking, arsenate of lead, or removing wild roses), rose slug-caterpillars (Euclea indetermina, controlled by hand-picking or arsenate of lead spray), Fuller's rose-beetle (Aramigus fulleri, controlled by persistent hand-picking), and mealy bugs (controlled by tobacco extract or a stiff water stream). Climbing roses on porches are more susceptible to insect attacks like aphides and slugs, though Microphyllae and Crimson Rambler are slug-resistant.

**Diseases:**
Fungal diseases are common and often best managed with a preventative fungicidal spray program. These include powdery mildew (Sphaerotheca pannosa, controlled by dusting with sulfur, potassium sulfide spray, ammoniacal copper carbonate, vaporized sulfur, and eliminating drafts), black spot (Diplocarpon rosae/Actinonema rosae, controlled by gathering and burning fallen leaves, ammoniacal copper carbonate spray, increased temperature with fertilizer, and pruning), rose rust (Phragmidium spp., controlled by collecting and burning infected parts or potassium sulfide spray), and stem canker (Coniothyrium wernsdorfiae/fuckelii, controlled by cutting and burning diseased canes). Downy mildew (Peronospora sparsa) is a somewhat rare greenhouse disease characterized by wilting and rapid killing of young leaves, treatable with sulfur dusting. Rose-galls (Bacterium tumefaciens), including Crown-Gall, require removal of affected parts, avoiding the use of the same knife, cutting off and burning branches, and removing or sterilizing soil by steam. La France roses are severely affected by 'die back' (anthracnose), for which no cure has been found. Overlapping leaves in propagating beds can invite fungus. A major concern with budded plants is suckering from the rootstock, which must be removed to prevent it from choking the desired rose wood. Strong and vigorous growths resulting from close pruning are better able to resist insects or disease.

description: The acerola is typically found in dry, thorn-woodlands as a deciduous tree. It grows in San Diego County, coastal Southern California and in more extreme areas with protection. There are trees in Riverside, Calif. and San Bernardino County. In general, acerola has poor cold tolerance, with young plants typically killed at temperatures below 30° F. Trees can survive brief exposure to 28° F with loss of leaves. Trees are sensitive to wind (shallow root systems). The acerola is drought tolerant, and will adopt a deciduous habit; irrigation results in leaf and flower flush. Plants can easily adapt to pot culture in well-draining, limed soil. Large, relatively fast growing bushy shrub or small tree (to 15 feet). Can be pruned to any desired shape, but grows best as a managed shrub. Multiple or single trunks which can be trained. Occasionally, bushes appear to be composed of canes. Branches are brittle, and easily broken. Leaves may be irritating to some people. The root system is shallow, and trees can be toppled by wind, but they can be uprighted and recover over time. Acerola leaves are dark to light green, glossy when mature, obviate to lanceolate, with minute hairs which can be irritating. Foliage will drop during water stress, but recovers well with flush and flowering. The flowers are sessile or on short-peduncled cymes, with small pink to white flowers with five petals. Up to 90% of flowers fall from tree, but "Blossom Set" can be used to counter this effect. Flowering can occur throughout the year, but is typically in cycles associated with rain. Irrigation can be used to induce flowering. Flowering occurs primarily on old growth. Pollination rarely observed, but thought to be by the solitary bee, Centris. Honeybees do not appear effective (contested). Cross-pollination may or may not be required depending on variety or strain (contested). In available cultivars, fruit does set without obvious pollinators or need for cross-pollination. Fruits are round to oblate, cherry-like but with 3 lobes. They are bright red (rarely yellow-orange) with thin skin, easily bruised. The pulp is juicy, acid to sub-acid occasionally nearly sweet, with a delicate flavor and apple notes. The fruit is very high in Vitamin C, up to 4,000 mg per 100 g fresh weight, but typically around 1,500 mg C. Green fruits have twice the Vitamin C level of mature fruits. Fruits develop to maturity in less than 25 days. Seeds typically three with fluted wings, forming a triangle. Many aspects of seed viability have not been studied.
cultivation: The plant prefers full sun for fruit development, giving rise to the problem of winter protection in harsher climates. Shaded trees fruit, but at reduced fruit densities, and the plants themselves become somewhat spindly. Due to its shallow and smaller root system, acerola can be interplanted with other crops more closely than many trees. Acerolas grows in marl, limestone, clay and other heavy soils as long as it drains well; waterlogging of roots will cause plant death. Soil pH should be 6.5-7.5 as acid soils do not promote vigorous growth. Liming of trees and working into the soil is a common practice and necessary for high productivity. The acerola does best with 1000-2000 mm of water. However, as mentioned, acerola is drought-tolerant. Irrigation can be used to cause flowering and can regulate flower cycles. Under good constant irrigation, acerola will flower all year, with between 1 and 3 flowering peaks. Acerola does well with both overhead and drip irrigation. Acerolas requires a good, balanced fertilization schedule, and regular (once a year) liming of soil. Foliar sprays are very effective, and are used commercially. Mineral nutrition is very important, with good levels of boron and iron required. The plant will tolerate heavy pruning, but requires time for recovery. In more tropical areas, plants do not seem overly affected by pruning. Plants are pruned commercially with citrus pruners. Can be kept as a small bush (e.g. 5 ft) and will produce well. As with most frost-sensitive plants, the acerola will need some protection when grown in areas were frost can occur. Growing with overhead protection or growing next to a wall or building may be sufficient, but the plant may also be covered with heavy cloth or plastic sheeting draped over a frame for added protection. Container grown plants can be moved too a frost secure area. The fruit deteriorates rapidly once removed from tree; sensory differences can be noted within 4 hours. The fruit undergoes rapid fermentation, and is typically unusable by 3-5 days. Unrefrigerated fruit develops mold quickly The best uses are direct eating, jams and jellies, and syrups. Juices, which are popular in Brazil, do not hold their sensory characteristics for extended periods. The fruit has also been used for baby food, as a supplement source for Vitamin C, as an ice cream and pop-sickle ingredient, and in many home recipes. Frozen fruit falls apart when thawed. In the tropics, there are typically 3 harvest periods per year. In more temperate areas, one and possibly 2 harvests occur. With regular irrigation, some fruit production may occur through much of the year. Eight year old trees can yield from 30 to 60 pounds of fruit. From seed, plant can fruit in the 2nd or 3rd year; cuttings may fruit in the first year. Productivity increases over a 15 to 20 year period, and then levels or declines. Forty year old productive trees are known in southern Florida.
propagation: Acerolas can be propagated by seed, cutting, grafting, and other standard methods. The plant does not appear stringent in its requirements. Seed viability can be very low; in some groves, seedlings are never observed. Cuttings are considered the simplest method of propagation and, with the use standard IBA hormone, give near 100% success rates. Grafting onto rootstocks has not been systematically studied, although grafts onto Byrsonima crassifolia rootstock have been successful.
pests_and_diseases: The Acerola is susceptible to root-knot nematode (Meloidogyne spp.) which causes serious problems with young trees and slower losses of productivity in older trees. It is also attacked by a variety of common insects, such as aphids, whitefly and scale. In other areas of the world (Mexico, Caribbean) weevils (Anthonomus spp.) are serious pests, and can limit fruit production. In tropical areas, Cercospora fungi can be a major cause disease.

description: Jackfruit is adapted to humid tropical and near-tropical climates. Mature trees have survived temperatures of about 27° F in southern Florida, but these were frozen to large limbs. Young trees are likely to be killed at temperatures below 32° F. Unlike its relative, the breadfruit, the jackfruit is not injured by cool weather several degrees above freezing. There are only a dozen or so bearing jackfruit trees today in southern Florida, and these are valued mainly as curiosities. There are also several trees planted in the Asian exhibit at the San Diego Zoo. What they will do or how high they will grow remains a question. The tree is too large to make a suitable container-grown plant. The jackfruit tree is handsome and stately. In the tropics it grows to an enormous size, like a large eastern oak. In California it is very doubtful that it would ever approach this size. All parts contain a sticky, white latex. The leaves are oblong, oval, or elliptic in form, 4 to 6 inches in length, leathery, glossy, and deep green in color. Juvenile leaves are lobed. Male and female flowers are borne in separate flower-heads. Male flower-heads are on new wood among the leaves or above the female. They are swollen, oblong, from an inch to four inches long and up to an inch wide at the widest part. They are pale green at first, then darken. When mature the head is covered with yellow pollen that falls rapidly after flowering. The female heads appear on short, stout twigs that emerge from the trunk and large branches, or even from the soil-covered base of very old trees. They look like the male heads but without pollen, and soon begins to swell. The stalks of both male and female flower-heads are encircled by a small green ring. Jackfruit is the largest tree-borne fruit in the world, reaching 80 pounds in weight and up to 36 inches long and 20 inches in diameter. The exterior of the compound fruit is green or yellow when ripe. The interior consists of large edible bulbs of yellow, banana-flavored flesh that encloses a smooth, oval, light-brown seed. The seed is 3/4 to 1-1/2 inches long and 1/2 to 3/4 inches thick and is white and crisp within. There may be 100 or up to 500 seeds in a single fruit, which are viable for no more than three or four days. When fully ripe, the unopened jackfruit emits a strong disagreeable odor, resembling that of decayed onions, while the pulp of the opened fruit smells of pineapple and banana. There are two main varieties. In one, the fruits have small, fibrous, soft, mushy, but very sweet carpels with a texture somewhat akin to a raw oysters. The other variety is crisp and almost crunchy though not quite as sweet. This form is the more important commercially and is more palatable to western tastes.
cultivation: Location : The jackfruit tree should have a well-drained, frost-free location that is sunny and warm. Soil : The jackfruit flourishes in rich, deep soil of medium or open texture. Planting on top of an old compost heap would be ideal. The faster one can force a tropical plant to grow, the better the chance of keeping it alive. The tree needs the best drainage and cannot tolerate "wet feet". Irrigation : The tree will not tolerate drought. Water frequently during warm months and warm periods in cooler months. Less water is necessary during colder weather. Fertilization : The jackfruit's requirements are not known, but frequent, weak solutions of all-purpose fertilizer will speed the plant's growth without causing burn. In the regions where it is commonly grown, it succeeds without much care from man, the sole necessity being abundant moisture. Frost protection : Although mature jackfruit trees will take several degrees of frost, it is prudent to provide young plants with overhead protection if possible and plant them on the south side of a wall or building. Small plants should be given complete protection with a covering on cold nights and even a light bulb if possible. Pruning : Little or no pruning is required other than to remove any dead branches from the interior of the tree, so that sufficient light is obtained for the developing fruit. Harvest : Jackfruits mature 3 to 8 months from flowering. When mature, there is usually a change of fruit color from light green to yellow-brown. Spines, closely spaced, yield to moderate pressure, and there is a dull, hollow sound when the fruit is tapped. After ripening, they turn brown and deteriorate rather quickly.
propagation: Propagation is usually by seeds, which can be kept no longer than a month before planting. Germination requires 3 to 8 weeks. The seedlings should be moved when no more than 4 leaves have appeared. A more advanced seedling, with its long and delicate tap root is very difficult to transplant successfully. Cutting-grown plants and grafted seedlings are possible. Air-layering is common in India.
pests_and_diseases: A variety of pests and diseases afflict the jackfruit tree and fruit regions where it is commonly grown. In California the white fly is a minor pest.

description: Gooseberries (Ribes uva-crispa subsp. var.) are deciduous shrubs, growing to 3 feet tall and 6 feet wide. They are fast-growing under optimum conditions and suitable for training as a standard. American types have weeping stems that can root where they touch the ground. The buds emerge early in spring, with alternate, single, deeply lobed leaves that are glossy dark green (European types) or pale to gray-green (American types). Stems are thin, woody, and thorny, with American types being densely bristly. Inconspicuous green flowers with pink-flushed petals open in early spring, borne laterally on one-year-old wood and short spurs of older wood. Flowers are self-fertile and pollinated by wind and insects. The fruit is a berry, borne singly or in pairs, containing many minute seeds. Fruit color can be green, white, yellow, or shades of red to almost black. European fruits can be very large (up to 1 inch long), while American fruits are smaller (to 1/2 inch), perfectly round, and turn pink to wine-red at maturity. Gooseberries thrive in summer humid, cool regions with significant winter chilling and are fairly productive in cooler coastal areas. They are perennial plants.
cultivation: Gooseberries prefer morning sun, afternoon part-shade, and good air circulation. While most productive in full sun, leaves can sunburn in intense sunlight, especially in California. They can be grown in the high shade of fruit trees or on the north side of buildings. American varieties are more sun tolerant. Plants quickly decline if soil or air temperature exceeds 85°F. They tolerate a wide range of soils, except waterlogged ones, but prefer well-drained, heavy clay loams that retain moisture and stay cool, especially in hot summers. Sandy soils are less suitable. A thick organic mulch helps keep the soil cool. Gooseberries have fibrous, shallow roots, making them ideal for drip irrigation; consistent watering is crucial as drought stress can lead to leaf loss and mildew. They have a high requirement for potassium (half an ounce per square yard annually) and a moderate need for nitrogen (four to eight ounces per square yard), but excessive nitrogen promotes disease. Magnesium is also important, so dolomitic limestone is recommended for acidic soils. Pruning is essential to manage growth and facilitate harvest. Bushes are typically grown on a permanent short 'leg' (about six inches), with new shoots continually replacing older ones. Stems are allowed to grow for 4-5 years, then oldest ones are removed. Pruning should aim for an open head to improve air circulation, ease fruit picking, and provide shade to prevent scalding. The best time to prune is autumn or winter, removing weak young shoots, unwanted strong shoots, and heading back or cutting out side shoots. Branches older than three years should be removed, as should any touching the ground. Gooseberries begin bearing in the second year, with a full crop by the fourth season. Annual application of well-rotted barnyard manure in autumn, followed by spring harrowing, helps maintain soil condition. Average yield is 8-10 pounds per bush. Fruit for culinary use is picked underripe, while dessert fruit must be fully ripe.
propagation: Gooseberries can be propagated from cuttings or by layering. Layering is generally more reliable, especially for American varieties. For layering, prune bushes severely in autumn to encourage strong young growth. In early July, heap earth around the bush, covering all but the shoot tips. American varieties root by autumn and can be transplanted; English varieties may take two years. Cuttings from well-ripened wood (6-8 inches long) can be taken in early fall, even with some leaves, dipped in hormone, and potted in ordinary soil, kept in part shade for the first year. They can be buried over winter and planted deep in nursery rows in spring. Both American and English varieties can be propagated from greenwood cuttings in a greenhouse or hotbed with bottom heat. Tip layering is a surer method, though it yields fewer plants. Seeds require moist stratification (just above freezing) for 3-4 months; plants from seed bear in 5 years, from cuttings in 2 years.
pests_and_diseases: Gooseberries are susceptible to several pests and diseases. Aphids commonly attack young leaves, causing distortion. Spider mites are prevalent in summer and require spraying after harvest. The clear-winged borer lays eggs on stems in April; larvae bore into the pith, causing wilting and death of stems. Infested stems should be cut out, and spraying may be necessary. The gooseberry sawfly, present in the Pacific Northwest, hollows out berries. Ribes species are hosts for White Pine blister rust, which is lethal to 5-needle pines, leading to gooseberry bans in some lumber-growing counties. Botrytis and Anthracnose can cause leaf rot and loss of young growth, especially on ground-lying or splashed stems. Gooseberry mildew is a common problem for both European and American types, exacerbated by coastal fog, drought stress, or overhead irrigation; keeping plants turgid and applying Benomyl spray before flowering and after harvest can control it. Roots are susceptible to Oak Root fungus (Armillaria mellea) and Phytophthora. The currant-worm (Pteronus ribesii) is a widespread pest; its larvae strip leaves, weakening plants and reducing fruit quality. For the first brood, a weak paris green mixture (1 oz to 10 gallons water) can be sprayed or dusted. For the second brood, white hellebore powder (dusted or 1 oz to 2 gallons water) should be used. The gooseberry fruit-worm (Zophodia grossulariae) causes premature coloring and webbing of berries; hand-picking injured clusters is the best control, and poultry may help destroy larvae.

description: Jackfruit is adapted to humid tropical and near-tropical climates. Mature trees have survived temperatures of about 27° F, but young trees are likely to be killed below 32° F. It is a handsome and stately tree, growing to an enormous size in the tropics, with all parts containing a sticky, white latex. The leaves are oblong, oval, or elliptic, 4 to 6 inches long, leathery, glossy, and deep green. Male and female flowers are borne in separate flower-heads. Jackfruit is the largest tree-borne fruit in the world, reaching up to 80 pounds, 36 inches long, and 20 inches in diameter. The exterior is green or yellow when ripe, and the interior consists of large edible bulbs of yellow, banana-flavored flesh enclosing a smooth, oval, light-brown seed. A single fruit can contain 100 to 500 seeds, viable for only three or four days. When fully ripe, the unopened fruit has a strong disagreeable odor, while the opened pulp smells of pineapple and banana. There are two main varieties: one with small, fibrous, soft, mushy, very sweet carpels, and another that is crisp and almost crunchy, which is more commercially important and palatable to western tastes.
cultivation: The jackfruit tree requires a well-drained, frost-free, sunny, and warm location. It flourishes in rich, deep soil of medium or open texture, ideally on top of an old compost heap, and needs excellent drainage, unable to tolerate "wet feet." Irrigation should be frequent during warm months and warm periods in cooler months, with less water needed during colder weather, as the tree will not tolerate drought. Fertilization requirements are not fully known, but frequent, weak solutions of all-purpose fertilizer can speed growth. In common growing regions, it succeeds with abundant moisture and little human care. For frost protection, young plants should be provided with overhead cover and planted on the south side of a wall or building; small plants need complete protection with a covering and possibly a light bulb on cold nights. Little to no pruning is required, other than removing dead branches from the interior to ensure sufficient light for developing fruit. Jackfruits mature 3 to 8 months from flowering, indicated by a color change from light green to yellow-brown, spines yielding to moderate pressure, and a dull, hollow sound when tapped. After ripening, they turn brown and deteriorate quickly.
propagation: Propagation is usually by seeds, which remain viable for no more than a month before planting. Germination takes 3 to 8 weeks. Seedlings should be moved when they have no more than 4 leaves, as more advanced seedlings with long, delicate tap roots are very difficult to transplant successfully. Cutting-grown plants and grafted seedlings are also possible. Air-layering is a common method in India.
pests_and_diseases: A variety of pests and diseases afflict the jackfruit tree and fruit in regions where it is commonly grown. In California, the white fly is noted as a minor pest.

description: Land cress (Barbarea verna), also known by many other common names including American cress and early wintercress, is a biennial herb in the Brassicaceae family. It is native to southwestern Europe and cultivated in Florida. It has been cultivated as a leaf vegetable in England since the 17th century. It is considered a satisfactory substitute for watercress and can be used in sandwiches, salads, cooked like spinach, or in soup. It needs full sun and frequent watering.
cultivation: It is easier to cultivate than watercress, requiring less water. It can be grown easily in any garden, loves water but does not do well when partially submerged for long periods. It needs full sun and frequent watering in any garden, unless near a direct source of water. The plant easily self-seeds on a neutral soil.
propagation: By seed
pests_and_diseases: Very resistant to club root and insect attacks (whitefly, cabbage root fly, cabbage white butterfly, and cabbage moths)

description: Cranberries (Vaccinium subgenus Oxycoccos or Oxycoccos) are evergreen dwarf shrubs or trailing vines, low and creeping, up to 2 m long and 5 to 20 cm in height, with slender, wiry stems and small evergreen leaves. They are found in acidic bogs throughout the cooler parts of the Northern Hemisphere. The flowers are dark pink with distinct reflexed petals, pollinated by domestic honey bees. The fruit is an epigynous berry, initially white, turning deep red when ripe, larger than the leaves, edible with an acidic taste. They are perennial shrubs, originating from NE US and adjacent Canada, thriving in sun with wet, moist, or moderate water conditions, and are hardy in USDA Zones 3 to 7. A specific species, Vaccinium macrocarpon (Larger American Cranberry), has slender, creeping, elongated stems 1-4 ft. long, somewhat coarser and stouter, with ascending flower-branches. Its leaves are oblong or oval, obtuse or retuse, 1/3 – 1/2 in. long, with a less revolute margin. Flowers are larger with shorter filaments, and the red berry is larger, 1/3-1 inch long.
cultivation: Cranberries require regular water during the growing season. Commercial growers flood beds during harvest or winter for cold protection. Thin layers of sand (1 inch or less) are spread every 2-3 years to reduce pests and rejuvenate vines, keeping them short and close. Ideal bogs must be drainable (surface water no higher than 1 foot below surface), retain summer moisture, have sufficient water for flooding, a fairly level surface for uniform flooding, and not be overly susceptible to frosts. Winter flooding (around December 1st) prevents heaving and winter-killing. Spring growth can be controlled by drainage time to avoid early frosts, and reflooding can protect against later severe frosts. Reflooding around June 1st for no more than 48 hours can protect against insects without harming the crop. Summer irrigation is managed by adjusting water levels in ditches. Before planting, land must be cleared of natural growth, stumps, and roots, and leveled. Some methods involve flooding for two years to kill vegetation, making it easier to clear dead roots. In Massachusetts, cleared bogs are covered with 3 to 5 inches of sand. Bogs are kept free from other plants primarily by hand-pulling, and irrigation water is regulated. Fertilizing has shown success in increasing crops.
propagation: Plants are propagated by moving established plants to a sand bed. Stems (vines) are pushed into the sand at intervals and held down for roots to establish. Frequent watering is needed during rooting until roots form and new shoots sprout. Light applications of nitrogen fertilizer are common through the first growing season. Cuttings, preferably 8-10 inches long, are secured by mowing vigorous vines and thrust diagonally into the bog surface 12-14 inches apart, with 3-4 inches exposed. If sanded, cuttings must extend into the muck below. Vines send out runners that root at intervals, forming upright stems that cover the bog. It takes three to five years from planting for the ground to mat over and a crop to be expected.
pests_and_diseases: Cranberries are susceptible to false blossom. Flooding is a primary method to control pests, including sacrificing a year's crop by flooding to clear severe insect infestations, which can lead to increased yields the following year. Spraying with bordeaux mixture is commonly practiced to prevent 'scald,' a fungous disease. Various insects attack the roots, leaves, blossoms, and fruit. Water is considered the safest and surest weapon against many insects. Arsenical poisons are expensive, have indifferent success, and are suspected of being harmful to the vines.

description: The Arecales family, better known as Palm Trees or Palms, comprises roughly 202 known genera and 2600 species, mostly restricted to tropical, subtropical, and warm temperate climates. Palms are among the most striking and extensively cultivated plant families, characterized by tall, mostly straight, unbranched trunks surmounted by a spreading canopy of huge pinnate or digitate leaves. They are abundant in America and Asia, and conspicuous in the Pacific Islands. Palms are highly ornamental and of great economic value, providing food, shelter, and materials for manufactured products. Exceptions to unbranched stems are few, mainly in the genus Hyphaene. Foliage is primarily fan-veined or feather-veined. Flowers are generally small, dull-colored, but can be produced in prodigious quantities. Seeds vary greatly in size and shape. Many palms are unisexual, while others produce both male and female flowers on the same spadix. Many palms are armed with stout thorns or prickles. Economically, palms are incredibly versatile, with examples like the Palmyra palm, coconut palm, date palm, palm oil, rattan, sago, Piassaba fiber, vegetable ivory, betel-nut, and edible 'cabbage'. Botanically, as the trunk rises, old leaves and petioles fall, forming a shaggy capital. Palms are mostly trees up to 200 feet, but some are climbing or low shrubs. Inflorescences (spadices) arise from leaf axils, often branched, and covered by a dry spathe. Flowers are small, regular, hermaphrodite, monoecious, or dioecious, typically with three sepals and three petals, and nearly always six stamens. The ovary is free, usually with three cells and a solitary ovule. Fruits vary from dry and hard to fleshy and drupe-like, often with a hard fibrous coating. Seeds usually contain copious albumen. The species most used in commercial horticulture in the United States include Livistona chinensis, Howea Belmoreana and H. Forsteriana, Chrysalidocarpus lutescens, Cocos Weddelliana, Caryota urens, several Phoenix species (P. canariensis widely planted outdoors), Seaforthia elegans, and some Ptychosperma and Livistona types. Seedlings of many species are very much alike, often having a long, narrow, simple seed-leaf, making species recognition difficult in juvenile form. Palms in general are tropical in nature, though some species are found at higher elevations with cool nights. Cocos species are ornamental palms, with dwarf sections like C. australis being very ornamental and hardy. Other species like C. flexuosa, C. plumosa, C. coronata, and C. Romanzoffiana are graceful, fast-growing, and extensively planted for street or sidewalk trees, hardy as far north as central Florida. The common coconut (C. nucifera) thrives in southern Florida, ripening nuts and becoming naturalized. Australis group species are well-adapted to high pine land, forming beautiful specimens, flowering regularly, and bearing edible fruit.
cultivation: Palms are popular greenhouse subjects, succeeding best under shaded roofs and not requiring very high temperatures or much direct sunlight, being most satisfactory when young. Small palms are in high demand for room and table decoration, with species like Chrysalidocarpus lutescens, Howea Belmoreana and H. Forsteriana, Cocos Weddelliana, Livistona chinensis, and some Phoenix species being popular. Some palms, like sabals, palmettoes (Serenoa serrulata, Rhapidophyllum Hystrix), Nannorhops, and Chamaerops, can endure considerable frost. An abundance of water is required. The best soil is rotted sod mixed with a fair proportion of stable manure, and peat or sand for lighter, more open soil for delicate species. Summer care includes shading, as glass-grown foliage is tender. Repotting should be done in spring and summer, using pots only 1-2 inches larger and ramming the soil firmly. Midsummer's high temperature and humidity promote growth, provided watering, syringing, and proper ventilation are maintained. Night ventilation and liberal daytime airing prevent moisture accumulation and fungoid growths. Kentias and cocos are very popular for florists. Phoenix canariensis and other strong-growing Phoenix are used outdoors, while Phoenix Roebelenii is popular for house decoration. Livistona rotundifolia is a charming dwarf palm but requires constant vigilance against insects and a high temperature with moist atmosphere. Most species germinate readily in a warm greenhouse if seeds are fresh. Seeds are typically sown thickly in well-drained 6-inch pots filled with light soil, covered with 1/2 inch of soil, watered thoroughly, and given bottom heat, ensuring they do not become very dry. For young and growing palms, a minimum winter night temperature of 56° to 60° F is safest, with a day temperature increase of 15° to 20° being acceptable. Cocos palms are fast growers, with some types reaching 20 feet in fifteen years with ordinary care. They thrive on high pine land if well fertilized and watered during dry spells. Fertilizers rich in ammonia, phosphoric acid, and potash should be applied in December, January, and February. Old dry leaves, spathes, and fruit-stems should be removed at the end of September.
propagation: Most species are propagated from seed. Seeds are often imported, and their quality is crucial for successful stock development. Most species germinate readily in a warm greenhouse if fresh. Seeds are usually sown thickly in about 6-inch pots, which are well-drained and nearly filled with light soil, then covered with 1/2 inch of soil, watered thoroughly, and placed with bottom heat, never allowed to become very dry. Germination time varies greatly; Livistona chinensis germinates in two or three weeks and is ready for potting in about two months, while some attalea seeds may take up to three years.
pests_and_diseases: Fungoid growths can be prevented by proper ventilation. Snails can be troublesome, especially on young kentias, and can be discouraged by sprinkling slaked lime under benches. Insects are frequently troublesome, with various scale insects causing the greatest damage; peak reproduction periods are in May and September, and growers use dips and spraying with insecticides to control them. Red-spiders and thrips can also become established if forcible syringing is not consistently applied. Successful practice requires close observation and prompt removal of all insects. Livistona rotundifolia is easily spoiled by insects and requires constant vigilance. Other pests are known, as documented in reports like the Missouri Botanical Garden for 1898. A disease threatening the coconut industry in Tropical America was also reported in 1902. Cocos species are sometimes attacked by a fungous disease causing brown streaks in young leaves; the remedy involves pulling leaves apart and cutting out the injured young leaf near the growing bud, which may need repetition.

description: The avocado (Persea americana, also known as Persea gratissima) is a dense, fast-growing evergreen tree, typically forming a broad canopy and reaching up to 80 feet, though budded trees are considerably dwarfed. Grafted plants fruit in 1-2 years, while seedlings take 8-20 years. Its alternate, glossy, deep green leaves are elliptical to oblong-lanceolate, 4-10 inches long, persistent for 2-3 years, with new growth often wine-colored and Mexican varieties having an anise scent when crushed. Small, yellow-green flowers appear in January-March on loose axillary racemes, with each panicle producing 1-3 fruits. Avocado flowers are perfect but exhibit a unique pollination mechanism (Type A and Type B), with cross-pollination enhancing production. The fruit is a large fleshy drupe, highly variable in form (oblate, spherical, pyriform), color (light/dark green, purple, crimson, maroon), and size (1-6 inches in diameter, a few ounces to three or four pounds). The skin can be soft and pliable or coarsely granular, woody, and up to ⅛ inch thick. Fruits vary by type: West Indian are large, smooth, and low in oil; Guatemalan are medium, ovoid/pear-shaped, and pebbled; Mexican are small with paper-thin skins. The flesh is buttery when ripe, deep green near the skin, yellowish closer to the large, inedible seed, and has a delicious nutty flavor. It contains a large amount of vegetable oil (sometimes up to 18 percent), making it more of a food than a dessert. Avocados are high in monosaturates and can reduce blood cholesterol. The single, large, spherical or conical seed is often as large as a hen's egg, with two distinct coats. They thrive in mild-winter areas like California, Florida, and Hawaii, with different types adapted to varying temperature tolerances (West Indian: 32°F, Guatemalan: 26-30°F, Mexican: 19-24°F). They require some protection from high winds.
cultivation: Avocado cultivation is conducted commercially in Florida and California, and trees are also valued for shade and ornament. They are most productive in full sun, though they can grow in shade. In cooler regions, plant where winter sun is abundant. Varieties exist for both cool and strictly tropical climates, with some mature types withstanding temperatures as low as 20°F, while others are damaged below 27-28°F. Commercial varieties are grouped into Mexican, Guatemalan, and West Indian-South American types, each with distinct characteristics regarding fruit size, skin thickness, hardiness, and ripening season. Winter-fruiting varieties are most valuable for northern markets. Ideal fruit for shipping is about one pound, round or oval, with a sufficiently thick and tough skin, and a small, tight seed. Planting: Budded trees are planted 20-25 feet apart in orchards, while seedlings require at least 30 feet. Allow up to 20 feet of space. They are not suitable for hedgerows but can be planted 2-3 trees in a single large hole for space and pollination. Windbreaks are essential in windy areas. Transplanting is best in early spring after frost danger, before new growth, with a ball of soil around the roots and moderate pruning. Established trees are tough, but indoor plants need low night temperatures to bloom. Whitewashing trunks and branches prevents sunburn. Soil and Water: Avocados prefer loose, well-drained soils like decomposed granite or sandy loam; good drainage is paramount, and they will not survive in poorly drained locations. Sloping ground is preferred, and they grow well on hillsides, tolerating acid or alkaline soil. Over-irrigation is a common cause of failure, leading to root rot; soil moisture should be carefully monitored, especially before winter. In dry climates, regular and frequent irrigation is essential, especially for young trees. Fertilization: Young trees (after one year) benefit from balanced fertilizer four times yearly. Bearing trees benefit from a fertilizer containing 3% nitrogen, 5% phosphoric acid, and 12% potash, applied in several doses during the growing season (3-10 pounds per tree). Older trees need nitrogenous fertilizer in late winter and early summer. Organic nitrogen and leguminous cover-crops encourage young tree growth. Iron and zinc deficiencies can be corrected with foliar sprays. Pruning: Pruning is generally avoided as branches exposed to sun are susceptible to sunburn; only columnar cultivars require early pinching. Pruning involves heading back upward-shooting seedlings, trimming weak growths, and covering wounds with grafting wax or paint. Trees should be headed low to prevent wind damage and ease picking. Fruit is self-thinning. Frost Protection: Frost protection is crucial; choose hardy cultivars (Mexican types for colder regions), plant on slopes for air drainage, or near houses. Young trees need protection (palm leaves, corn stalks, burlap, covers, heat, trunk wraps) for the first 2-3 winters, and hardening by withholding water in late fall if irrigated. Harvest and Storage: Harvest time varies by cultivar, with commercial standards requiring 8% oil content. Mexican types ripen in 6-8 months, Guatemalan in 12-18 months. Guatemalan types can be stored for weeks, while Mexican types discolor quickly and need immediate consumption. For market, fruit should be graded by size, color, and form, then carefully packed in light wooden crates with good ventilation, individually wrapped in strong paper to prevent bruising. Refrigeration at 40-45°F is essential for long-distance shipping; temperatures below 40°F cause flesh decomposition.
propagation: Before 1900, propagation was exclusively by seed, leading to variable and often inferior fruit. Commercial cultivation now primarily uses budding. Seedlings are unsuitable for commercial use due to variability but are planted for ornamental value, selecting seeds from desirable fruits. Mexican type seeds are commonly used for rootstock due to their strong growth. Cleaned seeds should be planted as soon as they are ripe, singly in pots or rows in open ground, pointed end up, with the tip projecting above the soil. They require light, porous, humus-rich soil and continuous moisture (not soggy). Germination takes 2-3 weeks. Young plants are rapid growers and require frequent repotting. Seedlings are ready for budding the following year. Budding: Avocado is budded on seedlings of the same species. Shield budding, similar to citrus, is the most successful method. Budding season varies by locality: late autumn/winter in Florida, May/June (or fall) in California, winter/early spring in Hawaii, and typically January when suitable buds are available. Budwood should be recent growth, hardened, with plump, well-developed buds, cut large (at least 1¼ inches long, thick enough to take a small amount of wood). A keen budding knife is essential. The incision in the stock is preferably an inverted T. The bud is gently pushed in, tied firmly with waxed tape (eye exposed). After 3-5 weeks, the wrap is loosened, and fully removed when the bud grows 3-4 inches. The bud is forced into growth by partly girdling or cutting the stock above it. Adventitious buds on the stock must be rubbed off weekly. When the bud is 8-10 inches high, the stock is trimmed close above it and the stump painted. Other methods: Clonal rootstocks can be propagated using the etiolation technique, involving grafting seedlings with a root rot tolerant clonal scion, cutting the top, and encouraging etiolated growth in a dark, humid environment with a tar paper collar filled with vermiculite, which is then rooted as cuttings. Larger stocks are worked by bark grafts in the spring. Scions are collected in December-January after buds are well-formed, and grafts are protected with moistened plastic bags and vented paper bags. Inarching and grafting are practiced to a limited extent, but budding is preferred. Cuttings can be grown with bottom heat but lack the vigor of budded trees. Unproductive seedlings can be top-worked by severe spring pruning, leaving branch stumps, and budding the resulting sprouts when they reach ⅜ inch diameter.
pests_and_diseases: Avocado trees are susceptible to several pests and diseases. Pests include rats and squirrels (requiring protection like tin trunk wraps), leaf-rolling caterpillars (Tortrix and Amorbia) which may damage branch terminals, Avocado Brown Mite (controlled with powdered sulfur), Six-spotted Mite (more harmful, may need miticides), and snails (problem in California).
