In California, two or three scale insects have been noted, with black scale (Saissetia olex) sometimes requiring combative measures. The avocado mealy bug (Pseudococcus nipae) is troublesome in Hawaii. Diseases: Fungi: Dothiorella (Botryosphaeria ribis) canker: Infects the trunk, causing dead patches and leading to darkened, rancid-smelling spots in the fruit flesh after harvest. Mexican types are immune to trunk cankers but not fruit infection; there is no economical control. Root Rot (Phytophthora cinnamomi): A major soil-borne fungus in California, causing yellowing and dropping leaves. Prevention involves selecting disease-free plants, avoiding previously infected sites, ensuring good drainage, and preventing transport of the fungus by equipment. Wither-tip fungus (Colletotrichum gleosporioides): Attacks avocados in Florida and can be controlled with bordeaux mixture. Virus: Sun Blotch: A viral disease causing yellow streaking on stems, mottling of leaves, fruit deformation, and rectangular cracking of the trunk. It is spread by infected scions, contaminated tools, and root grafts, emphasizing the importance of using virus-free propagating wood.

description: Guava (Psidium guajava) is an evergreen, shallow-rooted shrub or small tree, typically growing 10-12 feet in California but up to 33 feet in other regions. It has smooth, mottled green or reddish-brown bark that peels in thin flakes, revealing a 'bony' trunk. The plant branches close to the ground and often produces suckers. Leaves are opposite, oval or oblong-elliptic, 2-6 inches long, dull-green, stiff, leathery, with pronounced veins, and slightly downy on the underside; crushed leaves are aromatic. Faintly fragrant white flowers, about 1 inch wide, are borne singly or in clusters in leaf axils, with 4 or 5 petals that are quickly shed, leaving a prominent tuft of white stamens. Guavas are primarily self-fruitful and bloom throughout the year in mild-winter areas, with the heaviest bloom in spring. Fruits are round, ovoid, or pear-shaped, 2-4 inches long, with 4 or 5 floral remnants at the apex. Varieties differ in flavor and seediness; ripe fruits are soft, creamy, with an edible rind. Flesh color can be white, pink, yellow, or red, with a sweet, musky, pungent aroma. Seeds are numerous but small and edible in good varieties. The plant is best adapted to warm climates, thriving in both humid and dry conditions, but can only survive a few degrees of frost (recovering from 29°F but young trees are sensitive). It can withstand considerable neglect, including temporary waterlogging and high temperatures, and tends to bear fruit better in areas with a definite winter or cooler season. The Cattley or strawberry guava (P. Cattleianum) is a related species known for superior hardiness (withstanding 22°F), forming an arborescent shrub 15-20 feet tall, with thick, leathery, glossy leaves and purplish-maroon or sulfur-yellow fruit that lacks the pungency of P. Guajava.
cultivation: Guavas require a frost-free location and prefer full sun. They tolerate various soil conditions but thrive in rich, well-drained soils high in organic matter, with a pH range of 5 to 7. While they can survive dry summers, regular deep watering is best, allowing the ground to dry several inches deep between waterings to prevent delayed bloom and fruit drop. Pruning primarily involves shaping the tree and removing water shoots and suckers; guavas can withstand heavy pruning and can be used as informal hedges or screens, as fruit is borne on new growth. They are fast growers and heavy feeders, benefiting from regular fertilizer applications, with mature trees potentially needing up to 1/2 pound of actual nitrogen per year, applied monthly before heavy pruning. For frost protection in cooler areas, overhead cover and planting on the warm side of a building are effective. Additional protection during freezes can be provided by a fabric-covered frame over the plant, possibly with electric lights for warmth. Potted plants can be moved to protected sites. Fruits ripen year-round in warmer regions, indicated by a change in color and aroma. For best flavor, allow fruit to ripen on the tree, or pick green-mature and ripen off the tree at room temperature (hastened by placing in a brown paper bag with a banana or apple). Mature green fruit can be stored for 2-5 weeks at 46°-50°F with 85-95% humidity, but colored fruit bruises easily and deteriorates quickly.
propagation: Guava seeds remain viable for many months and typically germinate in 2-3 weeks, though it can take up to 8 weeks. Since guavas do not reliably come true from seed, vegetative propagation is widely practiced. While grafting is possible, it is not widely used. Patch-budding by the Forkert Method is considered the most reliable technique, with side-veneer grafting, approach grafting, and marcotting also being satisfactory. Shield-budding, performed similarly to citrus, also shows promise. Guavas can also be grown from root cuttings, using 5-10 inch lengths of roots (excluding the smallest and largest) laid flat in a moist, prepared bed and covered with 2-4 inches of soil. Air-layering and cuttings of half-ripened wood (1/4-1/2 inch pieces with bottom heat and rooting hormone) are also viable methods. However, trees grown from cuttings or air-layering lack a taproot and are prone to being blown down in their first few years. A common difficulty with budded and grafted guavas is the production of water sprouts and suckers from the rootstocks. Seeds should be planted as fresh as possible in a light sandy loam, taking care to avoid over-watering young plants. Once the second leaves form, plants should be potted off and grown in pots until transplanted into permanent positions in late spring, as they are somewhat difficult to transplant from open ground.
pests_and_diseases: In humid climates, foliage diseases such as anthracnose can be a problem, controllable with regular fungicide applications. Root-rot nematodes can reduce plant vigor where present. In southern Florida, guava whitefly, guava moth, and Caribbean fruit fly can be major issues, though they have not been reported in California. In California, mealy-bugs, scale, common white flies, and thrips can be problematic. The black scale (Saissetia oleae) also frequently attacks P. guajava in California, requiring control through fumigation or spraying. In some tropical countries where fruit flies are an issue, fruits are covered with paper sacks when small to protect them. The Mediterranean fruit-fly can injure fruits, and a scab has been observed in Brazil. Generally, the Cattley guava (P. Cattleianum) is remarkably free from insect pests or fungous diseases, and P. guajava also tends to require little attention in many other countries.

description:
The peach (Prunus persica), including the fuzzless nectarine, is a species of Prunus native to China, bearing an edible juicy fruit. It is a deciduous tree, 4–10 m tall, belonging to the Rosaceae family, and classified with the almond in the subgenus Amygdalus. Leaves are lanceolate (7–16 cm long, 2–3 cm broad, pinnately veined). Flowers are pink, 2.5–3 cm diameter, with five petals, produced in early spring before leaves. The fruit has yellow or whitish flesh, a delicate aroma, and a velvety (peaches) or smooth (nectarines) skin. The single, large, red-brown, oval seed (1.4–2 cm long) is surrounded by a wood-like husk. Peaches are stone fruits (drupes), divided into clingstones and freestones. White-fleshed peaches are typically very sweet with little acidity, while yellow-fleshed peaches have an acidic tang coupled with sweetness. Nectarines are a cultivar group with smooth skin due to a recessive gene, generally slightly smaller and sweeter.
The species is widely cultivated, with adaptability extending due to hardier varieties and effective disease control. A hardy form, A. Davidiana from China, is used as a stock for cherries. Common types are grouped into races: Peen-to (flat peach), South China (e.g., Honey), Spanish or Indian (e.g., Cabler), North China (e.g., Greensboro, Waddell, Carman), and Persian (e.g., Crawford, Mountain Rose). Commercial varieties often show mixed characteristics, like Elberta, which is also prized for its large size, beauty, and fine keeping qualities. Three distinct types of peach blossoms are recognized: large (North China type), medium (Elberta, Belle), and small (Early and Late Crawford). Double-flowered and purple-leaved forms are cultivated as ornamentals.
The peach is of wide commercial importance, known for its size, flavor, color, and shipping qualities. Varieties are recognized for shipping (e.g., Alexander, Elberta), drying (e.g., Foster, Lovell, Muir), and canning (e.g., Tuskena, Orange, Phillips). Peaches without red at the pit are preferred for canning to prevent syrup discoloration. In Southern states, peaches are known for their bright color, rich, and sweet flavor, though they may be less juicy than those grown farther North. The industry has shifted from Middle-Atlantic and Great Lakes districts to southern and western districts, partly due to the San Jose scale. Major peach-producing regions include the Great Lakes Belt, Middle Atlantic states, the Piedmont area, the Central or Mississippi Valley district, and the Pacific Coast Belt. Florida cultivates a special type from the South China race due to its warm climate, though their bitter-sweet flavor is not generally market-profitable.
cultivation:
Peach plants require a chilling period and are not very cold-hardy, tolerating temperatures to around −26 °C to −30 °C, though flower buds are killed between −15 °C and −25 °C. They need significant summer heat (mean temperatures of the hottest month between 20 °C and 30 °C). Spring frosts can damage or kill flowers if temperatures drop below about −4 °C. The ideal climate has winter extremes not much lower than zero, no prolonged warm periods in winter, absence of late spring frosts, and bright sun during ripening. Extremes of warmth or cold are detrimental.
Peaches should be located in full sun with good air flow. They are best planted in early winter to allow roots to establish, or in spring as early as soil conditions permit. Fall planting is successful in mild winters with sandy, well-drained soils. Trees are typically set 20-24 feet apart each way.
Peaches thrive on a wide range of soil types, preferring sandy loam, gravelly, or stony loams that are deep and well-drained, and also sands. Heavy, poorly drained soils should be avoided. In California, they thrive on sandy, loamy soils adapted to irrigation and well-drained, as well as heavier red and black soils mixed with gravel in foothill and coast regions. For commercial plantings, uniformly favorable areas near good shipping-points with available labor are crucial. Elevation is important, and sites should be accessible, avoiding uneven, gullied, wet, or hilly areas. Protection from severe winds is desirable. Land preparation involves deep plowing, sometimes subsoiling, and for modern orchards, dynamiting planting holes. A well-defined plan should precede planting, with packing-houses and buildings strategically located.
For optimum growth, peach trees require a constant supply of water, increased shortly before harvest, with drip irrigation being ideal. Irrigation is essential in arid climates, often secured by pumping for fall cover-crops. They have a high nutrient requirement, needing more nitrogen than most other fruit trees. An NPK fertilizer can be applied regularly, and an additional mulch of poultry manure in autumn. If leaves are yellow or small, or growth is minimal, more nitrogen is needed (e.g., blood meal, bone meal, calcium ammonium nitrate). Fertilization is crucial for growth and flower-bud production; trees in full bearing should achieve 12-18 inches of annual growth. Average fertilizer application includes nitrate of soda, muriate of potash, and acid phosphate, with nitrogenous fertilizers applied sparingly in severe winter regions. Cover-crops (e.g., Canada field peas, fenugreek, vetch, cowpeas) and barnyard manure are preferred, though commercial fertilizers are becoming more common. Intercropping with vegetable crops can be done between rows for the first two seasons. Tillage involves plowing or discing the soil in spring to a fine, mellow condition, maintained by frequent harrowing until mid-season. Cultivation ceases 10-14 days before fruit ripening, with early varieties receiving additional cultivation after harvest.
Pruning is vital. At planting, trees are root and top pruned (roots shortened by one-third, top cut to 18-24 inches). After the first season, 3-4 well-placed main branches are selected for the framework. Annual dormant-season pruning involves retaining strong side branches, cutting back leading branches to the first good side branch, and thinning out shoots to ensure light exposure and fruit color, avoiding a central leader. Fruit-buds develop on one-year-old wood. In the second winter, severe shortening-in and thinning are practiced to achieve a goblet form. From the third year, 2-3 laterals are allowed on frameworks and severely shortened, with less severe pruning in the fourth year. Regular annual pruning is needed for good crops and longevity. After several years, severe cutting back may be needed to reduce top size and stimulate vigorous wood, especially if fruit-buds are destroyed in winter.
Fruit should be thinned when they reach 2 cm in diameter (about 2 months after flowering) to ensure good size, sugar, and flavor. Thinning is a regular practice to prevent branch breakage and improve fruit size, typically done after the natural "drop" or when fruits are the size of a shelled hickory-nut, spacing them 4-8 inches apart.
Harvest occurs from late May into August, lasting about a week per tree. Harvesting requires organized labor and careful picking based on fruit color, not pressure. Fruit is often picked into distinct picking-baskets and transported by low-wheeled wagons to packing-houses. Fresh fruit is best consumed on the day of picking and does not keep well. For distant markets, refrigeration is necessary during rail transport, with fruit shipped in spaced, iced refrigerator cars, often from orchards with railroad side-tracks. Post-harvest, precooling is practiced. Packing involves tight arrangement in various packages (e.g., Jersey/Delaware basket, Georgia six-basket carrier, Michigan bushel, Climax basket, California box) to prevent movement during transit. Mechanical graders are used but are still evolving. For fresh shipment, peaches are wrapped in soft paper and packed in 20-pound boxes. For local consumption, they are shipped in open lug boxes.
For drying, free-stone peaches are halved, pitted, and sun-dried on trays, preceded by sulfur fumigation for at least four hours. Peeled peaches, achieved by a hot lye bath followed by a cold water bath, sell at twice the price of unpeeled. Canning is a major industry, primarily using cling peaches (e.g., Tuskena, Orange, Phillips) and mechanized processes, with women halving the fruit.
In cold climates, protection methods include covering trunks and branches with materials like cornstalks or straw. Trees can be laid on the ground by manipulating roots when young or by bending newly planted trees with a long prostrate trunk. Under irrigated conditions, the ground is softened with water, trees are pushed over, tied, and covered with burlap and earth. Careful judgment is needed when gradually uncovering trees in spring, and raised trees are supported by props.
propagation:
Most peach trees sold by nurseries are named cultivars budded onto a suitable rootstock. The peach is universally propagated by means of pits or seeds. It is possible to grow a tree from a peach or nectarine seed, but the fruit quality of the resulting tree will be very unpredictable. Budding onto plum or cherry stocks is sometimes done but can dwarf the tree and make it susceptible to stock troubles. "Natural seedling pits" from Tennessee and North Carolina are considered best for propagation due to higher viability and hardier stock, though seeds from canning factories (commercial varieties) are also used.
For propagation, seeds are treated in two ways: in severe freezing climates, they are planted in autumn in nursery rows (4-6 feet apart, a few inches apart in rows, covered 2 inches deep); in less severe climates, pits are stratified shallowly in autumn, dug up in spring, kernels separated, and planted in nursery rows, with uncracked pits broken by a hammer. Pits not affected by frost usually fail to grow the first season. Seeds should be planted in good soil and cultivated carefully to ensure seedlings reach 24-30 inches high by late August for budding.
Buds are inserted in late August or early September, uniting with the stock without immediate growth. The following spring, seedlings are cut back above the inserted buds, and stock shoots are removed, allowing the desired bud to develop into a vigorous shoot or tree (3-6 feet high by season's end), ready for sale. "June buds" are produced by budding vigorous seedlings in June and selling the smaller resulting trees in the fall or following spring, primarily for planting in the South. Peach trees are transplanted in California when they are one year old from the bud. June buds are purchased when stock is scarce, provided they mature fully in the nursery before digging. The latitude where trees are raised has little impact if they are well-grown and free from pests and diseases; however, purchasing trees from nearby sources is generally recommended.
pests_and_diseases:
Peach trees are prone to various pests and diseases. The peach industry has faced significant challenges, notably from the San Jose scale, which caused widespread destruction from about 1900 to 1907, necessitating the adoption of spraying practices. In California, the San Jose scale is controlled by predaceous insects and lime-sulfur washes.
Insects:
*   **Borer:** Larvae feed under bark, weakening trees. Controlled by physical removal or asphaltum coatings. Low-headed trees can make removal more difficult.
*   **San Jose scale:** Controlled by dormant lime-sulfur spray.
*   **Plum-curculio:** Damages green fruits, causing fall or blemishes. Controlled by arsenate of lead spray.
*   **Other pests:** Bark-beetles (attack weakened trees), black peach aphis, and various beetles/grasshoppers (often managed by good culture). The crown root-borer is troublesome in some sections but is always under control.
Diseases:
*   **Leaf curl (Peach leaf-curl):** A fungous disease causing curled, distorted leaves and defoliation, which reduces crop yield. Controlled by lime-sulfur spray before leaf-buds open or a thorough spraying of Bordeaux.
*   **Brown-rot (Monilia):** A fungous disease causing rapid fruit decay, blossom death, and cankers, promoted by hot, muggy weather. The fruit is very susceptible to brown rot and a dark reddish spot. It caused serious annual damage until the self-boiled lime-sulfur summer spray proved to be a successful remedy. Controlled by thorough summer spraying with self-boiled lime-sulfur.
*   **Peach-scab:** A fungous disease causing black dots/blotches on fruit, leading to cracking and brown-rot entry. Controlled by summer spraying with self-boiled lime-sulfur.
*   **Mildew:** A fungous disease appearing as a white powdery substance on leaves/fruit, controlled by self-boiled lime-sulfur.
*   **Yellows, Little-peach, Rosette:** Diseases of unknown cause, transmitted by budding. Yellows causes premature, red-spotted, insipid fruit and wiry twig-growths. Little-peach causes drooping foliage and smaller, late-maturing fruit. Rosette causes tufts of leaf-development and is often fatal. Infected trees must be destroyed.
When establishing an orchard, it is crucial to plant trees that are free from yellows, little-peach, rosette, root-gall, scale, and peach-borers.
Winter Injuries: Include bud-killing (due to severe cold), twig-killing, collar injury (weather damage to bark at/below ground, prevented by mounding soil), bark-splitting, and sap-wood injury. These can weaken trees and make them susceptible to pests like bark-beetles.

description: Physalis peruviana, also known as Cape Gooseberry, Goldenberry, Husk Cherry, Peruvian Ground Cherry, or Poha, is a stronger grower than P. pubescens, standing partially erect and attaining a height of 1 1/2-3 ft. Its leaves are thicker, less regularly toothed, more pointed, heart-shaped at the base, and very pubescent or fuzzy. Flowers are larger (1/2-5/8in long), open-bell-shaped, with a widely spreading light yellow limb or border, and the interior of the throat blotched and veined with 5 purple spots, with blue-purple anthers. The husk is thicker, larger, somewhat hairy, and has a much longer point. This species is native to Brazil but became naturalized in the highlands of Peru and Chile. It is an annual in temperate regions and a perennial in the tropics, growing wild between 2,500 and 10,000 ft in the Andes and 1,000 to 8,000 ft in Hawaii. Plants are frost tender, killed at about 30° F. It is a soft-wooded, perennial, somewhat vining plant usually reaching 2 to 3 ft in height, potentially 6 ft with support. The purplish, spreading branches are ribbed and covered with fine hairs. Heart-shaped, nearly opposite leaves are 2-1/2 to 6 inches long and slightly velvety. Bell-shaped, nodding yellow flowers with dark purple-brown spots in the throat form in leaf axils, cupped by a purplish-green, hairy calyx. Fruit buds appear after 12 to 13 stem internodes. After flowering, the calyx expands into a straw-colored husk enclosing the fruit, which takes 70 to 80 days to mature. The fruit is a berry with smooth, waxy, orange-yellow skin and juicy pulp containing numerous very small yellowish seeds. Fruits drop when ripening, changing from green to golden-yellow. Unripe fruit is said to be poisonous to some. Cape gooseberries are self-pollinated, with pollination enhanced by gentle shaking or light water spraying.
cultivation:
Location: The plant likes a sunny, frost-free location, sheltered from strong winds. It does well planted next to a south-facing wall or in a patio.
Soil: The cape gooseberry will grow in any well drained soil but does best on sandy to gravelly loam. Very good crops are obtained on rather poor sandy ground.
Irrigation: The plant needs consistent watering to set a good fruit crop, but can't take "wet feet". Where drainage is a problem, the plantings should be on a gentle slope or the rows should be mounded. Irrigation can be cut back when the fruits are maturing. The plants become dormant during drought.
Fertilization: The cape gooseberry seems to thrive on neglect. Even moderate fertilizer tends to encourage excessive vegetative growth and to depress flowering. High yields are attained with little or no fertilizer.
Pruning: Very little pruning is needed unless the plant is being trained to a trellis. Pinching back of the growing shoots will induce more compact and shorter plants.
Frost Protection: In areas where frost may be a problem, providing the plant with some overhead protection or planting them next to a wall or a building may be sufficient protection. Individual plants are small enough to be fairly easily covered during cold snaps by placing plastic sheeting, etc. over a frame around them. Plastic row covers will also provide some frost protection for larger plantings. Potted specimens can be moved to a frost-secure area.
Harvest: The fruit is harvested when it falls to the ground, but not all fallen fruits may be in the same stage of maturity and must be held until they ripen. It may take some experience to tell when the calyx-enclosed fruits are fully ripe. Properly matured and prepared fruits will keep for several months.
The ripe fruit can be eaten out of hand or used in a number of other ways. The unique flavor of the fresh fruit makes it an interesting ingredient in salads and cooked dishes. Cape gooseberries cooked with apples or ginger make a very distinctive dessert. The fruits are also an attractive sweet when dipped in chocolate or other glazes or pricked and rolled in sugar. The high pectin content makes cape gooseberry a good preserve and jam product that can be used as a dessert topping. The fruit also dries into tasty "raisins".
propagation: The plant is widely grown from seed. There are 5,000 to 8,000 seeds per ounce, which are sometimes mixed with pulverized soil or ashes for uniform sowing. High humidity is required for good germination. The plants can also be propagated from 1 year old stem cuttings treated with a rooting hormone. Plants grown this way flower early and yield well but are less vigorous than seedlings.
pests_and_diseases: Cape gooseberries are bothered by several diseases, including Alternaria spp. and powdery mildew. The plants are also prone to root rots and viruses when grown on poorly drained soil. A host of insect pests also attack the plants, namely cut worm, stem borer (Heliotis suflixa), leaf borer (Epiatrix spp.), fruit moth (Phthorimaea), Colorado potato beetle, flea beetle and striped cucumber beetle (Acalymma vittata). Greenhouse grown plants are attacked by white fly and aphids. The stored fruit can be adversely affected by Penicillium and Botrytis molds.

description: Solanum quitoense, known as naranjilla in Ecuador and lulo in Colombia, is a subtropical perennial plant from northwestern South America, a member of the Nightshade family. It is an attractive shrub with large heart-shaped leaves up to 45 cm in length, covered in short purple hairs on leaves and stems. Naranjilla are delicate plants, growing best in partial shade and needing protection from strong winds and direct sunlight. The fruit has a citrus flavour, described as a combination of rhubarb and lime, and its green juice is often used as a drink. New growth is densely covered in protective trichomes, varying from purple to white. It is a perennial shrub with white flowers, edible fruit, and grows in USDA Zones 10 to 12.
pests_and_diseases: Pests easily destroy a crop of this species, limiting its agricultural use. One common type of infection is caused by the root-knot nematode. The ripe fruit can be attacked by fungus fairly easily, so it is often picked unripe to avoid rotting. Hybrids, such as with S. sessiliflorum, are an increasingly popular solution to the nematode pest problem.
